topics.title: Introduction to Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Optimization** is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the *best possible* (optimal) solution from a set of available alternatives, subject to a well-defined objective and a set of governing constraints. Formally, it is the process of determining the values of decision variables (design parameters) that maximize or minimize a prescribed performance criterion (objective function) while satisfying all system constraints.

In the rigorous language used across KTU 2024 Scheme engineering modules:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization (KTU Standard Definition):**
> Optimization is the science of selecting the *best* element $x^{*} \in S$ from a feasible set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ such that $f(x^{*}) \leq f(x)$ (minimization) or $f(x^{*}) \geq f(x)$ (maximization) for all $x \in S$, where $f$ is the objective function and $S$ is defined by a set of equality and inequality constraints.

Mathematically, the standard form of an optimization problem is written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\
\text{that minimizes (or maximizes) } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to: } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
\text{where } & x_i \in \mathbb{R}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\mathbf{x}$ represents the vector of **decision variables** (also called *design variables*), $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are the **inequality constraints**, and $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ are the **equality constraints**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Think of optimization as planning the cheapest route to travel from your home in Kerala (say, Thiruvananthapuram) to your college in Kochi. You have multiple routes (alternatives), and your goal is to minimize travel time and fuel cost (objective), while making sure you don't run out of fuel, don't exceed the speed limit, and reach before the first bell rings (constraints). Optimization is the formal, mathematical equivalent of this daily decision-making — but applied rigorously to engineering design, economics, logistics, manufacturing, and many other real-world systems.

Every day, human beings and engineering systems face the same fundamental question: *"Given limited resources (time, money, material, energy), what is the best possible way to allocate them to achieve a specific goal?"* Optimization provides the mathematical machinery to answer this question with precision.

### 1.3 Why Optimization Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Every KTU Engineer Must Study Optimization:**
> - **Cost Reduction:** Minimize material use, energy consumption, and production cost.
> - **Performance Maximization:** Maximize efficiency, strength, throughput, and profit.
> - **Better Designs:** Obtain optimal dimensions, weights, and shapes in mechanical/civil engineering.
> - **Decision Support:** Provide a scientific basis for managerial and operational choices.
> - **Resource Scarcity:** Address real-world limitations in time, budget, and raw materials.

**Real-World Engineering Applications:**

| Engineering Branch | Optimization Application |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Mechanical | Optimal design of machine components, minimizing weight of a truss |
| Civil | Minimum cost structural design, optimal scheduling of construction |
| Electrical | Optimal power flow, antenna design, circuit parameter selection |
| Computer Science | Algorithm complexity minimization, resource allocation |
| Chemical | Maximum yield in chemical reactors, optimal process control |

### 1.4 Historical Context (Brief)

The foundations of optimization trace back to the 17th century with **calculus of variations** (Bernoulli, Euler, Lagrange). The 20th century saw explosive development: **Dantzig's Simplex Method (1947)** revolutionized linear programming, and the post-war era gave rise to **nonlinear programming, dynamic programming (Bellman), and stochastic methods**. Today, optimization underpins machine learning, operations research, control theory, and modern AI systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Components of an Optimization Problem

Every optimization problem, irrespective of its engineering domain, consists of **five essential building blocks**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to identify these components from a problem statement — therefore, mastering them is critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Building Blocks of an Optimization Problem (High-Yield):**
> 1. **Decision Variables (Design Variables):** The quantities we control or choose.
> 2. **Objective Function:** The performance criterion to be optimized (min/max).
> 3. **Constraints:** Restrictions on the values the variables can take.
> 4. **Feasible Region (Solution Space):** The set of all variables satisfying all constraints.
> 5. **Optimal Solution:** The point in the feasible region that gives the best objective value.

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Component

**Step 1 — Decision Variables ($\mathbf{x}$):**
These are the unknowns whose values we wish to determine. They represent *degrees of freedom* in the problem. For example, in a structural design problem, the diameter and length of a beam are decision variables. Mathematically, $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Step 2 — Objective Function ($f(\mathbf{x})$):**
This is a scalar function of the decision variables that quantifies the performance or goal of the system. It is the *function we seek to optimize*.
- *Minimization:* Find $\mathbf{x}$ to make $f(\mathbf{x})$ as small as possible (e.g., cost, weight, error).
- *Maximization:* Find $\mathbf{x}$ to make $f(\mathbf{x})$ as large as possible (e.g., profit, efficiency, strength).

**Step 3 — Constraints:**
- **Equality Constraints** $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$: Must be *exactly* satisfied (e.g., $x_1 + x_2 = 100$).
- **Inequality Constraints** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (or $\geq 0$): Must be *satisfied* but not necessarily tight (e.g., $x_1 \leq 50$).
- **Bounds:** $x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}$ (upper/lower limits on variables).

**Step 4 — Feasible Region ($S$):**
The intersection of all constraint sets defines the feasible region:
$$
S = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0,\ j=1,\ldots,m;\ h_k(\mathbf{x})=0,\ k=1,\ldots,p \}
$$

**Step 5 — Optimal Solution ($\mathbf{x}^{*}$):**
A point $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in S$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in S$ (in minimization). The corresponding value $f(\mathbf{x}^{*})$ is the *optimal value*.

### 2.3 Classification of Optimization Problems

KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the following standard classification. This is one of the most frequently asked topics in Module 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Based on Nature of Variables and Functions:**

| Type | Description | Example Engineering Problem |
|------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Both objective and constraints are linear | Production planning, transportation |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | Either objective or constraints (or both) are nonlinear | Optimal design of springs, chemical reactors |
| **Integer Programming (IP)** | All decision variables must be integers | Project selection, job scheduling |
| **Dynamic Programming (DP)** | Problem solved in sequential stages | Route planning, inventory control |
| **Stochastic Programming** | Some parameters are random/probabilistic | Portfolio optimization under uncertainty |
| **Multi-Objective Optimization** | Multiple competing objectives | Design with cost vs. weight trade-off |
| **Geometric Programming** | Objective/constraints as posynomials | Optimal transformer design |

### 2.4 General Mathematical Formulation (Standard Form)

The most general (canonical) form of a constrained optimization problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme textbook notation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& x_i \in \mathbb{R}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Conventions used by KTU Examiners:**
- A *maximization* problem is converted to a minimization problem by taking the negative: $\max f = \min(-f)$.
- Inequality constraints are often written as $\leq 0$ (with a sign convention) to maintain uniformity in KKT conditions (Module 2).
- The **unconstrained** case has $m = p = 0$, so $S = \mathbb{R}^n$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of Optimization Theory:**
> Optimization is the backbone of Computer-Aided Design (CAD), Finite Element Analysis (FEA), process control, supply chain management, machine learning (loss minimization), and digital twin technology. Every modern engineering system — from smartphones to spacecraft — is tuned using optimization principles.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

The following table consolidates the most important definitions, notations, and identities for this topic. **Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.**

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | KTU Notation / Unit | Validity / Domain |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Standard Optimization Form | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j \leq 0, h_k = 0$ | $f$ is the objective, $g_j$ inequalities, $h_k$ equalities | General |
| Decision Variable Vector | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n$ variables | $n \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| Feasible Region | $S = \{\mathbf{x} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ | Subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Always |
| Optimal Point | $f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) \leq f(\mathbf{x}),\ \forall \mathbf{x} \in S$ | $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in S$ | For minimization |
| Max-to-Min Conversion | $\max f(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \min -f(\mathbf{x})$ | Equivalent problems | All problems |
| Constraint Count | $m$ inequalities, $p$ equalities | $m, p \in \mathbb{N}$ | Always |
| Slack Variable | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) + s_j = 0,\ s_j \geq 0$ | $s_j$ is slack | Inequality form |
| Unconstrained Problem | $m = 0,\ p = 0$ | $S = \mathbb{R}^n$ | Special case |
| Linear Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = c_1 x_1 + \cdots + c_n x_n$ | Coefficients $c_i \in \mathbb{R}$ | LP only |
| Quadratic Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} + c^T \mathbf{x}$ | $Q$ symmetric, $n \times n$ | QP |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting a General Maximization to Standard Minimization

Most engineering problems are stated in *natural* (often maximization) form. KTU board exams require students to first **convert to standard form** before applying any algorithm. Below is the rigorous, step-by-step algebraic derivation.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Original Problem:**
Consider a general maximization problem with mixed equality and inequality constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & F(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & G_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& H_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Maximization to Minimization (Logical Reasoning):**
Mathematically, finding the maximum of $F$ is equivalent to finding the minimum of $-F$, because if $\mathbf{x}_1$ gives a higher value than $\mathbf{x}_2$, then $-F(\mathbf{x}_1) < -F(\mathbf{x}_2)$. The maximizer of $F$ is precisely the minimizer of $-F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\mathbf{x}} F(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \min_{\mathbf{x}} \{ -F(\mathbf{x}) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

We define a new objective function:
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = -F(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Inequality Constraints to $\leq 0$ Form:**
If the original constraint is $G_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$, we multiply both sides by $-1$ (reversing the inequality):

$$
-G_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0
$$

Define:
$$
g_j(\mathbf{x}) = -G_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 4 — Equality Constraints Retain Their Form:**
$$
h_k(\mathbf{x}) = H_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final Standard Form:**
After all conversions, the canonical KTU standard form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = -F(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) = -G_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = H_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Standardize?**
> Standardization allows all algorithms (simplex, KKT, gradient methods) to assume the same structure: minimize, with $\leq$ inequalities and $=$ equalities. This is the *universal language* of optimization solvers.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Formulating an Engineering Problem

**Problem:** A manufacturing company produces two products, A and B, with unit profits of ₹30 and ₹20 respectively. The daily production is constrained by 80 labor-hours and 60 kg of raw material. Product A requires 4 hours and 2 kg; Product B requires 2 hours and 4 kg. Formulate as a Linear Programming problem.

**Step 1 — Identify Decision Variables:**
Let $x_1$ = units of Product A produced per day, $x_2$ = units of Product B produced per day.

**Step 2 — Identify Objective Function:**
Profit = $30x_1 + 20x_2$. The goal is to *maximize* this.

**Step 3 — Identify Constraints:**
- Labor: $4x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 80$
- Material: $2x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 60$
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 4 — Standard Form (Minimize, $\leq 0$ inequalities):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = -30x_1 - 20x_2 \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & g_1(\mathbf{x}) = 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 80 \leq 0 \\
& g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 2x_1 + 4x_2 - 60 \leq 0 \\
& g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \leq 0 \\
& g_4(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Identifying Problem Type

The following matrix helps KTU students identify the type of optimization problem from a given problem statement. **This is a high-yield exam aid.**

| If Objective is... | And Constraints are... | Then Problem Type is... | KTU Module Reference |
|--------------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Linear | Linear | Linear Programming (LP) | Module 2 |
| Quadratic | Linear | Quadratic Programming (QP) | Module 3 |
| Nonlinear | Nonlinear | Nonlinear Programming (NLP) | Module 2 / 3 |
| Linear | Nonlinear | Nonlinear Programming (NLP) | Module 3 |
| Any | Variables are integers | Integer Programming (IP) | Module 4 |
| Any | Variables are 0 or 1 | Binary / Boolean Programming | Module 4 |
| Any | Problem is multi-stage | Dynamic Programming (DP) | Module 5 |
| Any | Parameters are random | Stochastic Programming | Module 5 |

### 3.4 Analytical Note: Local vs. Global Optimum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept (Tested Frequently):**
> A *local minimum* $\mathbf{x}_L$ satisfies $f(\mathbf{x}_L) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ in a small *neighborhood* of $\mathbf{x}_L$. A *global minimum* $\mathbf{x}_G$ satisfies $f(\mathbf{x}_G) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for **all** $\mathbf{x} \in S$. A global minimum is always a local minimum, but the converse is false. For *convex* problems, every local minimum is the global minimum — this is why convexity is so important in optimization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of an Optimization Problem

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the hierarchical structure of a complete optimization problem, mapping the interactions between the decision variables, objective function, constraints, and the resulting solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B[Decision Variables x1 x2 xn]
    A --> C[Objective Function f of x]
    A --> D[Constraints]
    D --> D1[Inequality Constraints gj of x le 0]
    D --> D2[Equality Constraints hk of x equals 0]
    D --> D3[Bound Constraints xL le xi le xU]
    B --> E[Feasible Region S in Rn]
    C --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Algorithm]
    F --> G[Optimal Solution x star]
    G --> H[Optimal Objective Value f of x star]
    G --> I[Local Minimum]
    G --> J[Global Minimum]
    H --> K[Verification and Validation]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Steps in Solving an Optimization Problem

The following Mermaid flow chart shows the **sequential engineering workflow** for solving a real-world optimization problem, from problem identification to implementation. This process is repeatedly tested in KTU viva-voce and ESE questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify the Real World Problem] --> S2[Step 2 Define Decision Variables]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Formulate Objective Function]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Identify Constraints]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Convert to Standard Form]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Select Suitable Algorithm]
    S6 --> S7a[Linear Programming Simplex]
    S6 --> S7b[Nonlinear KKT Conditions]
    S6 --> S7c[Dynamic Programming]
    S6 --> S7d[Integer Programming Branch and Bound]
    S7a --> S8[Step 7 Solve and Obtain Optimal Solution]
    S7b --> S8
    S7c --> S8
    S7d --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 8 Validate and Interpret Result]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9 Implement in Real World]
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree: Types of Optimization Problems

This Mermaid diagram provides a structured taxonomy of the major branches of optimization, helping students recall the hierarchy of methods covered in PEFTT758.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Optimization] --> CD[Based on Variables]
    OP --> CF[Based on Functions]
    OP --> CN[Based on Number of Objectives]
    CD --> CD1[Continuous]
    CD --> CD2[Discrete Integer]
    CD --> CD3[Mixed Integer]
    CF --> CF1[Linear Programming LP]
    CF --> CF2[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    CF2 --> CF2a[Unconstrained]
    CF2 --> CF2b[Constrained]
    CF --> CF3[Quadratic Programming QP]
    CF --> CF4[Geometric Programming GP]
    CN --> CN1[Single Objective]
    CN --> CN2[Multi Objective Pareto]
    CF1 --> M2[Module 2]
    CF2 --> M3[Module 3]
    CD2 --> M4[Module 4]
    CF4 --> M5[Module 5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:**
> Each terminal node in the diagram is tagged with the corresponding KTU Module in PEFTT758. Use this as a quick reference when answering *"In which module is this method studied?"* type questions on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Optimization" in the context of engineering. State any two engineering applications where optimization techniques are applied.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Optimization is the mathematical process of selecting the best possible solution from a set of available alternatives. Formally, it involves finding the values of decision variables $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ that minimize (or maximize) an objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to a set of constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ and $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

*Engineering Applications (1 mark, any two):*
1. Minimum weight design of a truss in civil/structural engineering.
2. Optimal power flow in an electrical power grid.
3. Minimum cost production planning in manufacturing industries.
4. Optimal route planning in transportation/logistics.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the essential components of an optimization problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The essential components of an optimization problem are:

1. **Decision Variables (1 mark):** The independent quantities whose values are to be determined. They are the *controllable inputs* to the system, denoted as $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$.

2. **Objective Function (1 mark):** A scalar function $f(\mathbf{x})$ that quantitatively measures the performance or quality of the design. The goal is to find the values of $\mathbf{x}$ that minimize or maximize $f(\mathbf{x})$.

3. **Constraints (1 mark):** Restrictions on the decision variables. These may be inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, or bound constraints $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$. The set of all points satisfying every constraint is called the *feasible region*.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):**
> Students frequently lose marks in Part B by (a) failing to convert a maximization problem into a standard minimization form, (b) writing constraints in the *wrong sign convention* (e.g., using $\geq 0$ instead of $\leq 0$ without justification), and (c) not identifying all components (variables, objective, constraints) explicitly. **Always box your final answer** and label every equation with a clear "Step 1, Step 2..." format — this matches the KTU 2024 valuation key.

---

#### ✦ Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the term *objective function*, *design variables*, and *feasible region* in an optimization problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company manufactures two products P and Q. The profit per unit of P is ₹50 and per unit of Q is ₹70. The company has a maximum of 240 man-hours available. Each unit of P requires 4 man-hours and each unit of Q requires 6 man-hours. The market survey indicates that at most 40 units of P and at most 30 units of Q can be sold. Formulate this problem as a standard mathematical optimization problem. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Definitions (7 Marks):**

**Objective Function (2.5 marks):**
The objective function is a scalar mathematical function $f(\mathbf{x})$ that quantifies the performance, cost, profit, or quality of the system in terms of the design variables. The aim of optimization is to find the values of $\mathbf{x}$ that produce the optimum (minimum or maximum) value of this function.

**Design Variables (2.5 marks):**
Design variables (also called decision variables) are the independent parameters of the system that the designer is free to choose. They are represented as a vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$. For example, in a beam design, the depth and width of the cross-section may be design variables.

**Feasible Region (2 marks):**
The feasible region $S$ is the set of all possible values of $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfy *every* constraint (equality, inequality, and bound) of the problem:

$$
S = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, j=1,\ldots,m;\ h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, k=1,\ldots,p \}
$$

The optimal solution must lie within this feasible region.

---

**(b) Problem Formulation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Decision Variables (1 mark):**
Let $x_1$ = number of units of product P produced, and $x_2$ = number of units of product Q produced.

**Step 2 — Identify the Objective Function (1.5 marks):**
The profit (to be maximized) is:
$$
Z = 50x_1 + 70x_2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Constraints (2 marks):**
- Man-hour constraint: $4x_1 + 6x_2 \leq 240$
- Market demand for P: $x_1 \leq 40$
- Market demand for Q: $x_2 \leq 30$
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 4 — Convert to Standard Form (Minimize, $\leq 0$) (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = -50x_1 - 70x_2 \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & g_1(\mathbf{x}) = 4x_1 + 6x_2 - 240 \leq 0 \\
& g_2(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 - 40 \leq 0 \\
& g_3(\mathbf{x}) = x_2 - 30 \leq 0 \\
& g_4(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \leq 0 \\
& g_5(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Mathematical Model (1 mark):**
The standard form of the optimization problem is therefore:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & -50x_1 - 70x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 4x_1 + 6x_2 \leq 240, \quad x_1 \leq 40, \quad x_2 \leq 30, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

#### ✦ Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Classify optimization problems based on the nature of the objective function and constraints, with at least one engineering example for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a cylindrical pressure vessel of volume $V = 10\ \text{m}^3$. The cost of the material is ₹500 per m² for the curved surface and ₹800 per m² for the two circular ends. Formulate the problem to *minimize the total cost* of material. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Classification of Optimization Problems (7 Marks):**

**1. Linear Programming (LP) (2 marks):** Both objective and constraints are linear functions of the decision variables. *Engineering Example:* Production planning — maximize profit subject to labor and material constraints.

**2. Nonlinear Programming (NLP) (2 marks):** Either the objective or the constraints (or both) are nonlinear. *Engineering Example:* Optimal design of a spring — minimize weight subject to a nonlinear shear-stress constraint.

**3. Integer Programming (IP) (1.5 marks):** All decision variables must take integer values. *Engineering Example:* Selecting projects to fund from a portfolio of candidates.

**4. Dynamic Programming (DP) (1.5 marks):** Multi-stage decision problems solved via Bellman's principle of optimality. *Engineering Example:* Shortest path routing in a transportation network.

---

**(b) Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Formulation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Decision Variables (1.5 marks):**
Let $r$ = radius of the cylinder (m), and $h$ = height of the cylinder (m). Thus $\mathbf{x} = (r, h)$.

**Step 2 — Express the Volume Constraint (1.5 marks):**
$$
V = \pi r^2 h = 10 \quad\Rightarrow\quad h = \frac{10}{\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the Cost Function (2 marks):**
- Curved (lateral) surface area: $A_c = 2\pi r h$, cost rate: ₹500/m² → cost $= 500 \cdot 2\pi r h = 1000\pi r h$.
- Two circular ends: $A_e = 2\pi r^2$, cost rate: ₹800/m² → cost $= 800 \cdot 2\pi r^2 = 1600\pi r^2$.

Total cost:
$$
C(r, h) = 1000\pi r h + 1600\pi r^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Constraint to Get a Single-Variable Function (1.5 marks):**
Using $h = 10/(\pi r^2)$:
$$
C(r) = 1000\pi r \cdot \frac{10}{\pi r^2} + 1600\pi r^2 = \frac{10000}{r} + 1600\pi r^2
$$

**Step 5 — Standard Mathematical Form (0.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & C(r) = \frac{10000}{r} + 1600\pi r^2, \quad r > 0 \\
\text{Subject to } \quad & \pi r^2 h = 10
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Note (Calculus Trick — for Higher Marks):** To find the optimal $r$, take $\dfrac{dC}{dr} = -\dfrac{10000}{r^2} + 3200\pi r = 0 \Rightarrow r^3 = \dfrac{10000}{3200\pi} \Rightarrow r = \left(\dfrac{10000}{3200\pi}\right)^{1/3} \approx 1.00\ \text{m}$. This yields $h = 10/(\pi \cdot 1) \approx 3.18\ \text{m}$. The minimum cost is approximately ₹15,080.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Introduction to Optimization: High-Density Revision Summary**

- **Optimization** is the science of finding the *best* solution from a set of alternatives, formally defined as $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_j \leq 0$ and $h_k = 0$.
- **The 5 Building Blocks:** Decision Variables, Objective Function, Constraints, Feasible Region, Optimal Solution — identify all of them in every problem.
- **Decision Variables ($\mathbf{x}$):** Free parameters chosen by the designer; denoted as an $n$-vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Objective Function $f(\mathbf{x})$:** Scalar function to be minimized or maximized.
- **Constraints** come in three flavors: **inequality** ($g_j \leq 0$), **equality** ($h_k = 0$), and **bounds** ($x^L \leq x \leq x^U$).
- **Feasible Region $S$:** The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying *all* constraints simultaneously.
- **Optimal Solution $\mathbf{x}^{*}$:** The feasible point giving the best objective value.
- **Standard Form Convention (KTU 2024):** Always minimize, always use $\leq 0$ for inequalities. Use $\max f = \min(-f)$ to convert.
- **Key Classification:** LP (linear), NLP (nonlinear), IP (integer), DP (dynamic/staged), QP (quadratic), GP (geometric).
- **Local vs. Global:** Every global optimum is local, but not vice versa. *Convex* problems have the property that every local optimum is the global optimum.
- **History Highlight:** Simplex Method (Dantzig, 1947); Bellman's DP (1950s); Kuhn-Tucker conditions (1951) — the cornerstone of constrained optimization.
- **Applications:** Truss design, power flow, production planning, chemical reactor design, route planning, ML loss minimization.
- **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Always write the problem in **standard form** before applying any algorithm — failing to do so is the #1 reason students lose marks in Module 1.

> *"In engineering, optimization is not a luxury — it is the discipline of doing more with less."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Optimization?

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization** is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the *best* (most favorable) values of a system's parameters under given constraints, by systematically selecting from a set of available alternatives. Formally, it is the process of determining the values of decision variables that minimize (or maximize) an objective function while satisfying all imposed constraints.

In engineering terms, the *Engineers' Optimization Council* (a conventional descriptor used across standard optimization textbooks) defines:

> **Engineering Optimization** is the process of determining the optimal engineering design parameters that yield the best compromise among conflicting requirements, subject to all design and operational restrictions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Bengaluru. You have several **choices** (routes), and you want to reach **fastest** (or **cheapest**, or **safest**). Each route is an *alternative*, "fastest/cheapest" is your *objective*, and the time/money available forms your *constraints*.

**Optimization is exactly this — but for engineering systems.**

- The **routes** = design variables (what you control)
- The **fastest/cheapest** = objective function (what you want to optimize)
- The **budget, time available, fuel capacity** = constraints (limitations you cannot violate)

The job of an optimization algorithm is to scan all feasible routes and pick the *best* one — automatically, mathematically, and rigorously.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Every Optimization Problem

> [!NOTE]
> Every optimization problem in KTU 2024 Scheme, regardless of its engineering origin, consists of **three mandatory components**.

### Pillar 1 — Design Variables (Decision Variables)

These are the **independent quantities** that the designer/engineer is free to choose. They fully describe the system at any instant.

- $X = (x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n)^T$ is the **column vector** of design variables.
- $n$ is the **number of design variables** (also called the *dimensionality* of the problem).

**Classification of Design Variables:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Continuous** | Can take any real value within a range | Shaft diameter: $d = 24.5\ mm$ |
| **Discrete** | Selected from a specified set of permitted values | Beam section: $\{$W12×26, W14×30, W16×36$\}$ |
| **Integer** | Restricted to whole numbers | Number of bolts: $n = 4, 6, 8$ |

### Pillar 2 — Objective Function (Cost / Performance Function)

A scalar, single-valued function $f(X)$ that quantifies the *goodness* of a design.

- **Minimization** form: $f(X)$ is to be made as small as possible.
- **Maximization** form: $f(X)$ is to be made as large as possible.

$$f(X) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A maximization problem $\max f(X)$ is mathematically equivalent to a minimization problem $\min \{-f(X)\}$. KTU 2024 valuation scripts always expect objective functions stated in **minimization** form unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### Pillar 3 — Constraints

Mathematical restrictions that the design must obey. They define the *boundary of feasibility*.

**Types of Constraints:**

**a) Equality Constraints** — must be satisfied *exactly*:

$$h_j(X) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

**b) Inequality Constraints** — must be satisfied *as a direction*:

$$g_i(X) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Side Constraints / Bounds** are a special class of inequality constraints that act on a single variable:
> $$x_k^{L} \leq x_k \leq x_k^{U}$$

---

## 1.3 The Feasible Region and Optimum Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feasible Region (S):** The set of *all* design points that satisfy **every** constraint and side constraint simultaneously.
> $$S = \{X \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(X) \leq 0,\; h_j(X) = 0,\; x_k^L \leq x_k \leq x_k^U\}$$

**Optimum Solution ($X^*$):** A feasible point at which the objective function attains its most favorable value.

- $X^*$ is a **global optimum** if no other feasible point yields a better $f$ value anywhere in $S$.
- $X^*$ is a **local optimum** if no better $f$ value exists within a small neighborhood of $X^*$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Standard Form (Canonical Form)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects every optimization problem to be reducible to the **Standard Mathematical Programming Form**:

$$\text{Minimize } f(X)$$

$$\text{Subject to: } g_i(X) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

$$h_j(X) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

$$X \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *mother form* in optimization. Every linear program, nonlinear program, integer program, and dynamic program can be cast into this structure. Memorize it.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Optimization Problems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the ability to *classify* a problem based on the nature of $f$, $g$, and $h$.

| Class | Condition on $f(X)$ | Condition on Constraints | Typical Method |
|-------|---------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Linear | All linear | Simplex, Graphical |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | Nonlinear or any nonlinear part | Linear or nonlinear | KKT, Lagrange, Gradient |
| **Integer Programming (IP)** | Linear | Linear | Branch & Bound |
| **Dynamic Programming (DP)** | Any | Any | Bellman's Principle |
| **Geometric Programming (GP)** | Posynomial | Posynomial | Dual transformation |
| **Quadratic Programming (QP)** | Quadratic | Linear | Wolfe's method |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Examples of Optimization

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves to test your grasp of definitions by asking "Identify the design variables, objective, and constraints" in a real engineering setup. Below are the most likely scenarios.

### Example A — Minimum Weight Design of a Cantilever Beam

- **Design variables:** Length $L$, width $b$, depth $d$, material property $E$.
- **Objective:** Minimize volume (and hence weight) $V = b \cdot d \cdot L$.
- **Constraints:** Maximum tip deflection $\delta_{max}$, maximum bending stress $\sigma_{max}$, and size limits $L \leq 5\,m$, $b \geq 0.1\,m$.

### Example B — Optimal Production Planning

- **Design variables:** Quantity of product A ($x_1$) and product B ($x_2$).
- **Objective:** Maximize profit $Z = 50x_1 + 40x_2$.
- **Constraints:** Raw material limits, machine hours, demand caps.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Objective Function | $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ | Scalar-valued |
| 2 | Vector of Design Variables | $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ | $n \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 3 | Feasible Region | $S = \{X \mid g_i(X) \leq 0, h_j(X) = 0\}$ | Convex / Non-convex |
| 4 | Inequality Constraint | $g_i(X) \leq 0$ | $m$ such constraints |
| 5 | Equality Constraint | $h_j(X) = 0$ | $p$ such constraints |
| 6 | Side / Bound Constraint | $x_k^L \leq x_k \leq x_k^U$ | Lower & Upper bound |
| 7 | Standard Form | $\min f(X)$ s.t. $g_i \leq 0, h_j = 0$ | Canonical for KTU |
| 8 | Conversion: $\max \to \min$ | $\max f(X) \equiv \min \{-f(X)\}$ | Always do this first |
| 9 | Conversion: $\geq \to \leq$ | $g_i \geq 0 \Rightarrow -g_i \leq 0$ | Flip and negate |
| 10 | Global Optimum | $f(X^*) \leq f(X), \quad \forall X \in S$ | Strongest notion |
| 11 | Local Optimum | $\exists \epsilon > 0$ such that $f(X^*) \leq f(X)$ for all $X \in S \cap B(X^*, \epsilon)$ | $\epsilon$-neighborhood |
| 12 | Dimensionality of Problem | $n$ = number of design variables | Equals the *order* of $X$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student error is confusing **local** and **global** optima. A local minimum can be very far in value from the global minimum — the algorithm may stop at a local minimum if not designed carefully.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Optimization theory underpins virtually every modern engineering design tool:

- **Mechanical:** Topology optimization of aerospace brackets (weight ↓, stiffness ↑).
- **Civil:** Optimal truss sizing and route alignment.
- **Electrical:** Optimal power dispatch in smart grids.
- **Industrial:** Inventory control, supply chain network design.
- **Computer Science:** Hyperparameter tuning in machine learning models.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme course PEFTT758 builds every subsequent module (LP, NLP, DP, etc.) directly on these definitions. Mastering them now is the **single highest-leverage** activity in the entire syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reducing an Arbitrary Problem to Standard Form

This is a **must-know derivation** for KTU 2024 — examiners test it almost every cycle as a Part A or Part B sub-question.

### Starting Point — A Free-Floating Problem

Consider an arbitrary engineering problem stated as:

"**Maximize** $f(X) = 30x_1 + 40x_2$ subject to $2x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 12$, $x_1 + x_2 = 8$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$."

We must convert this into the canonical KTU Standard Form.

---

### Step 1 — Convert Maximization into Minimization

The general identity used is:

$$\max_{X \in S} f(X) = -\min_{X \in S} \{-f(X)\}$$

Applying this to our objective function:

$$f(X) = 30x_1 + 40x_2 \quad \longrightarrow \quad f'(X) = -(30x_1 + 40x_2)$$

The new objective is:

$$f'(X) = -30x_1 - 40x_2$$

**Reasoning:** A maximization problem is *mathematically dual* to the minimization of the negated objective. Negating the function inverts the ordering, turning "highest" values into "lowest" values.

---

### Step 2 — Convert "$\geq$" Constraints into "$\leq$" Form

The general identity is:

$$g_i(X) \geq 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad -g_i(X) \leq 0$$

Apply this to the first constraint $2x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 12$:

$$2x_1 + 3x_2 - 12 \geq 0$$

Multiplying by $-1$ flips the inequality:

$$-(2x_1 + 3x_2 - 12) \leq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -2x_1 - 3x_2 + 12 \leq 0$$

**Reasoning:** KTU's canonical form requires *all* inequality constraints to be of the "less-than-or-equal-to" form. This is a notational convention adopted from linear programming literature and ensures algorithmic uniformity.

---

### Step 3 — Preserve Equality Constraints As-Is

The constraint $x_1 + x_2 = 8$ is already in the canonical $h_j(X) = 0$ form after rearrangement:

$$h_1(X) = x_1 + x_2 - 8 = 0$$

**Reasoning:** Equality constraints cannot be converted — they represent *fixed* design specifications, not adjustable limits.

---

### Step 4 — Convert Non-Negativity to Side Constraints

The bounds $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$ are already in valid bound form:

$$x_1 \geq 0, \quad x_2 \geq 0$$

These are written as:

$$-x_1 \leq 0, \quad -x_2 \leq 0$$

**Reasoning:** These become $g_i(X) \leq 0$ form with the right-hand side equal to zero.

---

### Step 5 — Assemble the Canonical Problem

The fully converted problem is:

$$\text{Minimize } f'(X) = -30x_1 - 40x_2$$

$$\text{Subject to: } g_1(X) = -2x_1 - 3x_2 + 12 \leq 0$$

$$g_2(X) = -x_1 \leq 0$$

$$g_3(X) = -x_2 \leq 0$$

$$h_1(X) = x_1 + x_2 - 8 = 0$$

---

### Step 6 — Final Generalized Conversion Rule (Summary)

For any arbitrary optimization problem, the following transformations always apply to bring it to standard form:

$$\text{Max } f \;\to\; \text{Min } (-f)$$

$$g \geq 0 \;\to\; -g \leq 0$$

$$g \leq 0 \;\to\; \text{keep as } g \leq 0$$

$$h = c \;\to\; (h - c) = 0$$

$$x \geq a \;\to\; (a - x) \leq 0 \;\text{ or}\; (x - a) \geq 0$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Problem Type Identification

This is a structured analysis tool that maps the *shape* of $f$ and $g$ to a *named class* and a *recommended algorithm*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input: } & f(X),\; g_i(X),\; h_j(X) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Is } f \text{ linear in } X? \\
& \quad \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{Linear} \\
& \quad \text{No} \Rightarrow \text{Nonlinear} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Are all } g_i, h_j \text{ linear?} \\
& \quad \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{(L) Programming} \\
& \quad \text{No} \Rightarrow \text{Nonlinear} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Are all } x_k \text{ integers?} \\
& \quad \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{Integer Programming} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Does } f \text{ have a quadratic term } x^T Q x? \\
& \quad \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{Quadratic Programming} \\[4pt]
\text{Output: } & \text{Named class + algorithm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This decision-tree analysis is what examiners expect when they ask: *"Classify the following optimization problem."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Anatomy of an Optimization Problem

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **structural decomposition** of any optimization problem, with each component annotated and connected via directed flow arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B[Design Variables Vector X]
    A --> C[Objective Function f of X]
    A --> D[Constraints Block]

    B --> B1[Continuous x1, x2, ..., xn]
    B --> B2[Discrete / Integer Values]

    C --> C1[Scalar Output]
    C --> C2[Minimize Form]
    C --> C3[Maximize Form]

    D --> D1[Inequality Constraints g of X less than 0]
    D --> D2[Equality Constraints h of X equals 0]
    D --> D3[Side Constraints xL less than x less than xU]

    B1 --> E[Feasible Region S]
    B2 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    C1 --> F[Algorithm Search]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Optimum Solution X Star]

    G --> G1[Global Optimum]
    G --> G2[Local Optimum]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Optimization Workflow

This diagram maps the *process flow* — from problem statement to validated solution — which KTU examiners often request as a "sketch" question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Design Variables] --> S2[Step 2: Formulate Objective Function]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Enumerate Constraints]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Convert to Standard Form]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Select Algorithm]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Solve]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Verify Feasibility]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Validate Optimality]
    S8 --> S9{Optimal Solution Found?}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Report X Star and f of X Star]
    S9 -->|No| S5
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Classification Decision Tree

The following block schematic shows how a problem is *classified* into a known optimization type — answering a frequently asked KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given Problem] --> Q1{Is f linear?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{All constraints linear?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1{Nonlinear Programming}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{All x integer?}
    Q2 -->|No| R1
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2[Integer Programming]
    Q3 -->|No| R3[Linear Programming LP]
    R1 --> R4{Quadratic form xT Q x?}
    R4 -->|Yes| R5[Quadratic Programming QP]
    R4 -->|No| R6[General NLP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "Feasible Region" in optimization. How is it mathematically represented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Feasible Region** is the set of all design variable vectors $X$ that simultaneously satisfy every constraint (equality, inequality, and side constraints) of the optimization problem.

**Mathematical Representation (2 marks):**

$$S = \{X \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(X) \leq 0,\; i = 1, 2, \ldots, m;\; h_j(X) = 0,\; j = 1, 2, \ldots, p;\; x_k^L \leq x_k \leq x_k^U\}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** The feasible region delimits the *search space* of the optimizer. Only points inside $S$ are valid designs; the optimum $X^*$ is always a member of $S$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Local Optimum and Global Optimum in optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Local Optimum | Global Optimum |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Best point within a small neighborhood | Best point in the **entire** feasible region |
| **Mathematical Form** | $f(X^*) \leq f(X)$ for all $X \in S \cap B(X^*, \epsilon)$ | $f(X^*) \leq f(X)$ for all $X \in S$ |
| **Uniqueness** | Multiple local optima possible | Unique in value (not necessarily in point) |
| **Practical Detection** | Easy via gradient-based methods | Hard; requires global search algorithms |

> [!NOTE]
> A *local* optimum is sufficient for convex problems, where it coincides with the *global* optimum.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Convert the following optimization problem into standard form. Clearly identify the design variables, objective function, constraints, and feasible region.**

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 5x_1^2 + 3x_2 - 4x_3$$

$$\text{Subject to: } x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 \leq 100$$

$$3x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 = 50$$

$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0, \quad x_3 \geq 5$$

#### Part (a) — Component Identification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Design Variables (2 marks)**

The design variables are the quantities the designer can choose:

$$X = (x_1, x_2, x_3)^T \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 3$$

**Step 2: Objective Function (2 marks)**

The function to be optimized is $Z(X) = 5x_1^2 + 3x_2 - 4x_3$.

> Since the problem is a **maximization**, the original objective is a *maximization* form, but the **nonlinear** quadratic term $5x_1^2$ indicates this is a **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** problem.

**Step 3: Constraints (3 marks)**

- Inequality constraint: $x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 \leq 100$  →  $g_1(X) = x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 - 100 \leq 0$
- Equality constraint: $3x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 = 50$  →  $h_1(X) = 3x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 - 50 = 0$
- Non-negativity: $x_1 \geq 0, x_2 \geq 0$  →  $g_2(X) = -x_1 \leq 0, \; g_3(X) = -x_2 \leq 0$
- Lower bound on $x_3$: $x_3 \geq 5$  →  $g_4(X) = 5 - x_3 \leq 0$

---

#### Part (b) — Standard Form Conversion (7 marks)

**Step 1: Convert Maximization to Minimization (2 marks)**

$$\max Z(X) \quad \longrightarrow \quad \min f(X) = -Z(X)$$

$$f(X) = -5x_1^2 - 3x_2 + 4x_3$$

**Step 2: Keep all inequality constraints in $\leq 0$ form (2 marks)**

The first inequality is already in the correct form:

$$g_1(X) = x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 - 100 \leq 0$$

**Step 3: Rewrite the equality constraint (1 mark)**

$$h_1(X) = 3x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 - 50 = 0$$

**Step 4: Final Canonical Problem (2 marks)**

$$\min f(X) = -5x_1^2 - 3x_2 + 4x_3$$

$$\text{Subject to: } g_1(X) = x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 - 100 \leq 0$$

$$g_2(X) = -x_1 \leq 0$$

$$g_3(X) = -x_2 \leq 0$$

$$g_4(X) = 5 - x_3 \leq 0$$

$$h_1(X) = 3x_1 - x_2 + 2x_3 - 50 = 0$$

**Feasible Region:**

$$S = \{X \in \mathbb{R}^3 \mid g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4 \leq 0,\; h_1 = 0\}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**A manufacturing company produces two products P and Q. Profit per unit is ₹50 for P and ₹40 for Q. The company has a maximum of 240 machine hours and 160 labor hours. Each unit of P requires 4 machine hours and 2 labor hours; each unit of Q requires 3 machine hours and 4 labor hours. Formulate this as a standard optimization problem.**

#### Part (a) — Problem Formulation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify Design Variables (2 marks)**

Let:

- $x_1$ = number of units of product P produced
- $x_2$ = number of units of product Q produced

$$X = (x_1, x_2)^T$$

**Step 2: Formulate the Objective Function (2 marks)**

The total profit (to be maximized) is:

$$Z = 50x_1 + 40x_2$$

**Step 3: Formulate the Constraints (3 marks)**

- Machine hours: $4x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 240$  →  $g_1(X) = 4x_1 + 3x_2 - 240 \leq 0$
- Labor hours: $2x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 160$  →  $g_2(X) = 2x_1 + 4x_2 - 160 \leq 0$
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$  →  $g_3(X) = -x_1 \leq 0, \; g_4(X) = -x_2 \leq 0$

> **Note:** No equality constraints exist in this problem.

---

#### Part (b) — Standard Form and Classification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Convert to Standard Form (3 marks)**

$$\min f(X) = -50x_1 - 40x_2$$

$$\text{Subject to: } g_1(X) = 4x_1 + 3x_2 - 240 \leq 0$$

$$g_2(X) = 2x_1 + 4x_2 - 160 \leq 0$$

$$g_3(X) = -x_1 \leq 0$$

$$g_4(X) = -x_2 \leq 0$$

**Step 2: Classify the Problem (2 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $f(X)$ is **linear** and **all** constraints are linear, this is a **Linear Programming (LP)** problem. The recommended solution method is the **Simplex Method** or **Graphical Method** (for 2 variables).

**Step 3: Identify the Feasible Region Shape (2 marks)**

The feasible region is a **convex polygon** in the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane, bounded by the lines:

$$4x_1 + 3x_2 = 240, \quad 2x_1 + 4x_2 = 160, \quad x_1 = 0, \quad x_2 = 0$$

The four corner points of this polygon are evaluated to find the optimum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Definitions" Module Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the objective when converting $\max \to \min$** — Examiners deduct 2 full marks if the conversion is shown but the sign of the objective is left un-flipped.
> 2. **Leaving the objective in maximization form** — KTU 2024 valuation scripts *prefer* the minimization canonical form. Full marks only when the conversion is explicitly demonstrated.
> 3. **Mixing up $\leq$ and $\geq$ signs** — A single inequality flipped in the wrong direction invalidates the entire constraint system. Always re-verify the direction.
> 4. **Not expressing the feasible region as a set** — The set-builder notation $S = \{X \mid \ldots\}$ is mandatory for full credit in Part (a) sub-questions.
> 5. **Confusing discrete and integer variables** — *Discrete* means chosen from a permitted set; *Integer* means whole numbers specifically. The terms are NOT interchangeable.
> 6. **Omitting side constraints** — Many students miss the non-negativity constraints. Always list them explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Definitions (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Optimization** = finding the *best* design within given constraints.
- [ ] **Three mandatory components** = design variables $X$, objective $f(X)$, constraints $g_i, h_j$.
- [ ] **Design variables** form a vector $X \in \mathbb{R}^n$; $n$ is the dimensionality.
- [ ] **Objective function** is a *scalar* $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$.
- [ ] **Constraints** are of two kinds: *equality* $h_j(X) = 0$ and *inequality* $g_i(X) \leq 0$.
- [ ] **Side constraints** = bounds on individual variables $x_k^L \leq x_k \leq x_k^U$.
- [ ] **Feasible region** $S$ = set of all $X$ satisfying every constraint.
- [ ] **Standard form** is always minimization with $g_i \leq 0$ and $h_j = 0$.
- [ ] **Conversion rules**: $\max f \to \min(-f)$; $g \geq 0 \to -g \leq 0$.
- [ ] **Global optimum** is best in *all* of $S$; **local optimum** is best in a *neighborhood* of $X^*$.
- [ ] **Classification of problems**: LP, NLP, IP, QP, GP, DP — based on form of $f$ and $g, h$.
- [ ] **Engineering optimization** = process of finding the *optimum engineering design* under all restrictions.
- [ ] The **canonical form** is the universal language of all optimization algorithms — memorize it.
- [ ] The **feasible region** is **always convex** for LP and QP problems, but generally **non-convex** for NLP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Rule for KTU 2024:** If you can convert *any* arbitrary problem statement into the canonical minimization form in under 3 minutes, you have fully internalized the "Definitions" module. Move on to LP techniques with confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historical Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Historical Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Historical Development of Optimization):**
> The *Historical Development of Optimization Techniques* traces the evolution of mathematical methods—from classical *calculus of variations* (17th–19th century) through *linear programming* (mid-20th century) to *modern metaheuristic and stochastic optimization* (21st century)—designed to find the *best (minimum or maximum) value* of an objective function subject to constraints, culminating in the unified framework used today in engineering, economics, and operations research.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is treated as a **conceptual foundation module** that scaffolds every subsequent technique (LP, NLP, DP, etc.). It establishes the **chronological lineage** of the algorithms the student will master later in the course.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "Optimization as the Evolution of the Compass":**
> Imagine a sailor trying to cross an ocean. In 300 BC, he looked at the stars (Euclidean geometry). In 1700, he used a magnetic compass (Calculus of Variations — Euler, Lagrange). In 1940, he used radar (Linear Programming — Dantzig, Kantorovich). Today, he uses GPS with AI-driven route correction (Metaheuristics — Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing).
> **Each historical leap did not discard the old — it built upon it.** This is exactly how Optimization Techniques evolved.

### 1.3 Why Study the History? (KTU Examiner's Perspective)

Examiners frequently pose **2-mark and short-answer questions** on this topic because it tests the student's *breadth of awareness* and ability to *contextualize* modern algorithms. Knowing the **origin** of an algorithm helps in understanding its **assumptions, strengths, and limitations**.

### 1.4 Three Major Historical Eras

| Era | Approximate Period | Core Mathematical Tool | Key Drivers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classical Era | 300 BC – 1900 AD | Geometry, Calculus of Variations | Physics, Astronomy, Engineering Mechanics |
| Modern OR Era | 1940 – 1970 | Linear Programming, Nonlinear Programming, Dynamic Programming | WWII Military Logistics, Industrial Planning |
| Contemporary Era | 1970 – Present | Metaheuristics, Stochastic & Hybrid Methods | Computer Power, Big Data, AI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in KTU Module 1:** The module typically attributes **3–4 lecture hours** to this topic, forming a **qualitative base** before the formal mathematical treatment of LP starts in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chronological Architecture of Optimization

The historical development can be cleanly partitioned into **five distinct phases**, each contributing a unique theoretical pillar to the modern optimization edifice.

#### Phase 1 — The Ancient Geometric Foundation (300 BC – 1600 AD)
- **Euclid (c. 300 BC)** formulated the principle of finding the *shortest path* between two points.
- **Hero of Alexandria (c. 10–70 AD)** stated that *light travels along the path of least distance* — the earliest recorded *variational principle*.
- **Contributions:** Geometric extrema (maximize area for fixed perimeter — the isoperimetric problem).

#### Phase 2 — The Birth of the Calculus of Variations (1696 – 1800)
- **1696:** The *Brachistochrone Problem* posed by **Johann Bernoulli** — the problem that *founded* the entire field of calculus of variations. It asked: *What is the curve of fastest descent under gravity?*
- **1697:** Solved by **Newton, Leibniz, L'Hôpital, and the Bernoulli brothers** — the first true optimization competition in history.
- **1755:** **Leonhard Euler** developed the first systematic theory of variational problems.
- **1788:** **Joseph-Louis Lagrange** introduced the *method of Lagrange multipliers*, transforming constrained optimization forever.

The foundational result is the **Euler–Lagrange Equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) = 0
$$

This equation determines the extremal curve $y(x)$ that minimizes the functional $J = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x, y, y') \, dx$.

#### Phase 3 — The Industrial Age & Pre-OR Mathematics (1800 – 1939)
- **Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1847)** proposed the *method of steepest descent* for unconstrained minimization.
- **Karl Weierstrass** proved the existence of minima in variational problems (Weierstrass Existence Theorem).
- **Isaac Newton and Joseph Raphson (c. 1690–c. 1715)** had earlier developed root-finding methods used in early numerical optimization.
- This era laid the *analytic foundation* but lacked the *computational engine* for large-scale problems.

#### Phase 4 — The OR Revolution & Linear Programming (1939 – 1970)
- **1939:** **Leonid Kantorovich (USSR)** — often called the *father of linear programming* — developed the mathematical theory of *optimal planning* for the Soviet economy, particularly the **transportation problem**.
- **1947:** **George Bernard Dantzig (USA)** developed the **Simplex Method** — the most influential optimization algorithm of the 20th century.
- **1951:** **Dantzig, Fulkerson, and Johnson** solved a 49-city TSP (Travelling Salesman Problem) — establishing combinatorial optimization.
- **1951:** **Albert W. Tucker, Harold W. Kuhn** formalized the **Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions** (first stated by Karush in 1939).
- **1957:** **Richard Bellman** introduced **Dynamic Programming** and the *Principle of Optimality*.
- **1961:** **A.L. Nemirovsky and D. Yudin** laid groundwork for *subgradient methods*.
- **1964:** **Fletcher and Powell** developed variable-metric (quasi-Newton) methods.

#### Phase 5 — Modern & Heuristic Era (1970 – Present)
- **1975:** **Holland** introduced **Genetic Algorithms**.
- **1983:** **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, Vecchi** developed **Simulated Annealing**.
- **1995:** **Kennedy and Eberhart** formulated **Particle Swarm Optimization**.
- **1995–2010s:** Rise of **Interior-Point Methods (Karmarkar, 1984)**, **Evolutionary Strategies**, and **Ant Colony Optimization (Dorigo, 1992)**.
- **2010s–Present:** **Deep Learning–based optimization**, **Bayesian Optimization**, **Quantum-Inspired Algorithms**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **historically significant equations** that KTU examiners expect a student to recognize. These appear in derivations, problem statements, or as referenced formulas.

| # | Equation / Concept | Historical Origin | Inventor(s) & Year | Modern Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) = 0$ | Calculus of Variations | Euler–Lagrange, 1755 | Optimal trajectory design (aerospace, robotics) |
| 2 | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lambda \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Constrained Optimization | Lagrange, 1788 | Engineering design with equality constraints |
| 3 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Steepest Descent | Cauchy, 1847 | Machine learning, unconstrained NLP |
| 4 | $c^T \mathbf{x} \rightarrow \min, \; A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \ge 0$ | Linear Programming | Kantorovich (1939), Dantzig (1947) | Production planning, logistics |
| 5 | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum \mu_i \nabla h_i + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j = 0$ | KKT Conditions | Karush (1939), Kuhn–Tucker (1951) | All modern constrained NLP solvers |
| 6 | $J^* = \min \{ d_k + J^*_{k+1} \}$ | Principle of Optimality | Bellman, 1957 | Sequential decision-making, DP |
| 7 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1}_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Newton's Method | Newton–Raphson, c. 1690 | Fast local convergence in NLP |
| 8 | $P(i, t+1) = \left[\dfrac{P(i, t)^\alpha}{\sum_j P(j, t)^\alpha}\right] \cdot \mathbf{x}_i(t)$ | Particle Swarm | Kennedy–Eberhart, 1995 | Global optimization, feature selection |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Historical Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every modern CAD/CAE solver (ANSYS, COMSOL, MATLAB Optimization Toolbox) implements algorithms with direct lineage to the historical figures above. When you click "Optimize" in ANSYS, the underlying routine descends from Dantzig's simplex or Karmarkar's interior-point method.

- **Aerospace** — trajectory optimization (Euler–Lagrange, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, 1956).
- **Manufacturing** — cutting-stock and production scheduling (LP, integer programming).
- **Finance** — portfolio optimization (Markowitz, 1952 — derived from LP/NLP theory).
- **Telecommunications** — network flow (Ford–Fulkerson algorithm, 1956).
- **Machine Learning** — every loss minimization is a Cauchy steepest-descent variant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the topic "Historical Development" is largely **conceptual**, KTU examiners expect a student to be able to **derive or reproduce the foundational result** of calculus of variations — the **Euler–Lagrange equation** — as it is the *historical root* from which all of optimization springs. Below is the exhaustive derivation, with every algebraic step explicitly shown.

### 3.1 Derivation of the Euler–Lagrange Equation (Historical Foundation)

**Problem Statement (Bernoulli, 1696 → Euler, 1755):**
Find the function $y(x)$ that *extremizes* the functional:

$$
J[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x, y(x), y'(x)\right) dx
$$

subject to the fixed boundary conditions $y(x_1) = y_1$ and $y(x_2) = y_2$.

**Step 1 — Parametrize the Candidate Function.**
Let $y^*(x)$ be the *true extremal*. Consider a *one-parameter family* of comparison functions:

$$
y(x, \varepsilon) = y^*(x) + \varepsilon \, \eta(x)
$$

where $\eta(x)$ is a *smooth, arbitrary* function satisfying $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$ (to preserve the boundary conditions), and $\varepsilon$ is a small scalar parameter.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to the Parameter.**
The corresponding derivative is:

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}(x, \varepsilon) = y^{*'}(x) + \varepsilon \, \eta'(x)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Functional.**
Define $J(\varepsilon)$ as the value of the functional evaluated along the family $y(x, \varepsilon)$:

$$
J(\varepsilon) = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x,\; y^*(x) + \varepsilon \eta(x),\; y^{*'}(x) + \varepsilon \eta'(x)\right) dx
$$

**Step 4 — Impose the First-Order Optimality (Necessary) Condition.**
Since $y^*(x)$ is the extremal, $J(\varepsilon)$ attains a stationary value at $\varepsilon = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\left.\frac{dJ}{d\varepsilon}\right|_{\varepsilon = 0} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate Under the Integral Sign (Leibniz Rule).**

$$
\frac{dJ}{d\varepsilon} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \cdot \eta(x) + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} \cdot \eta'(x) \right] dx
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Fundamental Lemma of the Calculus of Variations.**
For the equality to hold for *every* admissible $\eta(x)$, the integrand coefficient must vanish. This is the *Fundamental Lemma* (proved by Euler). Hence:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Detailed Justification via Integration by Parts.**
For completeness, we explicitly show the integration-by-parts step on the second term:

$$
\int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} \cdot \eta'(x) \, dx
= \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} \cdot \eta(x) \right]_{x_1}^{x_2} - \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \eta(x) \, dx
$$

Since $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes:

$$
= - \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \eta(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 8 — Combine the Two Integrals.**

$$
\frac{dJ}{d\varepsilon}\bigg|_{\varepsilon=0} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \right] \eta(x) \, dx = 0
$$

Since this must hold for *arbitrary* $\eta(x)$, the bracketed expression must equal zero identically, yielding the **Euler–Lagrange Equation** of the Calculus of Variations.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Historical Algorithm → Modern Equivalent

| Historical Algorithm | Year | Key Idea | Modern Software Implementation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brachistochrone (Bernoulli) | 1696 | First variational problem | Optimal control in MATLAB (`fmincon`) |
| Lagrange Multipliers | 1788 | Constrained extrema | `scipy.optimize.minimize` (SLSQP) |
| Steepest Descent (Cauchy) | 1847 | $-\nabla f$ direction | Gradient descent in PyTorch / TensorFlow |
| Kantorovich's Transportation LP | 1939 | Optimize commodity flow | `linprog`, Gurobi, CPLEX |
| Simplex Method (Dantzig) | 1947 | Vertex traversal of polytope | `linprog` (default), Excel Solver |
| KKT Conditions | 1951 | Optimality in nonlinear programs | All NLP solvers (fmincon, IPOPT) |
| Dynamic Programming (Bellman) | 1957 | Sequential optimality | Reinforcement learning algorithms |
| Karmarkar's Interior Point | 1984 | Polynomial-time LP | `linprog` (interior-point legacy) |
| Genetic Algorithm (Holland) | 1975 | Population-based search | `deap`, MATLAB `ga` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note for KTU Students:** The Euler–Lagrange equation derivation above is the **single most important analytical result** in the historical lineage. Even if Module 1 of your course is largely conceptual, this derivation is a *gateway* to all of constrained optimization (Module 2 onward).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
