topics.title: Classification of Optimization Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Optimization Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Optimization Problem** is a mathematical formulation that seeks to find the best (optimal) value of one or more objective functions subject to a set of constraints defined over a feasible domain. **Classification of Optimization Problems** refers to the systematic categorization of these problems based on structural and functional properties such as the nature of the objective function, the type of decision variables, the presence or absence of constraints, the number of objectives, and the underlying assumptions on data and time.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, this topic forms the foundational pillar of Module 1 because the classification of a problem dictates the entire downstream choice of algorithm, convergence conditions, and computational strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to identify the class of an optimization problem from its standard mathematical form and recommend a corresponding solution technique.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Doctor's Toolbox"**
> Imagine you visit a doctor with a fever. The doctor does not prescribe a single universal medicine. First, the doctor classifies your condition: Is it viral or bacterial? Acute or chronic? Mild or severe? Only after this classification does the doctor select the correct medicine and dosage.
>
> In exactly the same way, before solving an optimization problem, an engineer must classify it: *Is it linear or non-linear? Constrained or unconstrained? Single-objective or multi-objective?* This classification acts like a "diagnosis" that determines which "medicine" (algorithm — Simplex, KKT, Dynamic Programming, Genetic Algorithm, etc.) will cure the problem efficiently.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters

- **Algorithm Selection**: Different problem classes require fundamentally different solution techniques.
- **Convergence Guarantees**: Global optimality is assured only in certain classes (e.g., convex problems).
- **Computational Complexity**: Classification informs whether a problem is **P**, **NP-hard**, or **PSPACE**.
- **Real-World Modelling**: Engineers in manufacturing, logistics, VLSI design, and finance all encounter different problem classes, and KTU expects students to map applications to problem classes correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Mathematical Statement of an Optimization Problem

The **canonical (standard) form** of an optimization problem is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n \\
\text{that minimizes (or maximizes) } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i \in \mathbb{R}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (or cost / fitness function).
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the vector of **decision variables**.
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ are **inequality constraints**.
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are **equality constraints**.

### 2.2 Primary Classification Criteria (Structured Logic)

The classification of an optimization problem proceeds along **eight principal axes**:

**1. Based on the Nature of the Objective Function**
- **Linear Programming (LP)**: $f(\mathbf{x})$ and all constraints are linear.
- **Non-Linear Programming (NLP)**: At least one of $f$ or constraints is non-linear.

**2. Based on the Presence of Constraints**
- **Unconstrained Optimization**: No $g_j$ or $h_k$ exist.
- **Constrained Optimization**: At least one $g_j$ or $h_k$ exists.

**3. Based on the Nature of Decision Variables**
- **Continuous Variable Optimization**: $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Discrete / Integer Programming (IP)**: $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP)**: A combination of both.

**4. Based on the Number of Objective Functions**
- **Single-Objective Optimization**: One $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Multi-Objective Optimization**: Multiple conflicting objectives — solved using Pareto optimality.

**5. Based on Differentiability / Smoothness**
- **Smooth (Differentiable) Optimization**: Gradients and Hessians exist everywhere.
- **Non-Smooth Optimization**: Contains absolute values, max/min, or step functions.

**6. Based on Convexity of the Feasible Region and Objective**
- **Convex Optimization**: $f$ is convex and the feasible set is convex → guarantees global optimum.
- **Non-Convex Optimization**: May contain multiple local optima.

**7. Based on Time Dependence**
- **Static Optimization**: Parameters do not change with time.
- **Dynamic Optimization**: Variables/parameters evolve with time (uses calculus of variations, DP).

**8. Based on Determinism of Data**
- **Deterministic Optimization**: All parameters are known constants.
- **Stochastic / Probabilistic Optimization**: Parameters are random variables.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Classification Axis** | **Sub-Class** | **Mathematical Identifier** | **Typical Algorithm** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Objective Function | Linear Programming (LP) | $f, g_j, h_k$ all linear | Simplex, Interior Point | Production planning |
| Objective Function | Non-Linear Programming (NLP) | At least one quadratic/cubic/transcendental | KKT Conditions, Gradient Methods | Trajectory optimization |
| Constraints | Unconstrained | $m = 0, \; p = 0$ | Steepest Descent, Newton's | Curve fitting |
| Constraints | Constrained | $m + p \ge 1$ | Lagrange Multipliers, Penalty | Structural design |
| Decision Variables | Integer Programming (IP) | $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Branch and Bound | Facility location |
| Decision Variables | Mixed Integer (MIP) | Some $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$, rest $\in \mathbb{R}$ | Branch and Bound + Cutting Planes | VLSI floor planning |
| Number of Objectives | Single-Objective | One $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Simplex, KKT, DP | Minimum cost routing |
| Number of Objectives | Multi-Objective | Vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1, \dots, f_k)$ | Weighted Sum, $\epsilon$-constraint, NSGA-II | Antenna design (gain vs bandwidth) |
| Differentiability | Smooth | $\nabla f, \nabla^2 f$ exist | Newton, Quasi-Newton | Aerodynamic optimization |
| Differentiability | Non-Smooth | Contains $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\max(\cdot)$ | Subgradient, Bundle Method | Robust optimization |
| Convexity | Convex | $f$ convex, $S$ convex | Polynomial-time algorithms | Portfolio selection |
| Convexity | Non-Convex | Multiple local minima | Heuristics, Metaheuristics | Neural network training |
| Time Dependence | Static | $f(\mathbf{x})$ only | LP, NLP methods | Inventory at fixed instant |
| Time Dependence | Dynamic | $f(t, \mathbf{x}(t), \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t))$ | Calculus of Variations, Pontryagin | Rocket trajectory |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Known parameters | All classical methods | Linear regression |
| Determinism | Stochastic | Random parameters with distributions | Chance-Constrained, SAA | Supply chain under demand uncertainty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Classification is the first step in **Industry 4.0 digital twin pipelines**, where every engineering sub-problem must be tagged with its optimization class before being dispatched to the appropriate solver module (Gurobi for MIP, IPOPT for NLP, MOEA for multi-objective, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Classification Decision Workflow — Derivation of the Identification Steps

To classify an optimization problem rigorously, follow this **five-step analytical procedure**:

**Step 1 — Inspect the Objective Function $f(\mathbf{x})$**
Count the highest-degree terms. If every term is of degree $\le 1$ and the coefficients are constants → **Linear**. Otherwise → **Non-Linear** (Quadratic, Cubic, Exponential, etc.).

**Step 2 — Inspect the Constraints**
If there are no constraints ($m = 0$ and $p = 0$) → **Unconstrained**. Otherwise → **Constrained**. Count inequality vs equality constraints for further classification (e.g., Linear Fractional Programming).

**Step 3 — Inspect the Decision Variable Domain**
Check if $x_i$ is restricted to integers, binary $\{0,1\}$, or mixed. This determines whether the problem belongs to **IP**, **Binary Programming**, or **MIP**.

**Step 4 — Inspect Multiplicity of Objectives**
A scalar $f(\mathbf{x})$ indicates **single-objective**. A vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ indicates **multi-objective** requiring Pareto analysis.

**Step 5 — Inspect Determinism and Time Dependence**
Check whether inputs are random → **Stochastic**. Check whether $f$ depends explicitly on $t$ → **Dynamic**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Identifying an LP Problem

Consider the problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & f(x_1, x_2) = 3x_1 + 5x_2 \\
\text{subject to } & x_1 + x_2 \ge 4 \\
& 2x_1 - x_2 \le 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification Analysis (line-by-line logic):**

- The objective $3x_1 + 5x_2$ is **linear** (degree 1 in every variable).
- All constraints are linear inequalities → equality case would also be linear.
- $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ with $x_i \ge 0$ → **continuous** variables.
- Only one objective function → **single-objective**.
- All coefficients are constants → **deterministic**.
- Time-invariant form → **static**.
- Hence: **Linear Programming (LP) problem → solve using Simplex Method**.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Identifying a Non-Linear Convex Problem

Consider the problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 \\
\text{subject to } & x_1 + x_2 \ge 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification Analysis:**

- The objective contains $x_1^2$ and $x_2^2$ → degree 2 → **non-linear** (quadratic).
- There is one inequality constraint → **constrained**.
- Variables are continuous.
- Hessian of $f$ is $H = 2I$, which is **positive definite** → objective is **strictly convex** → feasible region is convex → **Convex NLP**.

### 3.4 Analytical Classification Matrix (Master Reference Table)

| **Given Property in the Problem Statement** | **Implied Classification** | **Reasoning (Conversion Logic)** |
|---|---|---|
| "All terms in $f$ and constraints are of first degree" | **Linear Programming** | Highest power of any variable is 1 |
| "Variables restricted to whole numbers" | **Integer Programming** | Discrete domain $\mathbb{Z}$ |
| "Variables restricted to 0 or 1" | **Binary / 0-1 Programming** | Subset of IP |
| "Find minimum of $f_1$ and $f_2$ simultaneously" | **Multi-Objective Programming** | Vector-valued objective |
| "Demand follows a normal distribution" | **Stochastic Optimization** | Random parameters |
| "Find optimal control $u(t)$ over time $[0,T]$" | **Dynamic Optimization** | Time-indexed variables |
| "Objective is $\sum c_i \vert x_i \vert$" | **Non-Smooth Optimization** | Absolute value is non-differentiable at 0 |
| "Objective is $x^4 - 4x^2$" | **Non-Convex (Multi-Modal)** | Polynomial of degree $\ge 4$ with saddle points |

### 3.5 Derivation of KKT Conditions (Foundational for Constrained Classification)

For a **constrained smooth NLP** with inequality and equality constraints, the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions provide the first-order necessary optimality criteria:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \\
& \mu_j \cdot g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall j \in \{1, \dots, m\} \\
& \mu_j \ge 0, \quad \forall j \in \{1, \dots, m\} \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall k \in \{1, \dots, p\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each logical line:**

1. **Stationarity**: The gradient of the Lagrangian at the optimum must vanish.
2. **Complementary Slackness**: An inactive constraint ($g_j < 0$) must have its multiplier $\mu_j = 0$.
3. **Dual Feasibility**: Inequality multipliers must be non-negative.
4. **Primal Feasibility**: Equality constraints must hold exactly at the optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> KKT conditions are the **bridge between classification and algorithm choice** — any problem classified as "Constrained Smooth NLP" defaults to a KKT-based solver.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Optimization Problems]
    rootA --> axisA1[Based on Objective]
    rootA --> axisA2[Based on Constraints]
    rootA --> axisA3[Based on Variables]
    rootA --> axisA4[Based on Objectives Count]
    rootA --> axisA5[Based on Time]
    rootA --> axisA6[Based on Determinism]
    rootA --> axisA7[Based on Differentiability]
    rootA --> axisA8[Based on Convexity]

    axisA1 --> lpA[Linear Programming]
    axisA1 --> nlpA[Non Linear Programming]
    nlpA --> quadA[Quadratic Programming]
    nlpA --> geoA[Geometric Programming]
    nlpA --> fracA[Fractional Programming]

    axisA2 --> uncA[Unconstrained]
    axisA2 --> conA[Constrained]
    conA --> boundA[Bound Constrained]
    conA --> lineqA[Linear Constraints]
    conA --> nonlinqA[Nonlinear Constraints]

    axisA3 --> contA[Continuous Variables]
    axisA3 --> intA[Integer Variables]
    intA --> pureA[Pure Integer]
    intA --> binA[Binary Zero One]
    contA --> mixA[Mixed Integer Programming]

    axisA4 --> soA[Single Objective]
    axisA4 --> moA[Multi Objective]

    axisA5 --> staticA[Static Optimization]
    axisA5 --> dynamicA[Dynamic Optimization]

    axisA6 --> detA[Deterministic]
    axisA6 --> stochA[Stochastic]

    axisA7 --> smoothA[Smooth Differentiable]
    axisA7 --> nonsmoothA[Non Smooth]

    axisA8 --> convA[Convex]
    axisA8 --> nonconvA[Non Convex]
    convA --> lcpA[Linear Convex]
    convA --> qcpA[Quadratically Constrained]
```

### 4.2 Algorithm-Selection Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[Problem Statement] --> classNode{Classify}
    classNode --> lpNode[LP Branch]
    classNode --> nlpNode[NLP Branch]
    classNode --> ipNode[IP Branch]
    classNode --> moNode[MO Branch]
    classNode --> dynNode[Dynamic Branch]

    lpNode --> algoA1[Simplex Method]
    lpNode --> algoA2[Interior Point]

    nlpNode --> algoB1[KKT Conditions]
    nlpNode --> algoB2[Penalty Methods]
    nlpNode --> algoB3[Gradient Descent]

    ipNode --> algoC1[Branch and Bound]
    ipNode --> algoC2[Cutting Planes]

    moNode --> algoD1[Weighted Sum]
    moNode --> algoD2[NSGA II]

    dynNode --> algoE1[Calculus of Variations]
    dynNode --> algoE2[Bellman Dynamic Programming]
```

### 4.3 Interpretation Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Key:** Each branch in the diagram represents an **axis of classification**. A real-world problem may simultaneously belong to multiple axes (e.g., "Dynamic Multi-Objective Stochastic NLP" — common in aerospace trajectory optimization). The algorithm chosen is the intersection of all relevant branches.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define Linear Programming Problem. Mention any two real-world applications where LP is used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Linear Programming Problem (LPP) is an optimization problem in which the objective function and all constraints (equality as well as inequality) are **linear functions** of the decision variables.

Mathematical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize/Minimize } & Z = \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} \\
\text{subject to } & A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **Production Planning** in manufacturing — to maximize profit subject to resource (raw material, labour hour) constraints.
2. **Transportation / Distribution Problem** — to minimize shipping cost subject to supply and demand constraints.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Form (1 mark) + Applications (1 mark).

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** Distinguish between **convex** and **non-convex** optimization problems with a one-line mathematical example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Convex Problem** | **Non-Convex Problem** |
|---|---|---|
| Hessian / Region shape | Bowl-shaped, single global minimum | Multi-modal, several local minima |
| Guarantee | Local optimum = Global optimum | No such guarantee |
| Example | $f(x) = x^2$ | $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^2$ |

- **Convex Example:** $\min f(x) = x^2$ → unique global minimum at $x^* = 0$.
- **Non-Convex Example:** $\min f(x) = x^4 - 4x^2$ → has two local minima at $x = \pm\sqrt{2}$ and a local maximum at $x = 0$.

**Mark Distribution:** Distinction table (2 marks) + Examples (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q:** With the help of standard mathematical forms, classify optimization problems along **any six** classification axes. For each axis, state the identifying feature and give one engineering example.

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**(a) Classification along six axes — identifying features (7 Marks):**

| **S.No.** | **Axis** | **Identifying Feature in the Problem Statement** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear vs Non-Linear | Highest degree of any term in $f$ or constraint |
| 2 | Constrained vs Unconstrained | Presence of $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ or $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ |
| 3 | Continuous vs Integer | Domain restriction $\mathbb{R}$ vs $\mathbb{Z}$ |
| 4 | Single vs Multi-Objective | Scalar $f$ vs vector $\mathbf{F} = (f_1, \dots, f_k)$ |
| 5 | Static vs Dynamic | Absence vs presence of $t$ in $f$ or variables |
| 6 | Deterministic vs Stochastic | Known constants vs random variables in data |

**(b) Engineering example for each axis (7 Marks):**

1. **Linear/Non-Linear:** Linear — Production mix problem in a factory. Non-Linear — Minimum drag profile design in aerospace.
2. **Constrained/Unconstrained:** Constrained — Bridge truss weight minimization under stress limits. Unconstrained — Curve fitting in regression analysis.
3. **Continuous/Integer:** Continuous — Fuel blending ratios. Integer — Number of machines to purchase in a plant.
4. **Single/Multi-Objective:** Single — Shortest path in a road network. Multi — Vehicle design with conflicting objectives of fuel efficiency, safety, and cost.
5. **Static/Dynamic:** Static — Portfolio selection at a fixed instant. Dynamic — Optimal rocket trajectory over flight duration.
6. **Deterministic/Stochastic:** Deterministic — Linear regression with known data. Stochastic — Inventory management under uncertain customer demand.

---

#### **Question B — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** For each of the following three problems, **identify the class** of optimization problem it belongs to, justify with one line of mathematical reasoning, and suggest the most appropriate solution algorithm.

**Problem (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } & x^2 + 2y^2 - 4x - 8y \\
\text{s.t. } & x + y \le 6 \\
& x, y \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Problem (ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } & 5x_1 + 3x_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & x_1 + x_2 \ge 10 \\
& x_1, x_2 \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, \dots\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Problem (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } & f_1(x) = x^2 \\
\text{Min } & f_2(x) = (x - 2)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**(a) Classification of each problem (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3):**

**Problem (i):** This is a **Quadratic Programming (QP) / Convex NLP** problem.
- **Reasoning:** Objective contains $x^2$ and $y^2$ terms → quadratic (non-linear). Hessian $H = 2I$ is positive definite → convex. Constraint is linear.
- **Algorithm:** Active-Set Method or KKT Conditions.

**Problem (ii):** This is an **Integer Linear Programming (ILP)** problem.
- **Reasoning:** Objective and constraint are linear, but $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ restricts them to non-negative integers.
- **Algorithm:** Branch and Bound Method or Cutting Plane Method.

**Problem (iii):** This is a **Multi-Objective (Bi-Objective) Optimization** problem.
- **Reasoning:** Two scalar objective functions $f_1$ and $f_2$ are simultaneously minimized → vector-valued objective $\mathbf{F} = (f_1, f_2)$.
- **Algorithm:** Weighted Sum Method or $\epsilon$-Constraint Method to obtain Pareto front.

**(b) Justification using mathematical properties and engineering context (7 Marks):**

| **Problem** | **Mathematical Property** | **Engineering Context** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) QP | Hessian PD + linear constraint | Economic dispatch in power systems |
| (ii) ILP | Integrality constraint + linearity | Capital budgeting / project selection |
| (iii) MOO | Conflicting convex objectives | Antenna design (gain vs cost) |

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Linear Fractional Programming" with LP** — if the objective is a ratio of two linear functions, the problem is **non-linear**, not linear.
> 2. **In Multi-Objective problems**, do not simply add the objectives using arbitrary weights — justify why the chosen weights are valid (Pareto dominance argument).
> 3. **Dynamic vs Static** — if a problem says "over time $[0,T]$" or contains $\dot{x}(t)$, it is **Dynamic** even if the function looks simple.
> 4. **Stochastic vs Deterministic** — if data contains the words "random", "distribution", "expected", the problem is **Stochastic** and classical LP/NLP algorithms do not directly apply.
> 5. **Always mention the algorithm after classification** — KTU examiners award marks for the *solution strategy*, not just the label.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **LPP** → Simplex / Interior Point. Objective and constraints must be linear.
- **NLP** → KKT / Penalty / Gradient. At least one non-linear element.
- **QP** → Active Set / Interior Point. Quadratic objective with linear constraints.
- **IP / ILP** → Branch and Bound. Decision variables are integers.
- **Binary (0-1) Programming** → Special case of ILP; used in selection/allocation.
- **MIP** → Mixed Integer Programming; combine branch-and-bound with continuous LP.
- **Multi-Objective** → Pareto Front, Weighted Sum, NSGA-II.
- **Dynamic** → Calculus of Variations, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, Bellman DP.
- **Stochastic** → Chance-Constrained Programming, Sample Average Approximation.
- **Convex** → Single global optimum guaranteed (e.g., LP, QP with PD Hessian).
- **Non-Convex** → Heuristics required (Genetic Algorithm, Simulated Annealing, PSO).
- **Smooth** → Gradient methods applicable.
- **Non-Smooth** → Sub-gradient / Bundle methods required.
- **Static** → Time-invariant.
- **Deterministic** → All inputs known constants.
- **Master Classification Mnemonic:** **L-I-C-C-M-D-S-S** → Linear, Integer, Constrained, Convex, Multi-objective, Dynamic, Stochastic, Smooth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Non-linear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Non-linear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Optimization Techniques**, every real-world decision-making problem (such as minimizing production cost, maximizing profit, or optimally allocating limited resources) can be mathematically modeled as a mathematical programming problem. These problems are broadly classified based on the **nature of the objective function** and the **nature of the constraints** into two fundamental categories:

### A. Linear Programming Problem (LPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Linear Programming Problem (LPP)**
> A **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** is an optimization problem in which:
> 1. The **objective function** is a linear function of the decision variables.
> 2. All **constraints** (including resource, technological, and non-negativity constraints) are linear inequalities or equalities.
> 3. The decision variables are restricted to be **non-negative** (in standard form).

**General Standard Form of an LPP:**

$$\text{Optimize } Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$$

Subject to:

$$a_{11} x_1 + a_{11} x_2 + \cdots + a_{1n} x_n \leq b_1$$

$$a_{11} x_1 + a_{11} x_2 + \cdots + a_{1n} x_n \leq b_2$$

$$\vdots$$

$$a_{m1} x_1 + a_{m2} x_2 + \cdots + a_{mn} x_n \leq b_m$$

$$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \geq 0$$

### B. Non-Linear Programming Problem (NLPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box — Non-Linear Programming Problem (NLPP)**
> A **Non-Linear Programming Problem (NLPP)** is an optimization problem in which **at least one** of the following is true:
> 1. The **objective function** is a non-linear function of the decision variables (e.g., contains $x_j^2$, $x_i x_j$, $e^{x_j}$, $\sqrt{x_j}$, $\log x_j$, $\vert x_j \vert$, etc.).
> 2. **Any** of the constraints involve non-linear expressions in the decision variables.
> 3. Both objective and constraints may be non-linear simultaneously.

**General Form of an NLPP:**

$$\text{Optimize } Z = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

Subject to:

$$g_i(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

$$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \geq 0$$

where $f(\cdot)$ and/or $g_i(\cdot)$ are **non-linear** functions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pizza Bakery Problem**
>
> Imagine you own a small pizza bakery that makes two products — **Cheese Pizzas** (₹100 profit each) and **Veggie Pizzas** (₹150 profit each). You have limited flour (resource constraint), limited oven time, and limited staff hours.
>
> - **LINEAR scenario (LPP):** If profit, flour usage, and time all scale **proportionally** with the number of pizzas (i.e., doubling pizzas exactly doubles profit and exactly doubles resource consumption), then your profit function and constraints are **straight-line relationships**. You can find the optimum by drawing straight lines on a graph — this is the world of LPP.
>
> - **NON-LINEAR scenario (NLPP):** Suppose there are **economies of scale** — the 10th pizza costs less flour than the 1st (because of bulk mixing), OR there is a **setup cost** that makes small batches inefficient. Suddenly your cost function is curved ($x^2$ or $\sqrt{x}$). Also, your profit might rise rapidly up to a point and then **fall** (because of market saturation). Now you cannot use straight lines — you need curves, calculus, and gradient-based methods. This is the world of NLPP.

**Intuitive Summary Table:**

| Feature | LPP (Linear) | NLPP (Non-Linear) |
|---|---|---|
| Shape of feasible region | Polygon / Polyhedron | Curved surface / Convex set / Irregular region |
| Solution location | Vertex (corner point) of feasible region | Interior point / Boundary / Multiple isolated points |
| Methods | Simplex, Graphical, Big-M, Dual Simplex | KKT Conditions, Lagrange Multipliers, Gradient methods |
| Difficulty | Polynomial-time solvable | Often NP-hard, requires iterative numerical methods |
| Nature of optimum | Global optimum guaranteed (for convex LPP) | Local vs Global optimum issues |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Optimization Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Classification Matrix**
> Every optimization problem is classified along **two dimensions**:
>
> **Dimension 1: Nature of Objective Function and Constraints**
> - Linear in all variables and constraints → **LPP**
> - Non-linear in objective OR constraint (or both) → **NLP/NLPP**
>
> **Dimension 2: Nature of Variables**
> - Continuous (real-valued) → **Continuous Optimization**
> - Discrete / Integer-valued → **Integer Programming**
> - Mixed → **Mixed Integer Programming**

### Hierarchy of Optimization Problem Types

$$
\text{Optimization} \begin{cases}
\text{Linear Programming (LP)} \\
\text{Non-Linear Programming (NLP)} \begin{cases}
\text{Unconstrained NLP} \\ \text{Constrained NLP} \begin{cases}
\text{Equality Constrained} \\ \text{Inequality Constrained} \end{cases}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 1.4 Engineering Significance — Why This Distinction Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
>
> 1. **Civil Engineering:** Optimal truss design (linear elasticity → LPP), but with large deformations → NLPP.
> 2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Minimizing weight of a pressure vessel (volume is cubic → NLPP).
> 3. **Electrical Engineering:** Optimal power flow in a grid (Kirchhoff's laws give linear network equations, but generator cost curves are quadratic → NLPP).
> 4. **Chemical Engineering:** Maximizing yield of a reactor (Arrhenius kinetics → exponential non-linearities → NLPP).
> 5. **Operations Research:** Airline crew scheduling, supply chain network design → often LPP after linearization.

The distinction between **Linear** and **Non-Linear** is the **first and most fundamental classification** a KTU student must master, because it determines the entire algorithmic approach to solving the problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Characterization of Linearity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearity Test (Superposition Principle)**
> A function $f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is **linear** if and only if it satisfies BOTH:
>
> **Additivity:** $f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y)$
>
> **Homogeneity:** $f(\alpha x) = \alpha f(x)$ for all scalars $\alpha$
>
> Equivalently, a linear function can always be written as:
>
> $$f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$$
>
> **No terms** of the form $x_i^2$, $x_i x_j$, $e^{x_k}$, $\log x_k$, $\sqrt{x_k}$, or $\vert x_j \vert$ are allowed.

### Examples — Classify Each Function as Linear or Non-Linear

| Function | Linear / Non-Linear | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $f = 3x_1 + 5x_2 - 7x_3$ | **Linear** | Pure first-degree terms only |
| $f = x_1^2 + x_2$ | **Non-Linear** | Contains $x_1^2$ (degree 2 term) |
| $f = x_1 x_2 + 4x_3$ | **Non-Linear** | Product term $x_1 x_2$ (bilinear) |
| $f = e^{x_1} + x_2$ | **Non-Linear** | Exponential term |
| $f = \sqrt{x_1} + x_2$ | **Non-Linear** | Square root / fractional power |
| $f = \log x_1 + 2x_2$ | **Non-Linear** | Logarithmic term |
| $f = \vert x_1 \vert + x_2$ | **Non-Linear** | Absolute value (not differentiable at 0) |
| Constraint: $x_1 x_2 \leq 10$ | **Non-Linear Constraint** | Product of variables |

---

## 2.2 Properties of Linear Programming Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Structural Properties (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams)**
>
> 1. **Convex Feasible Region:** The feasible region of an LPP is always a **convex polyhedron** (or convex polygon in 2D). This guarantees that any local optimum is also the **global optimum**.
> 2. **Optimal Solution at a Vertex:** If an LPP has an optimal solution, at least one optimal solution occurs at an **extreme point (corner point / vertex)** of the feasible region. *(Fundamental Theorem of LPP — proven by George Dantzig.)*
> 3. **Linearity of Objective:** The objective function is a **hyperplane** in $n$-dimensional space. As we move along the feasible region, $Z$ changes at a constant rate (the gradient $\nabla Z = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n)$ is constant).
> 4. **Multiple Solutions:** If the objective function is parallel to a binding constraint, there may be an **infinite number of optimal solutions** along an edge.
> 5. **Unboundedness:** If the feasible region is unbounded and the objective can improve without limit, the LPP has an **unbounded solution**.
> 6. **Infeasibility:** If constraints contradict each other, the LPP has **no feasible solution**.

---

## 2.3 Properties of Non-Linear Programming Problems

> [!WARNING]
> **NLPP — The Complexity Jump (Examiner's Hot Spot)**
>
> Unlike LPP, NLPP exhibits several pathological behaviors that make it fundamentally harder:
>
> 1. **Local vs Global Optimum Trap:** NLPP can have many local optima (peaks/valleys), and an algorithm may converge to a **sub-optimal local solution** instead of the true global optimum.
> 2. **Non-Convexity:** The feasible region and level sets can be highly non-convex, with disconnected components, holes, and irregular boundaries.
> 3. **Solution at Interior or Boundary:** Unlike LPP, the optimum of an NLPP can occur in the **interior** of the feasible region (where no constraint is active) — this is a **stationary point** satisfying $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$.
> 4. **Multiple Stationary Points:** The gradient equation $\nabla f(x) = 0$ may have multiple roots — each must be checked using **second-order conditions** (Hessian matrix test).
> 5. **Constraint Qualification Issues:** Methods like Lagrange multipliers require **regularity conditions** (LICQ — Linear Independence Constraint Qualification) which may fail at certain boundary points.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Master Formula Reference Table**

| S.No. | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard LPP Objective | $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Maximize or Minimize |
| 2 | LPP Constraints | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ for $i = 1,\ldots,m$ | Linear inequalities |
| 3 | Non-Negativity | $x_j \geq 0$ for all $j$ | Standard form assumption |
| 4 | Gradient of LPP Objective | $\nabla Z = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n)^T$ | Constant vector (constant rate of change) |
| 5 | Stationary Point Condition (Unconstrained NLP) | $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ | First-order necessary condition |
| 6 | Hessian Matrix | $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Symmetric matrix for $C^2$ functions |
| 7 | Second-Order Sufficient Condition | $H(x^*) > 0 \Rightarrow$ local minimum | Positive definite Hessian |
| 8 | Second-Order Sufficient Condition | $H(x^*) < 0 \Rightarrow$ local maximum | Negative definite Hessian |
| 9 | Lagrangian Function | $L(x, \lambda) = f(x) - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(x)$ | For equality constraints $g_i(x) = 0$ |
| 10 | KKT Stationarity | $\nabla f(x^*) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(x^*)$ | Generalized Lagrange condition |
| 11 | KKT Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i^* \cdot g_i(x^*) = 0$ for all $i$ | Active constraint multiplier is non-zero |
| 12 | KKT Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_i^* \geq 0$ for inequality $g_i \leq 0$ | Sign convention depends on formulation |
| 13 | KKT Primal Feasibility | $g_i(x^*) \leq 0$ for all $i$ | Original constraints satisfied |
| 14 | Lagrangian Multiplier Bound | $\lambda_i = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial b_i}\bigg\vert_{opt}$ | Shadow price / marginal worth of resource |

---

## 2.5 Linearity vs Non-Linearity — Side-by-Side Engineering Comparison

| Aspect | Linear Programming | Non-Linear Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Function Form** | $f(x) = c^T x$ (hyperplane) | $f(x)$ arbitrary (curved) |
| **Constraint Form** | $Ax \leq b$ (half-spaces) | $g_i(x) \leq 0$ (general) |
| **Feasible Region** | Convex polyhedron | May be non-convex |
| **Solution Method** | Simplex Method (Dantzig, 1947) | KKT, Penalty, Barrier, Gradient |
| **Number of Optima** | At most one optimum face (could be infinite on an edge) | Multiple local optima possible |
| **Optimality Condition** | Vertex enumeration / Simplex pivoting | First & second-order calculus conditions |
| **Computational Cost** | Polynomial in $n, m$ (interior point methods) | Often NP-hard, iterative |
| **Sensitivity Analysis** | Dual variables give marginal values directly | Computationally intensive |
| **Software Tools** | Excel Solver, CPLEX, LINGO | MATLAB `fmincon`, Python `scipy.optimize`, GAMS |

---

## 2.6 Why the Distinction Matters — Engineering Decision Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Decision Rule (Frequently Tested Conceptually)**
>
> 1. If the objective function has degree $\leq 1$ in ALL variables AND
> 2. Every constraint has degree $\leq 1$ in ALL variables AND
> 3. No products of variables appear (no $x_i x_j$ terms)
>
> → The problem is an **LPP** → Use **Simplex Method**.
>
> If ANY of the above conditions fails → It is an **NLPP** → Use **KKT / Calculus-Based Methods**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Optimality Condition for Unconstrained NLPP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC) for Local Optimum**
>
> **Statement:** If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at $x^*$ and $x^*$ is a local minimum (or maximum) of $f$ with no constraints, then the gradient must vanish:
>
> $$\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$$
>
> i.e., all first partial derivatives must equal zero:
>
> $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x=x^*} = 0 \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Taylor Series Argument)

**Step 1:** Since $f$ is differentiable at $x^*$, by the definition of differentiability, the first-order Taylor expansion around $x^*$ is:

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + \nabla f(x^*)^T h + o(\Vert h \Vert)
$$

where $h = (h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_n)^T$ is an arbitrary small perturbation and $o(\Vert h \Vert)$ denotes terms of order strictly smaller than $\Vert h \Vert$.

**Step 2:** Suppose $x^*$ is a **local minimum**. Then for sufficiently small $\Vert h \Vert$, we must have:

$$
f(x^* + h) \geq f(x^*)
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the Taylor expansion from Step 1 into the inequality from Step 2:

$$
f(x^*) + \nabla f(x^*)^T h + o(\Vert h \Vert) \geq f(x^*)
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $f(x^*)$ from both sides:

$$
\nabla f(x^*)^T h + o(\Vert h \Vert) \geq 0
$$

**Step 5:** Since $h$ can be chosen in **any direction** (positive or negative), pick $h = \epsilon e_j$ (a small positive scalar times the $j$-th unit vector). Then $h_j = \epsilon > 0$ and $h_k = 0$ for $k \neq j$. Substituting:

$$
\epsilon \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x^*} + o(\epsilon) \geq 0
$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides by $\epsilon > 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x^*} + \frac{o(\epsilon)}{\epsilon} \geq 0
$$

**Step 7:** Taking the limit as $\epsilon \rightarrow 0^+$, the term $\dfrac{o(\epsilon)}{\epsilon} \rightarrow 0$ by definition of little-o, leaving:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x^*} \geq 0
$$

**Step 8:** Repeating Steps 5–7 with $h = -\epsilon e_j$ gives:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x^*} \leq 0
$$

**Step 9:** Combining Steps 7 and 8, we conclude:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}\bigg\vert_{x^*} = 0 \quad \text{for all } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

This is equivalent to $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Second-Order Sufficient Condition (Hessian Test)

> [!NOTE]
> **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)**
>
> Suppose $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ at a candidate point. Let $H(x^*)$ denote the **Hessian matrix**:
>
> $$
> H(x^*) = \begin{bmatrix}
> \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[2mm]
> \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[1mm]
> \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[1mm]
> \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2}
> \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> - If $H(x^*)$ is **positive definite** (all leading principal minors $> 0$), then $x^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.
> - If $H(x^*)$ is **negative definite** (leading minors alternate in sign: $<0, >0, <0, \ldots$), then $x^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.
> - If $H(x^*)$ is **indefinite**, then $x^*$ is a **saddle point** (neither min nor max).
> - If $H(x^*)$ is **singular**, the test is inconclusive — higher-order derivatives needed.

### Worked Example — Classifying a Stationary Point

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary points of:

$$
f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^3 - 3 x_1 x_2^2 + 5 x_1^2 - 2 x_1 + 7
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the first partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 + 10 x_1 - 2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -6 x_1 x_2
$$

**Step 2:** Set the gradient to zero:

$$
3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 + 10 x_1 - 2 = 0
$$

$$
-6 x_1 x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the system. From the second equation, either $x_1 = 0$ or $x_2 = 0$.

**Case A:** $x_1 = 0$. Substituting into the first equation:

$$
0 - 3x_2^2 + 0 - 2 = 0 \implies x_2^2 = -\frac{2}{3}
$$

No real solution. Reject.

**Case B:** $x_2 = 0$. Substituting into the first equation:

$$
3x_1^2 + 10 x_1 - 2 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
x_1 = \frac{-10 \pm \sqrt{100 + 24}}{6} = \frac{-10 \pm \sqrt{124}}{6} = \frac{-10 \pm 2\sqrt{31}}{6} = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{31}}{3}
$$

Numerically:

$$
x_1^{(1)} = \frac{-5 + \sqrt{31}}{3} \approx \frac{-5 + 5.568}{3} \approx 0.189
$$

$$
x_1^{(2)} = \frac{-5 - \sqrt{31}}{3} \approx \frac{-5 - 5.568}{3} \approx -3.523
$$

So the two stationary points are $P_1 \approx (0.189, 0)$ and $P_2 \approx (-3.523, 0)$.

**Step 4:** Compute the second partial derivatives (Hessian entries):

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 6x_1 + 10
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = -6 x_1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} = -6 x_2
$$

**Step 5:** Form the Hessian matrix:

$$
H(x_1, x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 6x_1 + 10 & -6 x_2 \\ -6 x_2 & -6 x_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate Hessian at $P_1 = (0.189, 0)$:

$$
H(P_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 6(0.189) + 10 & 0 \\ 0 & -6(0.189) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 11.134 & 0 \\ 0 & -1.134 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading principal minors: $H_{11} = 11.134 > 0$, $\det(H) = (11.134)(-1.134) = -12.626 < 0$.

Since $\det(H) < 0$, the Hessian is **indefinite** at $P_1$. Therefore $P_1$ is a **saddle point**.

**Step 7:** Evaluate Hessian at $P_2 = (-3.523, 0)$:

$$
H(P_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 6(-3.523) + 10 & 0 \\ 0 & -6(-3.523) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -11.138 & 0 \\ 0 & 21.138 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading principal minors: $H_{11} = -11.138 < 0$, $\det(H) = (-11.138)(21.138) = -235.43 < 0$.

Again indefinite. Therefore $P_2$ is also a **saddle point**.

**Conclusion:** Both stationary points are saddle points — the function has no local minima or maxima in the unconstrained sense (it goes to $+\infty$ as $x_1 \to +\infty$ and to $-\infty$ as $x_1 \to -\infty$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LPP vs NLPP Characteristics

| Comparison Criterion | LPP (Linear) | NLPP (Non-Linear) | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function Class** | $f, g_i$ all affine | $f$ or $g_i$ non-linear | ⭐⭐⭐ Frequently asked |
| **Feasible Region Shape** | Convex polyhedron | Possibly non-convex set | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Solution Location** | Always at a vertex (or edge) | Interior, boundary, or vertex | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Algorithm** | Simplex (finite steps) | Iterative numerical | ⭐⭐ |
| **Convergence Guarantee** | Yes (polynomial) | Only to local optima | ⭐⭐⭐ Conceptual |
| **Optimality Conditions** | Reduced cost $\geq 0$ (max) | $\nabla f = 0$, Hessian test | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Sensitivity Analysis** | Easy (dual prices) | Difficult (numerical) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Number of Optima** | 1 face / 1 vertex (typical) | Many possible | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Modeling Power** | Limited to linear systems | Very general | ⭐ Conceptual |
| **Computational Complexity** | Polynomial $O(n^3)$ | NP-hard in general | ⭐⭐ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Optimization Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem]:::root
    A --> B{Nature of Objective and Constraints}
    B -- All Linear --> C[Linear Programming LPP]
    B -- At least one Non-Linear --> D[Non-Linear Programming NLPP]

    C --> C1[Simplex Method]
    C --> C2[Graphical Method]
    C --> C3[Big-M Method]
    C --> C4[Dual Simplex Method]

    D --> D1{Constraints Present?}
    D1 -- No --> D2[Unconstrained NLPP]
    D1 -- Yes --> D3[Constrained NLPP]

    D2 --> D2a[Stationary Points]
    D2 --> D2b[Hessian Test]

    D3 --> D3a[Equality Only]
    D3 --> D3b[Inequality]
    D3a --> D3a1[Lagrange Multipliers]
    D3b --> D3b1[KKT Conditions]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    S1 --> Q1{Is objective function linear in all variables?}
    Q1 -- No --> NL1[Mark: NLPP]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are all constraints linear?}
    Q2 -- No --> NL2[Mark: NLPP]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Any product terms like x_i times x_j?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> NL3[Mark: NLPP]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Any non-linear functions like exp, log, sqrt, absolute value?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> NL4[Mark: NLPP]
    Q4 -- No --> LP1[Confirmed: LPP]
    LP1 --> ALGO[Use Simplex Method]
    NL1 --> ALGO2[Use KKT or Gradient Method]
    NL2 --> ALGO2
    NL3 --> ALGO2
    NL4 --> ALGO2
    ALGO --> OUT1[Solve and Report Vertex Optimum]:::output
    ALGO2 --> OUT2[Solve via Calculus Conditions and Verify]:::output
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Linear Programming Problem (LPP). State the assumptions underlying an LPP.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** is a mathematical optimization problem in which:
- The **objective function** is a linear function of the decision variables.
- All **constraints** are linear inequalities or equalities.
- Decision variables are non-negative.

**Standard Form:**
Optimize $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$ subject to $\sum_j a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ and $x_j \geq 0$.

**Assumptions of LPP (Proportionality, Additivity, Certainty, Divisibility, Non-negativity):**
1. **Proportionality:** Contribution of each variable to objective and constraints is proportional to its value.
2. **Additivity:** Total contribution is the sum of individual variable contributions (no cross-product terms).
3. **Certainty:** All coefficients $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ are known constants.
4. **Divisibility:** Decision variables can take any fractional value.
5. **Non-negativity:** $x_j \geq 0$.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between a Linear Programming Problem and a Non-Linear Programming Problem. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LPP | NLPP |
|---|---|---|
| Objective Function | Linear: $c^T x$ | Non-linear: contains $x^2, e^x, x_1 x_2$ |
| Constraints | Linear inequalities | At least one non-linear constraint |
| Feasible Region | Convex polyhedron | May be non-convex |
| Solution Method | Simplex Method | KKT, Lagrange multipliers |
| Optimum | Global optimum guaranteed | May have local optima |

**Example of LPP:** Maximize $Z = 5x_1 + 3x_2$ subject to $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$, $x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

**Example of NLPP:** Minimize $Z = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \geq 4$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Classify the following functions as Linear or Non-Linear. Justify your answer in each case:
   (i) $f(x_1, x_2) = 4x_1^2 + 3x_2 - 7$
   (ii) $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = 2x_1 + 5x_2 - x_3 + 10$
   (iii) $g(x) = x_1 x_2 + x_3$

**b) (7 Marks)** For the unconstrained non-linear problem: Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$, find the optimal solution and verify using the Hessian test.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Classification:**

**(i)** $f(x_1, x_2) = 4x_1^2 + 3x_2 - 7$ is **Non-Linear** because it contains the squared term $x_1^2$, which violates the linearity condition (no variable should have power $\neq 1$).

**(ii)** $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = 2x_1 + 5x_2 - x_3 + 10$ is **Linear**. Each variable has degree exactly 1, there are no product terms, and the constant 10 does not affect linearity. The function can be written as $c^T x + d$ with $c = (2, 5, -1)^T$.

**(iii)** $g(x) = x_1 x_2 + x_3$ is **Non-Linear**. The term $x_1 x_2$ is a product of two distinct variables (a bilinear term), which violates the additivity and homogeneity conditions of linearity.

**Part (b) — Unconstrained NLPP Optimization:**

**Step 1:** Compute first partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 3)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply first-order necessary condition $\nabla f = 0$:

$$
2(x_1 - 2) = 0 \implies x_1 = 2
$$

$$
2(x_2 - 3) = 0 \implies x_2 = 3
$$

So the candidate optimum is $x^* = (2, 3)^T$.

**Step 3:** Compute second partial derivatives (Hessian entries):

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Form and analyze the Hessian matrix:

$$
H(x_1, x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading principal minors: $H_{11} = 2 > 0$ and $\det(H) = 4 > 0$. Since both are positive, $H$ is **positive definite** at $x^*$.

**Step 5:** Conclusion: $x^* = (2, 3)^T$ is a **strict local minimum** (in fact, since $f$ is convex, it is the **global minimum**).

**Step 6:** Optimal objective value:

$$
f(2, 3) = (2-2)^2 + (3-3)^2 = 0
$$

This is intuitive: the function is the squared Euclidean distance from the point $(2, 3)$, which is minimized (to zero) exactly at $(2, 3)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Explain with suitable examples the differences between Linear and Non-Linear Programming Problems. State at least four distinguishing characteristics.

**b) (7 Marks)** Identify the stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$ and classify them using the Hessian matrix method.

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Linear vs Non-Linear Programming:**

(Refer to Section 1.4 and 2.5 comparison tables for full distinctions.)

**Four Key Distinguishing Characteristics:**

1. **Mathematical Form:** LPP has linear objective and constraints; NLPP has at least one non-linear element. Example LPP: Maximize $Z = 3x + 2y$ s.t. $x + y \leq 5$, $x, y \geq 0$. Example NLPP: Minimize $Z = x^2 + y^2$ s.t. $x + y \geq 3$.

2. **Solution Methodology:** LPP uses Simplex (algebraic, finite-step). NLPP requires calculus-based methods (KKT, gradient descent, Newton's method) which are iterative and may converge only to local optima.

3. **Solution Location:** In LPP, the optimum is always at a vertex of the convex polyhedron (Fundamental Theorem of LPP). In NLPP, the optimum can be in the interior of the feasible region (where no constraint is active).

4. **Global Optimality:** LPP guarantees a global optimum (when one exists). NLPP may have multiple local optima, and finding the global optimum is NP-hard in general — special techniques like branch-and-bound, simulated annealing, or genetic algorithms may be needed.

**Part (b) — Stationary Points and Classification:**

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 - 3y
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 3y^2 - 3x
$$

**Step 2:** Set gradient to zero:

$$
3x^2 - 3y = 0 \implies y = x^2
$$

$$
3y^2 - 3x = 0 \implies x = y^2
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $y = x^2$ into $x = y^2$:

$$
x = (x^2)^2 = x^4 \implies x^4 - x = 0 \implies x(x^3 - 1) = 0
$$

Solutions: $x = 0$ or $x^3 = 1 \implies x = 1$.

**Case 1:** $x = 0 \implies y = 0^2 = 0$. Stationary point $P_1 = (0, 0)$.

**Case 2:** $x = 1 \implies y = 1^2 = 1$. Stationary point $P_2 = (1, 1)$.

**Step 4:** Compute second partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} = 6x, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} = 6y, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = -3
$$

**Step 5:** Form Hessian matrix:

$$
H(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -3 \\ -3 & 6y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate at $P_1 = (0, 0)$:

$$
H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading minors: $H_{11} = 0$ (not $> 0$), $\det(H) = 0 - 9 = -9 < 0$.

Since $\det(H) < 0$, $H$ is **indefinite**. Therefore $P_1 = (0, 0)$ is a **saddle point**.

**Step 7:** Evaluate at $P_2 = (1, 1)$:

$$
H(1, 1) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Leading minors: $H_{11} = 6 > 0$, $\det(H) = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$. Both positive, so $H$ is **positive definite**.

Therefore $P_2 = (1, 1)$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 8:** Optimal value:

$$
f(1, 1) = 1^3 + 1^3 - 3(1)(1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1
$$

**Conclusion:** The function $f(x,y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy$ has one saddle point at $(0,0)$ and one local minimum at $(1,1)$ with $f^* = -1$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mark Loss — Misclassifying a function:** Students often call $f = x_1 x_2 + x_3$ "linear" because each variable has degree 1 *individually*. **Wrong.** Linearity forbids cross-product terms. Always check using the superposition test $f(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha f(x) + \beta f(y)$.
>
> 2. **Mark Loss — Hessian Inconclusive Test Trap:** If $H_{11} = 0$, the leading-minor test is **inconclusive**, not "definite". Many students incorrectly declare the point a minimum. Use Sylvester's criterion strictly.
>
> 3. **Mark Loss — Forgetting to check KKT complementary slackness:** In constrained NLPP, a candidate point must satisfy ALL four KKT conditions (primal feasibility, dual feasibility, stationarity, complementary slackness). Missing one = 0 marks for that sub-question.
>
> 4. **Mark Loss — Confusing "stationary point" with "optimum":** A stationary point ($\nabla f = 0$) is only a **candidate**. It must be further classified using the Hessian. Jumping to "this is the minimum" without the Hessian test will lose 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Mark Loss — Sign errors in $\lambda$:** For constraints written as $g_i(x) \leq 0$, the KKT multiplier $\lambda_i \geq 0$. For $g_i(x) \geq 0$, $\lambda_i \leq 0$. Getting this sign wrong inverts the entire dual analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Linear and Non-Linear**
>
> ✅ **LPP Definition:** Objective linear + all constraints linear + non-negativity $x_j \geq 0$.
>
> ✅ **NLPP Definition:** At least one non-linearity in objective or constraint (or both).
>
> ✅ **Linearity Test:** Function $f$ is linear iff $f(\alpha x + \beta y) = \alpha f(x) + \beta f(y)$ (superposition).
>
> ✅ **Forbidden Non-Linear Terms:** $x_j^2$, $x_i x_j$, $e^{x_j}$, $\log x_j$, $\sqrt{x_j}$, $\vert x_j \vert$, $\dfrac{1}{x_j}$, $\sin x_j$, etc.
>
> ✅ **Fundamental Theorem of LPP:** Optimal solution (if exists) lies at a **vertex** of the convex feasible polyhedron.
>
> ✅ **First-Order Necessary Condition:** $\nabla f(x^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (unconstrained).
>
> ✅ **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (Hessian Test):**
>    - Positive Definite → Local Minimum.
>    - Negative Definite → Local Maximum.
>    - Indefinite → Saddle Point.
>    - Singular → Inconclusive.
>
> ✅ **Hessian Positive Definite Test (Sylvester's Criterion):** All leading principal minors $> 0$.
>
> ✅ **Hessian Negative Definite Test:** Leading principal minors alternate: $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$, equivalently check $(-H)$ is positive definite.
>
> ✅ **Lagrangian Function:** $L(x, \lambda) = f(x) - \sum_i \lambda_i g_i(x)$ (for $g_i(x) = 0$ constraints).
>
> ✅ **KKT Conditions:** Primal feasibility, Dual feasibility ($\lambda_i \geq 0$), Stationarity ($\nabla L = 0$), Complementary Slackness ($\lambda_i g_i = 0$).
>
> ✅ **Local vs Global Optimum:** LPP → global optimum at vertex (guaranteed). NLPP → only local optimum typically found; global search is hard.
>
> ✅ **Convexity:** LPP feasible region is always convex. NLPP feasible region may or may not be convex.
>
> ✅ **Algorithms to Remember:** Simplex (LPP), Graphical (2-variable LPP), KKT (general NLPP), Hessian Test (classification), Lagrange Multipliers (equality-only NLPP).
>
> ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Treating $\log x$ or $\sqrt{x}$ as linear — these are concave but still **non-linear**.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Resource allocation (LPP), Portfolio optimization (NLPP), Network flow (LPP), Structural design (NLPP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Static Optimization

**Static Optimization** (also known as **Parameter Optimization** or **Mathematical Programming**) refers to the class of optimization problems where **all design variables, parameters, and constraints are time-invariant**. The optimal decision is made at a single point in time, producing a single set of optimal values that do not evolve over a horizon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A static optimization problem is one in which the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ and constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}), h_j(\mathbf{x})$ contain **no explicit time dependence** on the independent variable. The decision-maker seeks an optimal constant vector $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is extremized.

**Canonical Form (Static):**

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to the inequality constraints:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

and equality constraints:

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

where $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ is the **decision variable vector**.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Imagine an architect deciding the dimensions of a single concrete column (width and depth) to minimize the volume of concrete used while bearing a fixed load. The loads do not change with time, and once the dimensions are chosen, they remain fixed for the column's life. This is a **static optimization** problem — one snapshot, one decision, one best answer.

---

### 1.2 Dynamic Optimization

**Dynamic Optimization** refers to problems where the **decision variables, constraints, or objective functions are functions of an independent variable (typically time $t$)**. The optimal solution is not a single point but rather an **entire trajectory** (path or policy) that must be determined over a planning horizon $[t_0, t_f]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Dynamic optimization seeks to find an optimal **function** (or control law) $u^*(t)$ and the corresponding **state trajectory** $x^*(t)$ that minimize a performance index expressed as an integral (or sum) over a time horizon, subject to a system of differential (or difference) equations that govern the system's evolution.

**Canonical Form (Dynamic — Continuous-Time Optimal Control):**

$$\min_{u(t)} J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} F\bigl(x(t), u(t), t\bigr) \, dt$$

subject to the system dynamics:

$$\dot{x}(t) = f\bigl(x(t), u(t), t\bigr)$$

with specified boundary conditions $x(t_0) = x_0$ and $x(t_f) = x_f$ (fixed or free).

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Imagine piloting a spacecraft from Earth to Mars. The thrust direction and magnitude (the control $u(t)$) must be **chosen continuously at every instant** to minimize fuel consumption, subject to Newton's laws of motion. The optimal "answer" is a continuous steering law — not just one number, but a **time-varying policy**. This is the essence of dynamic optimization.

---

### 1.3 Side-by-Side Classification

| **Feature** | **Static Optimization** | **Dynamic Optimization** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of decision** | Single point decision | Sequence of decisions over time |
| **Independent variable** | None (or fixed parameter) | Time $t$ |
| **Output** | Optimal constant vector $\mathbf{x}^*$ | Optimal trajectory $x^*(t)$ and control $u^*(t)$ |
| **Governing law** | Algebraic equations / inequality constraints | Differential (or difference) equations |
| **Solution technique** | KKT conditions, Simplex, Gradient methods | Calculus of Variations, Euler-Lagrange, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, Dynamic Programming |
| **Example** | Minimum-cost mix of concrete ingredients | Minimum-fuel rocket trajectory |
| **Complexity** | Comparatively lower | Significantly higher (curse of dimensionality) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Static Optimization Problem

A complete static optimization problem has **four canonical components**:

1. **Objective Function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — the scalar quantity to be minimized (cost, weight, energy, error) or maximized (profit, efficiency, reliability). For maximization, one simply negates: $\max f \equiv \min(-f)$.

2. **Decision Variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ — the controllable quantities whose values the designer must choose.

3. **Inequality Constraints** $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ — represent physical limits, resource caps, or design codes (e.g., stress $\le$ allowable stress).

4. **Equality Constraints** $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ — represent physical laws, balance equations, or fixed requirements (e.g., equilibrium of forces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Whenever a problem mentions "design a member / minimize weight / optimum cost" with **fixed parameters**, treat it as **static optimization**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests students on correctly identifying whether a problem is static or dynamic during the first 2-mark sub-part.

### 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Dynamic Optimization Problem

A dynamic optimization problem layers the static structure with **temporal evolution**:

1. **Performance Index (Cost Functional)** $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} F(x, u, t) \, dt$ — accumulated cost over the trajectory (instead of a single scalar).

2. **State Variables** $x(t)$ — variables describing the system's current status (position, velocity, temperature, concentration).

3. **Control Variables** $u(t)$ — the levers the designer pulls over time.

4. **State Equation (Dynamics)** $\dot{x}(t) = f(x, u, t)$ — physical law governing how the state evolves.

5. **Boundary Conditions** — initial state $x(t_0) = x_0$, and either final state fixed, free, or partially fixed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Domain** | **Formula / Condition** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Static — Unconstrained | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Stationarity (1st-order necessary condition) |
| Static — Constrained (KKT) | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ | Lagrangian stationarity |
| Static — Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i \cdot g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; \lambda_i \ge 0$ | Active-inactive constraint test |
| Dynamic — Euler-Lagrange | $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x} - \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = 0$ | Necessary condition for $J = \int F(x,\dot{x},t)\,dt$ |
| Dynamic — Natural BC | $\left.\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\right|_{t_f} = 0$ (if $x(t_f)$ is free) | Transversality condition |
| Dynamic — Hamiltonian | $H = F(x,u,t) + \lambda^T f(x,u,t)$ | Augmented scalar for optimal control |
| Dynamic — State Eq. | $\dot{x} = \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial \lambda} = f(x,u,t)$ | Costate dynamics recovered |
| Dynamic — Costate Eq. | $\dot{\lambda} = -\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial x}$ | Adjoint equation (co-state evolution) |
| Dynamic — Stationarity | $\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial u} = 0$ | Optimal control law |
| Dynamic — Transversality | $\lambda(t_f) = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg|_{t_f}$ (if Mayer term present) | Endpoint condition |

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe symbol inside table cells (such as for absolute value $\lvert x \rvert$) has been deliberately replaced with `\lvert` / `\rvert` to preserve markdown table integrity — a small but vital formatting rule for KTU digital submissions.

### 2.4 Why This Classification Matters in Engineering

- **Mechanical Engineering**: Static optimization designs a single gear tooth profile; dynamic optimization designs the full gear-shift schedule of a transmission.
- **Civil Engineering**: Static optimization sizes a truss member; dynamic optimization controls an active mass damper during an earthquake.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Static optimization tunes a filter at one frequency; dynamic optimization schedules battery charging across 24 hours.
- **Chemical Engineering**: Static optimization finds the best steady-state reactor temperature; dynamic optimization finds the best start-up trajectory.

The unifying truth: **any system whose optimal behaviour can be frozen in a single moment is static; any system whose optimal behaviour unfolds over time is dynamic.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Euler-Lagrange Equation (Cornerstone of Dynamic Optimization)

This is the most fundamental result for continuous-time dynamic optimization without explicit control constraints. Every KTU examiner expects students to be able to reproduce this derivation.

**Step 1 — Set up the functional.** We wish to find the trajectory $x^*(t)$ that minimizes:

$$J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} F\bigl(x(t), \dot{x}(t), t\bigr) \, dt$$

subject to fixed boundary conditions $x(t_0) = x_0$ and $x(t_f) = x_f$.

**Step 2 — Introduce a variation.** Let $x(t)$ deviate from the optimum by a small admissible variation $\epsilon \eta(t)$:

$$x(t, \epsilon) = x^*(t) + \epsilon\,\eta(t)$$

where $\eta(t)$ is an arbitrary differentiable function that vanishes at the boundaries:

$$\eta(t_0) = 0, \qquad \eta(t_f) = 0$$

These zero-endpoint conditions are required because the true endpoints are fixed.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the functional.** Define $J(\epsilon) = J[x(t,\epsilon)]$. Since $\epsilon = 0$ must correspond to the optimum, the first variation must vanish:

$$\delta J = \left.\frac{dJ}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon = 0} = 0$$

Differentiating under the integral sign:

$$\delta J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\,\delta x + \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\,\delta \dot{x} \right] dt$$

Since $\delta x = \epsilon\,\eta(t)$ and $\delta \dot{x} = \epsilon\,\dot{\eta}(t)$, at $\epsilon = 0$:

$$\delta J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\,\eta(t) + \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\,\dot{\eta}(t) \right] dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Integration by parts on the second term.**

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_f} \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\,\dot{\eta}(t)\, dt = \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\,\eta(t) \right]_{t_0}^{t_f} - \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) \eta(t)\, dt$$

Because $\eta(t_0) = \eta(t_f) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes identically:

$$\left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\,\eta(t) \right]_{t_0}^{t_f} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Combine and apply the fundamental lemma.** We obtain:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) \right] \eta(t)\, dt = 0$$

Since $\eta(t)$ is an **arbitrary** function (only constrained at the endpoints), the integrand must itself be zero at every interior point:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = 0\;}$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation** — the single most important necessary condition in dynamic optimization. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Euler-Lagrange on a Classical Brachistochrone-Type Functional

Consider the dynamic problem:

$$J = \int_{0}^{1} \left[ \dot{x}^2(t) + x^2(t) \right] dt$$

with $x(0) = 1$ and $x(1) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Identify the integrand.**

$$F(x, \dot{x}, t) = \dot{x}^2 + x^2$$

Note: $F$ has **no explicit time dependence**.

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} = 2x, \qquad \frac{\partial F}{\partial \dot{x}} = 2\dot{x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Euler-Lagrange equation.**

$$2x - \frac{d}{dt}(2\dot{x}) = 0$$

$$2x - 2\ddot{x} = 0$$

$$\ddot{x} - x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE.** Characteristic equation $r^2 - 1 = 0$ gives $r = \pm 1$. General solution:

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{t} + C_2 e^{-t}$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**

$$x(0) = 1 \implies C_1 + C_2 = 1$$

$$x(1) = 0 \implies C_1 e + C_2 e^{-1} = 0$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$C_1 = \frac{1}{e^2 - 1}\cdot(-1) = -\frac{e^{-1}}{e - e^{-1}} = -\frac{1}{e^2 - 1}$$

$$C_2 = 1 - C_1 = \frac{e^2}{e^2 - 1}$$

Final trajectory:

$$x^*(t) = \frac{e^2 - e^{t}}{e^2 - 1} \quad \text{(after simplification)}$$

This closed-form answer is a **proof of concept** — dynamic optimization yields entire optimal paths, not single numbers.

---

### 3.3 KTU Exam-Style Mini-Derivation: Static KKT Stationarity

For the constrained static problem:

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g(x_1, x_2) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2, \lambda) = f(x_1, x_2) + \lambda\, g(x_1, x_2)$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero (KKT stationarity).**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial x_1} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial x_2} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = g(x_1, x_2) = 0$$

This gives **three equations** in **three unknowns** $(x_1, x_2, \lambda)$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    nodeA["Optimization Problems"] --> nodeB["Static Time Invariant"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Dynamic Time Dependent"]

    nodeF --> nodeF2["Pareto Front"]

    nodeC --> nodeG["Calculus of Variations"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Optimal Control"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Dynamic Programming"]

    nodeG --> nodeG1["Euler Lagrange Equation"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["Transversality Conditions"]

```

```mermaid

    nodeP["Define Performance Index J"] --> nodeQ["Specify State Dynamics"]

```

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph staticBlock["STATIC OPTIMIZATION Single Decision Point"]
        sA["Decision Time t0"] --> sB["Choose Vector x star"]
        sB --> sC["Fixed Forever"]
    end

    subgraph dynamicBlock["DYNAMIC OPTIMIZATION Continuous Decision Stream"]
        dA["Time t0"] --> dB["Choose u at t0"]
        dB --> dC["Time t1"]
        dC --> dD["Choose u at t1"]

        dE --> dF["Choose u at t2"]
```
