## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Direct Search Methods (KTU 2024 ESE Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Direct search uses only function evaluations, **no derivatives**.
- **Five main families:** Pattern (Hooke–Jeeves), Conjugate (Powell), Simplex (Nelder–Mead), Rotation (Rosenbrock), Random.
- **Hooke–Jeeves key equation:** Pattern move is $\mathbf{x}_P^{\text{new}} = 2\mathbf{x}_B^{\text{new}} - \mathbf{x}_B^{\text{old}}$.
- **Step reduction:** When E-move fails, multiply all $\Delta_j$ by $\alpha$ (typically $0.5$).
- **Stopping criterion (H&J):** $\max_j \vert \Delta_j \vert < \varepsilon$ or $\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert < \varepsilon$.
- **Powell's method:** Converges in **at most $n$ iterations** for a positive-definite quadratic in $n$ variables.
- **Reflection test (Powell):** $f_R = f(2\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{x}_0)$; if it fails, **keep old directions**.
- **Nelder–Mead coefficients:** $\rho = 1$, $\gamma = 2$, $\beta = 0.5$, $\delta = 0.5$.
- **Centroid in Nelder–Mead:** Excludes the worst vertex $\mathbf{x}_h$.
- **Convergence rates:** Univariate (linear) < Hooke–Jeeves (linear) < Powell (superlinear, finite on quadratics).
- **Best use-case mapping:** Quadratic-like problems → Powell; noisy/black-box → Nelder–Mead; non-quadratic with smooth landscape → Hooke–Jeeves.
- **Real-world applications:** Aerodynamic shape design (mesh-adaptive direct search), hyperparameter tuning in ML (random search, simulated annealing), experimental optimization, robotics trajectory planning.
- **Common KTU pitfalls to avoid:** Wrong sign in pattern move, missing reflection test, including the worst vertex in the centroid, forgetting the stopping criterion, mixing up Hooke–Jeeves step sizes across iterations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient-based Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gradient-based Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient-Based Methods (Definition):**
> *Gradient-based methods* are a class of iterative numerical optimization algorithms used to locate the **local minimum** (or maximum) of a differentiable objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. These methods construct a sequence of iterates $\\{\mathbf{x}_k\\}$ using the gradient information $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (first-order methods) or additionally the Hessian information $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (second-order methods) to determine the search direction.

Mathematically, the iterative template is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_k$ is the current iterate (column vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$)
- $\mathbf{d}_k$ is the **search direction** chosen using gradient (and possibly Hessian) data
- $\alpha_k > 0$ is the **step length** (learning rate) — a scalar that determines how far to move along $\mathbf{d}_k$

The convergence criterion for a KTU-satisfactory algorithm is:
$$\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\| \leq \epsilon, \qquad \epsilon \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-6}]$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mountain Hiker Analogy (Intuitive Explanation):**
>
> Imagine you are a hiker trapped in thick fog on a mountain and wish to descend to the lowest valley. You cannot see the bottom, but you can feel the **slope of the ground beneath your feet**.
>
> - **Gradient** = slope direction (steepest ascent). The *negative* gradient points downhill.
> - **Step length** $\alpha_k$ = how big a stride you take.
> - **Gradient Descent (Steepest Descent)** = at every point, you turn and walk in the steepest downhill direction.
> - **Newton's Method** = before walking, you *feel the curvature* of the slope (Hessian). You then predict where the bottom is and jump toward it. This makes Newton faster but more expensive.
> - **Conjugate Gradient** = you walk in a smart direction that does not undo previous progress (like zig-zagging smartly without backtracking).
>
> The hiker keeps walking and re-checking the slope until the ground feels flat ($\nabla f \approx \mathbf{0}$).

---

## 1.3 Why Gradient-Based Methods Matter in KTU Engineering Curriculum

These methods form the **theoretical backbone** of:
- **Machine Learning & Deep Learning** training (SGD, Adam, RMSProp all descend on loss gradients).
- **Optimal Structural Design** (minimum weight truss, topology optimization).
- **Process Control & Parameter Estimation** (least-squares minimization).
- **Signal Processing** (adaptive filters, Wiener filter derivation).
- **Power Systems** (economic dispatch via gradient projections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Anchor (KTU standard vocabulary):**
> - **Objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ — the function being minimized.
> - **Stationary point** — point where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
> - **Local minimum** — $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ in a neighbourhood.
> - **Global minimum** — $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ over the entire feasible domain.
> - **Strongly convex** — Hessian satisfies $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succeq m\mathbf{I}$ for some $m > 0$.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Method Type | Order | Information Used | Convergence Speed | Cost per Iteration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gradient Descent (Steepest Descent) | First-order | $\nabla f$ only | Linear (slow near optimum) | **Cheap** — $O(n)$ |
| Newton's Method | Second-order | $\nabla f$ and $\nabla^2 f$ | Quadratic (very fast) | **Expensive** — $O(n^3)$ for linear system |
| Conjugate Gradient | First-order | $\nabla f$ and previous directions | Superlinear (for quadratic) | Moderate — $O(n)$ per step |
| Modified Newton | Second-order | $\nabla f$ and modified $\nabla^2 f$ | Superlinear / Quadratic | Moderate — Hessian modified for SPD |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Unified Iteration Template

Every gradient-based method follows a three-step *decision pipeline*:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Step Algorithmic Framework:**
> 1. **Direction Selection:** Compute $\mathbf{d}_k$ using gradient/Hessian data at $\mathbf{x}_k$.
> 2. **Line Search:** Determine step length $\alpha_k$ satisfying sufficient decrease (e.g., Armijo, exact line search, Wolfe conditions).
> 3. **Update & Check:** Move to $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$. Verify convergence $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})\| \leq \epsilon$; otherwise iterate.

---

## 2.2 Method 1 — Cauchy Steepest Descent (First Order)

**Direction Selection:**
$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

This is simply the **negative gradient** — the direction of steepest *local* descent.

**Step Length (Exact Line Search for quadratic case):**

For $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{Q}$ symmetric positive-definite:
$$\alpha_k = \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}$$

**Convergence Rate:** Linear, with factor:
$$\rho = \left(\frac{\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) - 1}{\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) + 1}\right)^2$$

where $\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) = \frac{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{Q})}{\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{Q})}$ is the **condition number**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Slow convergence when $\kappa \gg 1$** (ill-conditioned problems). This is the *raison d'être* for Newton's method and conjugate gradient.

**Descent Lemma:**
$$f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \frac{m}{2}\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2$$

for strongly convex $f$ with $\nabla^2 f \succeq m\mathbf{I}$.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Newton's Method (Second Order)

**Direction Selection:**
$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\left[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\right]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

The Newton step satisfies the **Newton equation**:
$$\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\,\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

**Convergence Rate:** **Quadratic** near a minimum with non-singular Hessian:
$$\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^*\| \leq C\|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*\|^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** If $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is **not positive-definite** (e.g., at a saddle point), $-\nabla^2 f^{-1}\nabla f$ may *not* be a descent direction. This motivates the *modified* Newton method.

**Newton's method as a fixed-point of the residual map:**
Define $N(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} - \left[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})\right]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$. Then $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = N(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and any local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a fixed point: $N(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{x}^*$.

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Conjugate Gradient (Fletcher–Reeves)

Designed for **quadratic** objectives but extended to general non-linear functions (Fletcher–Reeves, Polak–Ribière).

**Direction Recurrence:**
$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_{k-1}$$

**Fletcher–Reeves coefficient:**
$$\beta_k = \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})} = \frac{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})\|^2}$$

**Polak–Ribière coefficient (often preferred in non-quadratic case):**
$$\beta_k^{PR} = \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \left[\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})\right]}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})\|^2}$$

**Convergence Property (exact arithmetic, strictly convex quadratic):** Converges in *at most* $n$ iterations, where $n$ is the problem dimension.

---

## 2.5 Method 4 — Modified Newton's Method (Hessian Safeguarding)

When $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is **indefinite** or singular, we apply modifications to ensure positive-definiteness:

$$\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mu_k \mathbf{I}$$

where $\mu_k$ is chosen so that:
$$\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} \succeq \delta \mathbf{I}, \qquad \delta > 0$$

Common strategies:
- **Eigenvalue shift:** $\mu_k = \max\left(0,\, -\lambda_{\min}(\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)) + \delta\right)$
- **Gill–Murray safeguarded Newton**
- **Levenberg–Marquardt** (special case for least-squares)

---

## 2.6 Step-Length Selection Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Wolfe Conditions (standard for line search in KTU-scope problems):**
>
> *Sufficient decrease (Armijo condition):*
> $$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k, \qquad 0 < c_1 < 1$$
>
> *Curvature condition:*
> $$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \geq c_2 \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k, \qquad c_1 < c_2 < 1$$
>
> Typical values: $c_1 = 10^{-4}$, $c_2 = 0.9$.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas a KTU student must memorize for the ESE — pinned for last-minute revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iteration Template | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | $\alpha_k > 0$ |
| 2 | Steepest Descent Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Always a descent direction for $\nabla f \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| 3 | Exact $\alpha_k$ (Quadratic $f$) | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T \mathbf{Q}\, \nabla f_k}$ | Valid when $f = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x}$ |
| 4 | Steepest Descent Rate | $\rho = \left(\dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}\right)^2$ | $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ |
| 5 | Newton Direction | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Solve linear system — $O(n^3)$ |
| 6 | Newton Convergence | $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^*\| \leq C\|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*\|^2$ | **Quadratic** rate |
| 7 | CG Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f_k + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_{k-1}$ | $\mathbf{d}_0 = -\nabla f_0$ |
| 8 | Fletcher–Reeves $\beta_k$ | $\beta_k^{FR} = \dfrac{\|\nabla f_k\|^2}{\|\nabla f_{k-1}\|^2}$ | $\beta_0 = 0$ |
| 9 | Polak–Ribière $\beta_k$ | $\beta_k^{PR} = \dfrac{\nabla f_k^T(\nabla f_k - \nabla f_{k-1})}{\|\nabla f_{k-1}\|^2}$ | Restart on $\beta_k < 0$ |
| 10 | Armijo Condition | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$ | $c_1 \in (0,1)$ |
| 11 | Wolfe Curvature Condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \geq c_2 \nabla f_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$ | $c_2 \in (c_1, 1)$ |
| 12 | Modified Newton Hessian | $\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mu_k \mathbf{I}$ | Ensure $\succeq \delta \mathbf{I}$ |
| 13 | Optimality Condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (1st-order KKT for unconstrained) | Necessary |
| 14 | Second-Order Sufficient Condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ \mathbf{0}$ | Sufficient for local minimum |
| 15 | Termination Criterion | $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - Power-systems dispatch engineers use the **steepest descent rate** $\rho$ to estimate iterations needed in Economic Dispatch.
> - Structural optimization routines (e.g., NASTRAN) implement **modified Newton** for non-convex topology problems.
> - **Conjugate gradient** is the workhorse in deep-learning training of linear layers and large-scale PDE solvers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steepest Descent Direction

**Goal:** Show that $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ gives the direction of steepest local decrease.

Consider a Taylor expansion of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}_k$ along a unit direction $\mathbf{d}$:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}$$

The *directional derivative* in direction $\mathbf{d}$:
$$D_{\mathbf{d}} f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \lim_{\alpha \to 0^+} \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\alpha} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}$$

We want to **minimize** this linearized decrease rate, i.e., find $\mathbf{d}^*$ such that $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}^*$ is maximally negative.

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{d}^* &= \arg\min_{\|\mathbf{d}\| = 1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d} \\
&= -\frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **steepest descent direction** is $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (the unit vector is absorbed into the step length $\alpha_k$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact Step Length for Quadratic Objective

**Setup:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x} + c$, with $\mathbf{Q} \succ \mathbf{0}$ symmetric.

Gradient: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$.

We want to choose $\alpha_k$ minimizing $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ with $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = -(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b})$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(\alpha) &= \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) - \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + c \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x}_k + \tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k + c \\
&= f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k^T (\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}) + \tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k \\
&= f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \alpha \|\mathbf{d}_k\|^2 + \tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\phi'(\alpha) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi'(\alpha) &= -\|\mathbf{d}_k\|^2 + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k = 0 \\
\alpha_k &= \frac{\|\mathbf{d}_k\|^2}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k} = \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}
\end{aligned}$$

The second derivative $\phi''(\alpha) = \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k > 0$ (since $\mathbf{Q} \succ 0$) confirms a **minimum**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Convergence Rate $\rho$ for Steepest Descent

For the quadratic case, after exact line search the iterates satisfy:
$$\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^*\|_{\mathbf{Q}}^2 \leq \rho \, \|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*\|_{\mathbf{Q}}^2$$

where the $\|\mathbf{v}\|_{\mathbf{Q}}^2 = \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{v}$ energy norm and:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \left(\frac{\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) - 1}{\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) + 1}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Proof Sketch:**
Define the error $\mathbf{e}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*$. After exact line search:

$$\mathbf{e}_{k+1} = \left[\mathbf{I} - \frac{\mathbf{Q}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T}{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{Q} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}\right] \mathbf{e}_k$$

In the eigenbasis of $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^T$, the operator norm of the projection matrix along the eigendirections $\lambda_i$ is $\frac{\lambda_{\max} - \lambda_{\min}}{(\lambda_i + \sqrt{\lambda_i \lambda_{\max}})}$, yielding:

$$\rho = \left(\frac{\lambda_{\max} - \lambda_{\min}}{\lambda_{\max} + \lambda_{\min}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}\right)^2$$

**Implication:** When $\kappa \approx 1$ (well-conditioned), $\rho \to 0$ → fast convergence. When $\kappa \to \infty$, $\rho \to 1$ → pathologically slow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Newton's Method from Taylor Expansion

Taylor expansion of $\nabla f$ at $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}$ around $\mathbf{x}_k$ (note: expand the *gradient* not the function):

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) &\approx \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k) \\
\mathbf{0} &= \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k) \\
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k &= -\left[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\right]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \\
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{x}_k - \left[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)\right]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Newton update**. The set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = N(\mathbf{x}_k)$ where:

$$N(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} - \left[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})\right]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$$

**Quadratic Convergence Theorem:**

If $f$ is $C^3$, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, and $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is non-singular, then $\mathbf{x}_k \to \mathbf{x}^*$ and there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for $\|\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^*\| < \delta$:

$$\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^*\| \leq C\|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*\|^2$$

for some constant $C$ depending on third derivatives of $f$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Conjugate Directions Condition

For a quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x}$, two directions $\mathbf{d}_i, \mathbf{d}_j$ are called **$\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate** if:
$$\mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{Q}\, \mathbf{d}_j = 0, \qquad i \neq j$$

A set of $n$ mutually conjugate directions spans $\mathbb{R}^n$. With exact line search along conjugate directions, the minimizer is reached in **at most $n$ steps**.

**Proof of finite termination:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} &= \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k \\
\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* &= \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^* + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha_i \mathbf{d}_i
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying by $\mathbf{Q}$ and exploiting the optimality condition $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha_i \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_i = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha_i \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_i$$

Taking inner product with $\mathbf{d}_k$:
$$\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*) = -\mathbf{d}_k^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha_i \underbrace{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_i}_{=0}$$

For $k = n$, the span $\{\mathbf{d}_0, \ldots, \mathbf{d}_{n-1}\} = \mathbb{R}^n$, so $\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{0}$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Quadratic, 2D)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$ using steepest descent, starting at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

**Solution:**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 4 \\ 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{Q} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$\mathbf{Q}$ has eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 3 + \sqrt{5} \approx 5.236$, $\lambda_2 = 3 - \sqrt{5} \approx 0.764$, so $\kappa \approx 6.854$.

**Iteration 0:**
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = (-4, -2)^T$, so $\mathbf{d}_0 = (4, 2)^T$.

$$\alpha_0 = \frac{(-4)^2 + (-2)^2}{[4, 2]\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}} = \frac{20}{[4,2]\begin{pmatrix}20\\12\end{pmatrix}} = \frac{20}{104} = \frac{5}{26}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \tfrac{5}{26}(4,2)^T = \left(\tfrac{20}{26}, \tfrac{10}{26}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{10}{13}, \tfrac{5}{13}\right)^T$$

**Iteration 1:**
$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \left(\tfrac{40}{13} + \tfrac{10}{13} - 4,\; \tfrac{20}{13} + \tfrac{10}{13} - 2\right)^T = \left(-\tfrac{2}{13}, -\tfrac{4}{13}\right)^T$.

$\mathbf{d}_1 = \left(\tfrac{2}{13}, \tfrac{4}{13}\right)^T$. The student should continue this to verify zig-zag pattern.

**Exact minimum:** $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{Q}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (1, 0)^T$, $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Unified Gradient-Based Method Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize x0 and epsilon] --> B[Compute gradient at xk]
    B --> C{Direction Logic}
    C -->|First Order| D1[d_k equals negative grad fx_k]
    C -->|Second Order| D2[d_k from Newton Eq: H d = negative grad]
    C -->|Conjugate Recurrence| D3[d_k = negative grad fx_k + beta d_{k-1}]
    D1 --> E[Line Search: determine alpha_k]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Update x_{k+1} = x_k + alpha_k d_k]
    F --> G{Convergence Test}
    G -->|Norm of grad fx_{k+1} less than epsilon| H[Stop Return x_{k+1}]
    G -->|Not Converged| I[Increment k and Go Back to B]
    H --> J[Output Optimal x_star and fx_star]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Steepest Descent Geometric Behaviour (Zig-Zag)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ell[Contour Levels of ill-conditioned Quadratic]
        direction TB
        A1[Start x0] -- d0 --> A2[x1]
        A2 -- d1 perp d0 --> A3[x2]
        A3 -- d2 perp d1 --> A4[x3]
        A4 -- Many zig-zag steps --> A5[x_star minimum]
    end
    style A1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style A5 fill:#e6ffe6
    style Ell fill:#fff9e6
```

> **Reading aid:** Each iterate in steepest descent is **orthogonal in gradient direction** ($\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \perp \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$), producing the classic zig-zag trajectory toward $\mathbf{x}^*$. This is why convergence is slow when $\kappa \gg 1$.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Newton's Method vs Steepest Descent Direction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NM[Newton Step Geometry]
        direction TB
        N1[Current point xk]
        N2[Quadratic Taylor Model]
        N3[Solve Hessian d = neg gradient]
        N4[Compute Minimum of model]
        N5[Move directly to N4]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5
    end
    subgraph SD[Steepest Descent Geometry]
        direction TB
        S1[Current point xk]
        S2[Local linear approximation]
        S3[Direction neg gradient]
        S4[Line search along d]
        S5[Move to S4 best point]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    end
    style NM fill:#e0f7fa
    style SD fill:#fff3e0
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Given] --> Q1{Is Hessian easily available?}
    Q1 -->|No, n very large| R1[Use Steepest Descent or CG]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is f convex or Hessian SPD?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Use Standard Newton Method]
    Q2 -->|No, saddle risk| Q3[Use Modified Newton with Hessian shift]
    Q1 -->|Quadratic structure| R3[Use Conjugate Gradient Fletcher Reeves]
    R1 --> S1[Cost O of n per iteration]
    R2 --> S2[Cost O of n cubed but quadratic convergence]
    R3 --> S3[At most n iterations exact arithmetic]
    Q3 --> S4[Cost O of n cubed plus eigen or Cholesky safeguard]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for KTU:** Use the decision diagram above in the viva when asked "which method would you choose and why?"

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define *steepest descent method*. Write its iteration formula and explain why it is called "steepest."

**Model Answer:**
Steepest descent is an iterative first-order optimization method that at each step moves in the direction of the *negative gradient*, which is the direction of maximum local decrease of the objective function. Its iteration formula is:
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
The direction is called "steepest" because among all unit directions $\mathbf{d}$ with $\|\mathbf{d}\| = 1$, the negative gradient direction $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ minimizes the directional derivative $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}$, i.e., produces the steepest descent of the linearized function.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** State the *Fletcher–Reeves* conjugate gradient formula. Why is the *restart* procedure often used in non-quadratic extensions?

**Model Answer:**
The Fletcher–Reeves conjugate direction recurrence is:
$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_{k-1}, \qquad \beta_k = \frac{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2}{\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1})\|^2}$$
with $\beta_0 = 0$. Restart is used because for non-quadratic functions the conjugacy property degrades over iterations; periodically setting $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (i.e., $\beta_k = 0$) resets the method to pure steepest descent and prevents the accumulation of search directions that are no longer conjugate with respect to the local Hessian.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the optimal step length $\alpha_k$ for the steepest descent method when applied to the quadratic function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$ with $\mathbf{Q}$ symmetric positive-definite. Show that the convergence rate factor is $\rho = \left(\frac{\kappa-1}{\kappa+1}\right)^2$ where $\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$, perform **two iterations** of steepest descent with exact line search on $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$ and compute the true minimum.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Newton update rule $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - [\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)]^{-1}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ from the Taylor expansion of $\nabla f$. Discuss two failure modes of the standard Newton method and how *modified Newton* addresses them.

**(b)** [7 Marks] State and prove the *finite-termination* property of the conjugate gradient method on a strictly convex quadratic objective in at most $n$ iterations. Use the definition of $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate directions.

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions (Exhaustive)

#### **Question A (a) — Model Solution**

Refer to *Derivation 2* in Section 3.2 for the step-length derivation and *Derivation 3* in Section 3.3 for the convergence rate.

**Step-length key result:**
$$\alpha_k = \frac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}\, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}$$

**Convergence rate key result:**
$$\rho = \left(\frac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}\right)^2, \qquad \kappa = \frac{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{Q})}{\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{Q})}$$

**Discussion:** As $\kappa \to 1$, $\rho \to 0$ (fast); as $\kappa \to \infty$, $\rho \to 1$ (pathologically slow zig-zag).

---

#### **Question A (b) — Model Solution**

$f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$.

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} 4x_1 - 4 \\ 2x_2 - 2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{Q} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**True minimum:** $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{Q}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (1, 1)^T$, $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 2 - 1 - 4 - 2 = -5$.

**Iteration 0:** $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$, $\nabla f_0 = (-4, -2)^T$, $\mathbf{d}_0 = (4, 2)^T$.

$$\alpha_0 = \frac{16 + 4}{(4,2)\begin{pmatrix}4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}4 \\ 2\end{pmatrix}} = \frac{20}{(4,2)\begin{pmatrix}16 \\ 4\end{pmatrix}} = \frac{20}{64 + 8} = \frac{20}{72} = \frac{5}{18}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = (0,0)^T + \tfrac{5}{18}(4, 2)^T = \left(\tfrac{20}{18}, \tfrac{10}{18}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{10}{9}, \tfrac{5}{9}\right)^T$$

**Iteration 1:** $\nabla f_1 = \left(\tfrac{40}{9} - 4,\; \tfrac{10}{9} - 2\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{4}{9},\; -\tfrac{8}{9}\right)^T$, $\mathbf{d}_1 = \left(-\tfrac{4}{9}, \tfrac{8}{9}\right)^T$.

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{(4/9)^2 + (-8/9)^2}{\mathbf{d}_1^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_1} = \frac{(16 + 64)/81}{(-4/9)(4)(−4/9) + (8/9)(2)(8/9)} = \frac{80/81}{(64 + 128)/81} = \frac{80}{192} = \frac{5}{12}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \left(\tfrac{10}{9}, \tfrac{5}{9}\right)^T + \tfrac{5}{12}\left(-\tfrac{4}{9}, \tfrac{8}{9}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{10}{9} - \tfrac{5}{27},\; \tfrac{5}{9} + \tfrac{10}{27}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{25}{27},\; \tfrac{25}{27}\right)^T$$

Note $\mathbf{x}_2 = (25/27, 25/27)^T$ is converging to $(1, 1)^T$. Students should display $f(\mathbf{x}_1)$ and $f(\mathbf{x}_2)$ and comment on the zig-zag direction (each gradient is perpendicular to the previous).

---

#### **Question B (a) — Model Solution**

Refer to *Derivation 4* in Section 3.4.

**Two failure modes of standard Newton:**
1. **Indefinite Hessian at saddle point** → Newton direction is *not* a descent direction (move uphill).
2. **Singular Hessian** → direction undefined, linear system has no unique solution.

**Modified Newton fix:** Replace Hessian by $\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mu_k \mathbf{I}$ with $\mu_k \geq -\lambda_{\min}(\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)) + \delta$, ensuring $\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} \succ \delta \mathbf{I}$ and hence a well-defined descent direction.

---

#### **Question B (b) — Model Solution**

**Statement:** *For strictly convex quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$ with exact line search along $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate directions, the conjugate gradient method terminates in at most $n$ iterations.*

**Proof outline (from Section 3.5):**
1. Construct directions $\mathbf{d}_0, \mathbf{d}_1, \ldots$ that are mutually $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate. By Gram–Schmidt-type construction using residuals, this holds.
2. After $k$ steps, $\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{x}_0 + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \alpha_i \mathbf{d}_i$.
3. Show by induction that $\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ and $\mathbf{d}_k^T \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = -\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k$.
4. Therefore $\alpha_k = \mathbf{d}_k^T (\mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{x}_k)/\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$ yields the exact 1-D minimizer along $\mathbf{d}_k$.
5. Since $n$ conjugate directions span $\mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{x}^* \in \text{span}\{\mathbf{d}_0, \ldots, \mathbf{d}_{n-1}\}$ implies $\mathbf{x}_n = \mathbf{x}^*$ in exact arithmetic.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners regularly deduct marks — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Mistaking direction formula:** Many students write $\mathbf{d}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ instead of $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ in steepest descent. This is the *ascent* direction. **−1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the convergence rates:** Linear vs quadratic — must explicitly state that steepest descent is *linear* (geometric) with factor $\rho$, while Newton's is *quadratic* ($\|\mathbf{e}_{k+1}\| \leq C\|\mathbf{e}_k\|^2$).
>
> 3. **Skipping the line-search derivation:** Examiners expect the $\alpha_k$ derivation for the quadratic case. Writing only the final formula without derivation loses up to **3 of 7 marks**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the descent lemma conditions:** When stating "the gradient direction is always a descent direction," you must note the condition $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \neq \mathbf{0}$. Otherwise, partial credit only.
>
> 5. **Modified Newton detail:** A bare statement "add $\mu \mathbf{I}$" is insufficient. You must specify **how** $\mu_k$ is chosen (e.g., shift to make smallest eigenvalue $\geq \delta > 0$).
>
> 6. **CG restart:** For non-quadratic problems, the answer must mention **Powell restart** condition ($\beta_k < 0$ in Polak–Ribière) or periodic restart every $n$ steps.
>
> 7. **Armijo / Wolfe signs:** The Armijo condition has the form $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$. Sign errors here are common; re-derive carefully.
>
> 8. **No convergence proof for CG:** Simply stating "CG converges in $n$ steps" without the conjugacy proof is incomplete for full marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist (pin this for the night before the exam):**
>
> - **Iteration template:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$. Memorize.
> - **Steepest descent:** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$; linear convergence; rate $\rho = \left(\frac{\kappa-1}{\kappa+1}\right)^2$.
> - **Newton:** $\mathbf{d}_k = -[\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)]^{-1}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$; quadratic convergence; cost $O(n^3)$.
> - **Conjugate gradient:** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f_k + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_{k-1}$, with Fletcher–Reeves $\beta_k = \|\nabla f_k\|^2 / \|\nabla f_{k-1}\|^2$. At most $n$ iterations on a strictly convex quadratic.
> - **Modified Newton:** $\nabla^2 f_{\text{mod}} = \nabla^2 f + \mu \mathbf{I}$, ensures descent & PD.
> - **Polak–Ribière vs Fletcher–Reeves:** Polak–Ribière has built-in restart ($\beta < 0$), often better for non-quadratic.
> - **Line search:** Exact (quadratic) vs Armijo–Wolfe (general). Memorize both inequalities.
> - **Optimality KKT 1st-order (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
> - **Sufficient 2nd-order:** $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ \mathbf{0}$.
> - **Condition number $\kappa$:** governs steepest descent speed. Pre-conditioners improve $\kappa$.
> - **Termination:** $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ with $\epsilon$ typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$.
> - **Per-iteration costs:** Steepest descent $O(n)$, Newton $O(n^3)$, CG $O(n)$ per step but up to $n$ steps total $\Rightarrow O(n^2)$ for quadratic (still better than Newton when $n \gg 1$).
> - **Real-world:** ML training uses mini-batch SGD (steepest descent variant). PDE solvers favour CG because Hessian is sparse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Newton's Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Method (Newton-Raphson Method)** is a powerful open-form, iterative numerical procedure used in optimization and non-linear equation solving. It successively refines an initial estimate of a root (or stationary point) by leveraging the first-order Taylor series approximation of the function at the current iterate.

Mathematically, for finding a root of a single-variable non-linear equation $f(x) = 0$, Newton's Method generates a sequence of approximations $\{x_k\}$ via the recursive update rule:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

For **unconstrained optimization** of a smooth, unidimensional objective function $f(x)$, Newton's Method seeks the stationary point by setting the gradient to zero, i.e., $f'(x) = 0$. Applying the same Taylor series logic to $f'(x)$ yields:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The method is named after **Sir Isaac Newton (1669)** and independently described by **Joseph Raphson (1690)**. It is the cornerstone iterative technique in KTU Module 2 — Non-Linear Programming and Numerical Methods.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a rolling hill at night with a flashlight, and you want to find the **exact bottom of the valley** (the minimum of the function). You cannot see the entire landscape, only the small patch of ground directly under your feet.

**What do you do?**

1. You feel the **slope** of the ground beneath you (this is the first derivative $f'(x)$).
2. You estimate the **curvature** — how sharply the ground bends (this is the second derivative $f''(x)$).
3. You take a calculated step in the direction that the slope and curvature suggest will take you to the bottom fastest.

That single calculated step is exactly what Newton's Method does — it uses both the **slope** and the **curvature** of the function to make an *intelligent leap* towards the optimum, rather than blindly wandering.

### 1.3 Geometric Interpretation

Geometrically, at each iterate $x_k$, Newton's Method:
- Constructs the **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $(x_k, f(x_k))$.
- Finds where this tangent line crosses the **x-axis** (or the crossing point of tangent to $f'(x)$ for optimization).
- Declares this x-intercept as the next approximation $x_{k+1}$.

The iterative "tangent chasing" rapidly converges to the true root, with the error roughly squaring at each step (quadratic convergence).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Critical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | KTU Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Convergence Order | $p$ | **2 (Quadratic)** |
| Typical Tolerance | $\varepsilon$ | **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-8}$** |
| Derivative Required | $f'(x)$ | **First derivative mandatory** |
| Curvature Required | $f''(x)$ | **Required for optimization form** |
| Function Smoothness | $C^2$ | **Twice continuously differentiable** |
| Common Failure Mode | — | **Division by zero / divergent oscillations** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight for KTU Examinations:** The *quadratic convergence* property is the single most tested theoretical feature of Newton's Method. The error at step $k+1$ is proportional to the *square* of the error at step $k$ — meaning the number of *correct digits roughly doubles per iteration* once the iterates are close to the true solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

Newton's Method emerges from a beautiful application of the **first-order Taylor series expansion**. Let us break down the *why* and *how* systematically.

### Step 1: Start with the Goal
We wish to solve $f(x) = 0$ for an unknown scalar $x$. Let $x^*$ denote the true root, so $f(x^*) = 0$.

### Step 2: Linearize the Function
Assume we have a current approximation $x_k$ that is *close* to $x^*$. Approximate $f(x)$ near $x_k$ using a truncated Taylor series:

$$f(x) \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k)$$

This is a straight line — the tangent to the curve at $x_k$. Higher-order terms ($f''(x_k), f'''(x_k), \ldots$) are discarded.

### Step 3: Set the Approximation to Zero
We wish to find the $x$ where the linearized function equals zero:

$$0 = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k)$$

### Step 4: Solve for the New Approximation $x_{k+1}$
Rearranging gives the celebrated Newton's update formula:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$$

### Step 5: The "Why" Behind the Magic
- The slope $f'(x_k)$ measures **steepness**. A steep slope means a small step, a gentle slope allows a large step.
- The ratio $\dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$ essentially inverts the slope to "project" the function value back onto the x-axis.
- When $f'(x_k) \to 0$ (flat region), the step size explodes — this is precisely the **division-by-zero failure mode**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$ | **Newton-Raphson root formula** | Solving $f(x) = 0$ |
| 2 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | **Newton's optimization formula** | Finding $\min$ or $\max$ of $f(x)$ |
| 3 | $e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} \cdot e_k^{\,2}$ | **Error recurrence (root form)** | Proves quadratic convergence |
| 4 | $e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f'''(x^*)}{2 f''(x^*)} \cdot e_k^{\,2}$ | **Error recurrence (opt form)** | Proves quadratic convergence |
| 5 | $\left\vert e_{k+1} \right\vert \leq C \cdot \left\vert e_k \right\vert^{2}$ | **Quadratic convergence condition** | Convergence rate assertion |
| 6 | $\left\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \right\vert < \varepsilon$ | **Stopping criterion (consecutive)** | Practical termination test |
| 7 | $\left\vert f(x_k) \right\vert < \varepsilon$ | **Stopping criterion (residual)** | Alternative termination test |
| 8 | $\left\vert \dfrac{x_{k+1} - x_k}{x_{k+1}} \right\vert < \varepsilon$ | **Relative error test** | For non-zero roots |
| 9 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \left[ \nabla^2 f(x_k) \right]^{-1} \nabla f(x_k)$ | **Multivariate Newton form** | Vector optimization |
| 10 | $f(x) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)\Delta x + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(x_k)\Delta x^{2}$ | **Quadratic Taylor model** | Foundation derivation |

> **Important Notation Convention:** In all convergence error equations, $e_k = x_k - x^*$ denotes the *error* at iteration $k$, and $C$ is a positive constant dependent on the local function derivatives.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

Newton's Method is not a purely academic construct — it is the workhorse algorithm behind:

- **Structural Engineering:** Solving non-linear load-deflection equations in beam analysis.
- **Electrical Power Systems:** Solving the **power flow equations** in the Newton-Raphson load flow algorithm (used in every modern grid simulator).
- **Mechanical Design:** Finding equilibrium points in non-linear spring-mass-damper systems.
- **Computational Finance:** Implied volatility extraction from option pricing models (Black-Scholes inversion).
- **Machine Learning & AI:** As the engine of **second-order optimization** (e.g., Newton's method, L-BFGS variants).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Solving thermodynamic equilibrium equations and reaction kinetics.
- **Computer Graphics:** Ray-surface intersection in real-time rendering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "Give two applications," always cite at least one from **electrical engineering** (Newton-Raphson power flow) and one from **mechanical/structural engineering** to demonstrate breadth.

---

## 2.4 Conditions for Convergence

For Newton's Method to converge to a unique root $x^*$, the following sufficient conditions must hold in a neighborhood of $x^*$:

1. **Smoothness:** $f \in C^2$ (twice continuously differentiable).
2. **Non-vanishing derivative:** $f'(x^*) \neq 0$ (simple root).
3. **Good initial guess:** $x_0$ sufficiently close to $x^*$.
4. **Lipschitz continuity:** $f''$ is bounded by a constant $M$ near $x^*$.
5. **Bounded second derivative ratio:** $\left\vert \dfrac{f''(x)}{2 f'(x)} \right\vert \leq K < \infty$ in the neighborhood.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Failure Modes KTU Students Must Know:**
> - **Division by zero** when $f'(x_k) = 0$ — iteration undefined.
> - **Cycling/oscillation** near inflection points (e.g., solving $x^3 - 2x + 2 = 0$ starting from $x_0 = 0$).
> - **Divergence to infinity** when initial guess is poor.
> - **Convergence to a different root** if the function has multiple roots and the basin of attraction directs elsewhere.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: From Taylor Series to Newton's Formula

We begin with the **second-order Taylor series expansion** of $f(x)$ about a point $x_k$:

$$f(x) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k) + \dfrac{f''(x_k)}{2!}(x - x_k)^{2} + \dfrac{f'''(x_k)}{3!}(x - x_k)^{3} + \cdots$$

Let $x = x^*$ be the true root, so $f(x^*) = 0$. Substitute this into the Taylor series and **truncate after the linear term** to obtain a linearized model:

$$0 = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x^* - x_k) + \mathcal{O}\!\left((x^* - x_k)^{2}\right)$$

Discarding the higher-order remainder term (justified when $x_k$ is near $x^*$):

$$0 \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x^* - x_k)$$

Solving for the error $(x^* - x_k)$:

$$x^* - x_k \approx -\dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$$

Therefore, the *corrected estimate* for $x^*$ is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$$

This is the **Newton-Raphson update**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic Convergence Rate

To rigorously establish that the error squares at each step, we perform a more careful Taylor expansion. Let $e_k = x_k - x^*$ be the error. Expand $f(x_k)$ and $f'(x_k)$ in Taylor series about $x^*$:

$$f(x_k) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)\,e_k + \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2}\,e_k^{2} + \dfrac{f'''(\xi)}{6}\,e_k^{3}$$

$$f'(x_k) = f'(x^*) + f''(x^*)\,e_k + \dfrac{f'''(\zeta)}{2}\,e_k^{2}$$

Since $f(x^*) = 0$ and $f'(x^*) \neq 0$, we simplify:

$$f(x_k) = f'(x^*)\,e_k + \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2}\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})$$

$$f'(x_k) = f'(x^*) + f''(x^*)\,e_k + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{2})$$

Now compute $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$:

$$x_{k+1} = x^* + e_k - \dfrac{f'(x^*)\,e_k + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(x^*)\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})}{f'(x^*) + f''(x^*)\,e_k + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{2})}$$

Factor the denominator as $f'(x^*)\left[1 + \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{2})\right]$ and invert using the geometric series $(1+u)^{-1} = 1 - u + u^{2} - \cdots$:

$$\dfrac{1}{f'(x_k)} = \dfrac{1}{f'(x^*)} \left[1 - \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{2})\right]$$

Substitute back:

$$x_{k+1} = x^* + e_k - \left[f'(x^*)\,e_k + \tfrac{1}{2}f''(x^*)\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})\right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{f'(x^*)} \left[1 - \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{2})\right]$$

Multiplying out the numerator terms:

$$\text{Numerator} \cdot \text{Inverse} = e_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2} - \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})$$

Simplifying the leading second-order terms:

$$\text{Simplified} = e_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})$$

Therefore:

$$x_{k+1} = x^* + e_k - e_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{f''(x^*)}{f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})$$

The $e_k$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$e_{k+1} = \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2\,f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2} + \mathcal{O}(e_k^{3})$$

In the limit as $e_k \to 0$, the dominant term yields:

$$\boxed{\,e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2\,f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2}\,}$$

Since $e_{k+1}$ is proportional to $e_k^{2}$, **the order of convergence is exactly $p = 2$** (quadratic).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Root Finding

**Problem:** Find the positive root of $f(x) = x^{3} - 2x - 5 = 0$ using Newton's Method, starting with $x_0 = 2$.

**Step 1:** Compute the derivative.

$$f'(x) = 3x^{2} - 2$$

**Step 2:** Set up the iteration formula.

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{x_k^{3} - 2x_k - 5}{3x_k^{2} - 2}$$

**Step 3:** Iteration 1 (from $x_0 = 2$).

$$f(2) = 2^{3} - 2(2) - 5 = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$$

$$f'(2) = 3(2)^{2} - 2 = 12 - 2 = 10$$

$$x_1 = 2 - \dfrac{-1}{10} = 2 + 0.1 = 2.1$$

**Step 4:** Iteration 2 (from $x_1 = 2.1$).

$$f(2.1) = (2.1)^{3} - 2(2.1) - 5 = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061$$

$$f'(2.1) = 3(2.1)^{2} - 2 = 3(4.41) - 2 = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$$

$$x_2 = 2.1 - \dfrac{0.061}{11.23} = 2.1 - 0.00543 = 2.09457$$

**Step 5:** Iteration 3 (from $x_2 = 2.09457$).

$$f(2.09457) \approx 9.1848 - 4.1891 - 5 = -0.00430$$

$$f'(2.09457) = 3(2.09457)^{2} - 2 \approx 3(4.38738) - 2 = 11.16214$$

$$x_3 = 2.09457 - \dfrac{-0.00430}{11.16214} \approx 2.09457 + 0.000385 = 2.09496$$

**Step 6:** Iteration 4 (from $x_3 = 2.09496$).

$$f(2.09496) \approx 0.00001 \quad (\text{essentially zero})$$

Therefore the root is $x^* \approx 2.0946$, and notice how the error is squaring at each step — characteristic quadratic convergence.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Optimization

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^{4} - 14x^{3} + 60x^{2} - 70x$ using Newton's Method starting from $x_0 = 4$.

**Step 1:** Compute first and second derivatives.

$$f'(x) = 4x^{3} - 42x^{2} + 120x - 70$$

$$f''(x) = 12x^{2} - 84x + 120$$

**Step 2:** Set up the Newton's optimization iteration.

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} = x_k - \dfrac{4x_k^{3} - 42x_k^{2} + 120x_k - 70}{12x_k^{2} - 84x_k + 120}$$

**Step 3:** Iteration 1.

$$f'(4) = 4(64) - 42(16) + 120(4) - 70 = 256 - 672 + 480 - 70 = -6$$

$$f''(4) = 12(16) - 84(4) + 120 = 192 - 336 + 120 = -24$$

$$x_1 = 4 - \dfrac{-6}{-24} = 4 - 0.25 = 3.75$$

**Step 4:** Iteration 2.

$$f'(3.75) = 4(52.734) - 42(14.0625) + 120(3.75) - 70 = 210.9375 - 590.625 + 450 - 70 = 0.3125$$

$$f''(3.75) = 12(14.0625) - 84(3.75) + 120 = 168.75 - 315 + 120 = -26.25$$

$$x_2 = 3.75 - \dfrac{0.3125}{-26.25} = 3.75 + 0.01190 = 3.76190$$

**Step 5:** Iteration 3.

$$f'(3.76190) \approx 0.0001 \quad (\text{essentially zero})$$

Therefore the minimum occurs at $x^* \approx 3.7619$ with $f(x^*) \approx -23.95$.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Newton's Method — Functional Iteration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input x0, epsilon, max iterations] --> B[Compute f xk and f prime xk]
    B --> C{Derivative equals zero?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Stop: Method Fails - Division by Zero]
    C -- No --> E[Compute x new = xk - f xk divided by f prime xk]
    E --> F[Increment iteration counter k = k+1]
    F --> G{Convergence test |x new - xk| less than epsilon}
    G -- No --> H{k greater than max iter?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Stop: Maximum Iterations Reached]
    H -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> J[Stop: Converged - Output x new as root]
    D --> K[Report Failure to User]
    I --> K
    J --> L[End]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Current Point xk on curve y equals f of x] --> P2[Draw Tangent Line at xk]
    P2 --> P3[Tangent Line Equation: y equals f xk plus f prime xk times x minus xk]
    P3 --> P4[Set y equals zero to find x-intercept]
    P4 --> P5[Solve: x new equals xk minus f xk divided by f prime xk]
    P5 --> P6[New Point x new is closer to true root x star]
    P6 --> P7[Repeat Until |x new - xk| less than epsilon]
```

## 4.3 Newton Optimization Variant — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Start: Input x0 for optimization problem] --> M2[Compute gradient g = f prime xk]
    M2 --> M3[Compute Hessian H = f double prime xk]
    M3 --> M4{H equals zero?}
    M4 -- Yes --> M5[Stop: Hessian singular - cannot invert]
    M4 -- No --> M6[Compute update step delta x = H inverse times g]
    M6 --> M7[Update x new = xk minus delta x]
    M7 --> M8[Check convergence: gradient norm less than epsilon]
    M8 -- No --> M9{k less than max iter?}
    M9 -- Yes --> M2
    M9 -- No --> M10[Stop: Max iterations reached]
    M8 -- Yes --> M11[Stop: Optimal point found at x star]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Newton vs. Other Root-Finding Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph RM[Root Finding Methods]
        R1[Newton Method: x new = xk - f xk over f prime xk]
        R2[Bisection Method: Midpoint bracket search]
        R3[Secant Method: Approximate f prime xk by finite difference]
        R4[Regula Falsi: Linear interpolation between brackets]
    end

    subgraph P1[Convergence Properties]
        Q1[Newton: Order 2, needs derivative]
        Q2[Bisection: Order 1, robust but slow]
        Q3[Secant: Order 1.618, no derivative needed]
        Q4[Regula Falsi: Order 1, bracketed reliability]
    end

    R1 --> Q1
    R2 --> Q2
    R3 --> Q3
    R4 --> Q4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each Mermaid flowchart above traces the execution path of Newton's Method or a closely related variant. The key decision nodes are the *derivative zero check* and the *convergence test*, both of which the KTU examiner will expect you to identify in any algorithm-trace question.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the Newton-Raphson iteration formula for finding a root of $f(x) = 0$. Under what conditions does the method fail to converge?**

**Model Answer:**

The Newton-Raphson formula is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Conditions for failure:**
1. When $f'(x_k) = 0$ at any iteration (division by zero).
2. When the initial guess $x_0$ is too far from the true root.
3. When the function has an inflection point near the root, causing oscillations.
4. When $f$ is not sufficiently smooth (not twice continuously differentiable).
5. When $f'(x^*) = 0$ at the root itself (root of multiplicity $\geq 2$).

**(3 marks — full credit requires formula + at least 3 failure conditions.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is Newton's Method said to have quadratic convergence? Write the error relation that justifies this.**

**Model Answer:**

Newton's Method exhibits **quadratic convergence** because the error at iteration $k+1$ is proportional to the *square* of the error at iteration $k$. The error recurrence is:

$$e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2\,f'(x^*)}\,e_k^{2}$$

This shows $\vert e_{k+1} \vert \approx C \cdot \vert e_k \vert^{2}$ for some constant $C$ near the root $x^*$, which is the **defining equation of quadratic convergence** with order $p = 2$. Consequently, the number of correct decimal places approximately doubles with every iteration once the iterates are close to the root.

**(3 marks — full credit requires: definition of quadratic convergence, the error formula, and the doubling-of-digits interpretation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question is internally optional. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the Newton-Raphson iteration formula for solving $f(x) = 0$ starting from a Taylor series expansion. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Newton's Method, find the root of $x^{3} - 3x - 4 = 0$ lying between $2$ and $3$. Perform three iterations starting from $x_0 = 2$ and report the answer to four decimal places. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Derivation:**

We begin with the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ about a point $x_k$:

$$f(x) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k) + \dfrac{f''(x_k)}{2!}(x - x_k)^{2} + \cdots$$

**Assumption 1:** $x_k$ is sufficiently close to the true root $x^*$, so higher-order terms can be neglected.

Truncating after the linear term:

$$f(x) \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k)$$

**Assumption 2:** Let $x = x^*$ be the true root so that $f(x^*) = 0$. Substituting:

$$0 = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x^* - x_k)$$

**Assumption 3:** $f'(x_k) \neq 0$ so that we can divide by it.

Solving for $x^*$:

$$x^* = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$$

Since $x^* \approx x_{k+1}$ (the next iterate), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\,x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}\,}$$

This completes the derivation. **(7 marks — full credit requires Taylor expansion, stated assumptions, algebraic manipulation, and final formula.)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** $f(x) = x^{3} - 3x - 4 = 0$, $x_0 = 2$.

**Step 1:** Compute $f'(x) = 3x^{2} - 3$.

**Step 2:** Set up iteration formula:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{x_k^{3} - 3x_k - 4}{3x_k^{2} - 3}$$

**Iteration 1 (from $x_0 = 2$):**

$$f(2) = 8 - 6 - 4 = -2$$

$$f'(2) = 12 - 3 = 9$$

$$x_1 = 2 - \dfrac{-2}{9} = 2 + 0.2222 = 2.2222$$

**Iteration 2 (from $x_1 = 2.2222$):**

$$f(2.2222) = (2.2222)^{3} - 3(2.2222) - 4 = 10.9739 - 6.6666 - 4 = 0.3073$$

$$f'(2.2222) = 3(2.2222)^{2} - 3 = 3(4.9382) - 3 = 14.8146 - 3 = 11.8146$$

$$x_2 = 2.2222 - \dfrac{0.3073}{11.8146} = 2.2222 - 0.02601 = 2.1962$$

**Iteration 3 (from $x_2 = 2.1962$):**

$$f(2.1962) = (2.1962)^{3} - 3(2.1962) - 4 = 10.5941 - 6.5886 - 4 = 0.0055$$

$$f'(2.1962) = 3(2.1962)^{2} - 3 = 3(4.8233) - 3 = 14.4699 - 3 = 11.4699$$

$$x_3 = 2.1962 - \dfrac{0.0055}{11.4699} = 2.1962 - 0.00048 = 2.1957$$

**Final Answer:** $x_3 \approx \mathbf{2.1957}$ (correct to 4 decimal places).

**Verification:** $f(2.1957) = (2.1957)^{3} - 3(2.1957) - 4 \approx 10.587 - 6.587 - 4 \approx 0.0000$ ✓

**(7 marks — full credit requires: derivative, formula, all three iterations with intermediate computations, and final answer.)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define Newton's Method for finding the minimum of a function $f(x)$ in one variable. Starting from a Taylor series for $f(x)$, derive the iteration formula and explain why this method is called a *second-order* method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Newton's Method to minimize $f(x) = 2x^{3} - 9x^{2} + 12x + 3$ starting from $x_0 = 1$. Perform two iterations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Definition:**
Newton's Method for finding the minimum of a smooth, unconstrained function $f(x)$ is an iterative algorithm that seeks a stationary point by setting the first derivative to zero, i.e., solving $f'(x) = 0$. The iteration uses both the first and second derivatives of $f$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

**Derivation from Taylor Series:**

Expand $f(x)$ to second order about $x_k$:

$$f(x) \approx f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k) + \dfrac{1}{2}f''(x_k)(x - x_k)^{2}$$

For $x = x^*$ to be a minimum, the first-order optimality condition is $f'(x^*) = 0$. Differentiate the quadratic Taylor model and set to zero:

$$f'(x_k) + f''(x_k)(x^* - x_k) = 0$$

Solving for the next iterate $x_{k+1} = x^*$:

$$\boxed{\,x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}\,}$$

**Why "Second-Order" Method:**

The method is called *second-order* because it uses the **second derivative** (curvature information) in addition to the first derivative. This local quadratic approximation of the objective function is what enables the method to achieve quadratic convergence to the optimum — error squares at each iteration:

$$e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f'''(x^*)}{2\,f''(x^*)}\,e_k^{2}$$

This contrasts with first-order methods (such as gradient descent or the steepest descent method) that use only the gradient and converge linearly. **(7 marks — full credit requires: definition, Taylor expansion, derivation, and the second-order justification.)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Given:** $f(x) = 2x^{3} - 9x^{2} + 12x + 3$, $x_0 = 1$.

**Step 1:** Compute $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$.

$$f'(x) = 6x^{2} - 18x + 12$$

$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

**Step 2:** Set up Newton's optimization iteration:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)} = x_k - \dfrac{6x_k^{2} - 18x_k + 12}{12x_k - 18}$$

**Iteration 1 (from $x_0 = 1$):**

$$f'(1) = 6(1) - 18(1) + 12 = 6 - 18 + 12 = 0$$

$$f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = 12 - 18 = -6$$

$$x_1 = 1 - \dfrac{0}{-6} = 1 - 0 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** Since $f'(1) = 0$ exactly, the iteration converges in a *single step*. The starting point $x_0 = 1$ is already a stationary point of $f(x)$.

**Step 2: Verify by second-derivative test:**

$$f''(1) = -6 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 1 \text{ is a LOCAL MAXIMUM, not a minimum.}$$

**To find the actual minimum,** restart from a different initial guess. Try $x_0 = 3$:

$$f'(3) = 6(9) - 18(3) + 12 = 54 - 54 + 12 = 12$$

$$f''(3) = 12(3) - 18 = 36 - 18 = 18$$

$$x_1 = 3 - \dfrac{12}{18} = 3 - 0.6667 = 2.3333$$

**Iteration 2 (from $x_1 = 2.3333$):**

$$f'(2.3333) = 6(5.4444) - 18(2.3333) + 12 = 32.6664 - 41.9994 + 12 = 2.667$$

$$f''(2.3333) = 12(2.3333) - 18 = 27.9996 - 18 = 9.9996$$

$$x_2 = 2.3333 - \dfrac{2.667}{9.9996} = 2.3333 - 0.2667 = 2.0666$$

**Final Answer:** $x^* \approx \mathbf{2.0666}$ (minimum), with $f(x^*) \approx 2(2.0666)^{3} - 9(2.0666)^{2} + 12(2.0666) + 3 \approx 17.66 - 38.44 + 24.80 + 3 \approx 7.02$.

**(7 marks — full credit requires: derivative formulas, both iterations from a valid starting point, and verification of the nature of the extremum using the second-derivative test.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Newton's Method Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the derivative** — The Newton iteration *requires* $f'(x)$. Students often write the formula without confirming the derivative is non-zero.
> 2. **Sign error in iteration formula** — Confusing $x_{k+1} = x_k - f/f'$ with $x_{k+1} = x_k + f/f'$ costs full marks.
> 3. **Confusing root-finding with optimization formulas** — In KTU, the examiner specifically tests whether you know which form applies. Root form uses $f/f'$; optimization form uses $f'/f''$.
> 4. **Not stating the stopping criterion** — Always write $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert < \varepsilon$ or equivalent before showing iterations.
> 5. **Skipping Taylor expansion** — The derivation question demands the full Taylor series *with stated assumptions*, not just the final formula.
> 6. **Not verifying the nature of the extremum** — When the question says "minimize," always apply the second-derivative test ($f''(x^*) > 0$ for minimum) on the final answer.
> 7. **Rounding too early** — Carry at least 5–6 significant figures through intermediate computations to avoid compounding errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton-Raphson formula for root finding:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}$
- **Newton's optimization formula:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$
- **Multivariate form:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - [\nabla^{2} f(x_k)]^{-1} \nabla f(x_k)$
- **Convergence order:** $p = 2$ (quadratic) — error squares at each step once iterates are close to the root.
- **Error relation (root form):** $e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2f'(x^*)} e_k^{2}$
- **Error relation (optimization form):** $e_{k+1} \approx \dfrac{f'''(x^*)}{2f''(x^*)} e_k^{2}$
- **Geometric meaning:** Tangent-line intersection with the x-axis (or with $f'(x)$ axis in optimization form).
- **Stopping criteria:** $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert < \varepsilon$ **OR** $\vert f(x_k) \vert < \varepsilon$ **OR** relative error test.
- **Hard requirements:** $f \in C^{2}$, $f'(x^*) \neq 0$, good initial guess $x_0$ near the root.
- **Failure modes:** $f'(x_k) = 0$, cycling near inflection points, divergence for poor $x_0$, convergence to wrong root.
- **Key engineering application:** Newton-Raphson **power flow analysis** in electrical power systems.
- **KTU must-derive:** The full Taylor series derivation from first principles — this appears in nearly every exam cycle.
- **Quadratic convergence = "double the digits per step"** — the single most quotable property in viva voce.
- **Always verify the second derivative sign** at the converged point when the problem asks for a minimum or maximum.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — NEWTON'S METHOD**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech PEFTT758 — Optimization Techniques.*

topics.title: Conjugate Gradient Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conjugate Gradient Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Conjugate Gradient (CG) Method**, originally proposed by Hestenes and Stiefel (1952), is a powerful iterative numerical algorithm used to solve systems of linear equations whose coefficient matrix is **symmetric and positive-definite (SPD)**, and equivalently, to find the unconstrained minimum of a strictly convex quadratic function of the form:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c
$$

where $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) matrix, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and $c \in \mathbb{R}$. The method generates a sequence of search directions $\mathbf{d}_0, \mathbf{d}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{d}_k$ that are mutually **Q-conjugate** (i.e., $\mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$), and converges to the global minimizer in **at most $n$ iterations** for an $n$-dimensional quadratic — making it optimal among Krylov subspace methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conjugate Gradient is treated as a *second-order* iterative scheme that bridges the gap between the simplicity of Steepest Descent and the computational cost of Newton's Method. It uses only first-order gradient information yet achieves superlinear-like convergence for quadratic objectives.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Walking Down a Valley with Cleverly Chosen Footsteps"**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded on a mountain and must reach the lowest valley point. **Steepest Descent** tells you to always step directly downhill — but you may zig-zag across the valley and take hundreds of steps. **Newton's Method** tells you the exact shape of the valley but requires a costly map (the Hessian inverse). 
>
> **Conjugate Gradient** is the smart middle-ground: you take your first step straight downhill, but for the *next* step, you deliberately choose a direction that is **"perpendicular to the previous step, but in a curved-geometry sense"** (i.e., Q-orthogonal). This means each new step *undoes no work already done* — you never backtrack. For a 2D quadratic valley, you reach the bottom in **exactly 2 steps**; for $n$ dimensions, in **at most $n$ steps**.

---

### 1.3 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Mathematical Definition | Plain-English Meaning |
|------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| $\mathbf{x}_k$ | Current iterate | Where you are standing |
| $\mathbf{r}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Residual / negative gradient | "Local downhill direction" |
| $\mathbf{d}_k$ | Search direction | The actual step direction taken |
| $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate | $\mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_j = 0,\ i \neq j$ | "Curve-perpendicular" directions |
| $\alpha_k$ | Step length (scalar) | How far to step in $\mathbf{d}_k$ |
| $\beta_k$ | Conjugate update coefficient | How the new direction is built |
| SPD matrix | $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} > 0\ \forall\ \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Guarantees a unique valley minimum |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** CG is the *workhorse* solver in structural engineering (finite-element analysis), computational fluid dynamics, machine learning (training of large kernel models), and signal processing. Whenever you see a system $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with a large, sparse, SPD $\mathbf{Q}$, CG is almost always the algorithm of choice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Why Q-Conjugacy Works

The core insight is that if the search directions $\{\mathbf{d}_0, \mathbf{d}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{d}_{n-1}\}$ are mutually $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate (linearly independent by construction), and we perform an **exact line search** along each direction, then the minimum is reached in at most $n$ iterations.

**Proof Sketch of Optimality:**
After $k$ iterations, the iterate can be expanded as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{x}_0 + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \alpha_j \mathbf{d}_j
$$

At the true minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, the error $\mathbf{e}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^*$ satisfies:

$$
\mathbf{e}_k = \sum_{j=k}^{n-1} \xi_j \mathbf{d}_j
$$

Because conjugacy ensures that $\mathbf{d}_j^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_i = 0$ for $j \neq i$, the optimal $\alpha_k$ from line search simultaneously annihilates the $k$-th component of the error — hence in $n$ steps, all components vanish.

---

### 2.2 Derivation of the Fletcher–Reeves Update

Starting from the requirement that the new direction $\mathbf{d}_{k+1}$ is a linear combination of the current residual $\mathbf{r}_{k+1}$ and the previous direction $\mathbf{d}_k$:

$$
\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{r}_{k+1} + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_k
$$

Imposing the conjugacy constraint $\mathbf{d}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k = 0$:

$$
(-\mathbf{r}_{k+1} + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k = 0
$$

$$
-\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k = 0
$$

$$
\beta_k = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k}
$$

Using the residual recursion $\mathbf{r}_{k+1} = \mathbf{r}_k - \alpha_k \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k$ and the conjugacy property, this simplifies to the celebrated **Fletcher–Reeves (FR) formula**:

$$
\beta_k^{FR} = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{r}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Other classical variants include **Polak–Ribière** $\beta_k^{PR} = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T (\mathbf{r}_{k+1} - \mathbf{r}_k)}{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k}$ and **Hestenes–Stiefel** $\beta_k^{HS} = \frac{\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T (\mathbf{r}_{k+1} - \mathbf{r}_k)}{\mathbf{d}_k^T (\mathbf{r}_{k+1} - \mathbf{r}_k)}$ — all reduce to the same formula for exact line search on quadratics.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Units / Dimension |
|---|---------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$ | Quadratic objective | Scalar (Joules, cost) |
| 2 | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$ | Gradient (vector field) | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{r}_k = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k$ | Residual (= $-\nabla f$) | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_j = 0,\ i \neq j$ | $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugacy | Scalar (= 0) |
| 5 | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_k}$ | Exact step length (scalar) | Scalar (m, rad) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | Iterative update | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 7 | $\mathbf{r}_{k+1} = \mathbf{r}_k - \alpha_k \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k$ | Residual recursion | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | $\beta_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{r}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{r}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k}$ | Fletcher–Reeves coefficient | Scalar (dimensionless) |
| 9 | $\mathbf{d}_{k+1} = \mathbf{r}_{k+1} + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | New search direction | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 10 | Stop: $\vert \mathbf{r}_k \vert \leq \varepsilon$ or $\vert \mathbf{r}_k \vert / \vert \mathbf{r}_0 \vert \leq 10^{-6}$ | Convergence test | Scalar (tolerance) |
| 11 | Condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) = \lambda_{\max} / \lambda_{\min}$ | Controls convergence speed | Scalar (ratio) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** Students often confuse the residual $\mathbf{r}_k = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k$ with the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$. Note that $\mathbf{r}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Both are valid descent directions; the sign convention determines whether you write the direction as $\mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{r}_k$ or $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f$.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Structural FE Analysis:** Solving $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ for displacement fields where $\mathbf{K}$ is the SPD stiffness matrix. CG handles millions of DOF without forming $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$.
- **Machine Learning / Kernel Methods:** CG is used to train Support Vector Machines and Gaussian Process regressors by solving kernel linear systems.
- **Image Reconstruction:** Computed Tomography (CT) reconstruction uses CG to invert large sparse systems.
- **Optimal Control:** Solving the discrete-time Riccati equation in Linear-Quadratic Regulators (LQR) is essentially a CG-like Krylov iteration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Step Length $\alpha_k$

We seek $\alpha_k$ that minimizes the one-dimensional function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ along the direction $\mathbf{d}_k$.

$$
\phi(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) - \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) + c
$$

Expanding the quadratic term:

$$
\phi(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k + \frac{\alpha^2}{2}\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{d}_k + c
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\alpha$ and setting to zero (first-order optimality):

$$
\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = \mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k = 0
$$

Recognizing that $\mathbf{x}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{d}_k = -(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$:

$$
\alpha \mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k
$$

$$
\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k}
$$

When the line search is **exact** and the directions are $\mathbf{Q}$-conjugate, we can further simplify $\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k$ (using the fact that $\mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{r}_k + \beta_{k-1}\mathbf{d}_{k-1}$ and conjugacy), yielding:

$$
\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{r}_k^T \mathbf{r}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_k}
$$

---

### 3.2 Convergence Rate of the Conjugate Gradient Method

The CG method satisfies the following elegant error bound in the energy norm $\vert\vert \mathbf{e} \vert\vert_{\mathbf{Q}} = \sqrt{\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{e}}$:

$$
\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert_{\mathbf{Q}} \leq \left( \frac{\sqrt{\kappa(\mathbf{Q})} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa(\mathbf{Q})} + 1} \right)^{k+1} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}^* \vert\vert_{\mathbf{Q}}
$$

where $\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) = \lambda_{\max} / \lambda_{\min}$ is the spectral condition number. The convergence factor:

$$
\rho = \frac{\sqrt{\kappa} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa} + 1}
$$

is strictly less than 1 (and approaches 0 as $\kappa \to 1$). This explains why **preconditioning** (transforming $\mathbf{Q}$ to a better-conditioned matrix) is critical for large-scale CG.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** When $\kappa(\mathbf{Q}) = 1$ (perfectly conditioned), $\rho = 0$ and CG converges in **a single iteration** regardless of $n$ — because all eigenvalues are equal, the quadratic is a perfect sphere.

---

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example (2-Variable Quadratic)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + 2x_2^2 - 5x_1 - 7x_2$ using CG.

#### Step 1: Identify the $\mathbf{Q}$ matrix and $\mathbf{b}$ vector

$$
\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(Check: $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} = 2x_1^2 + 2x_1x_2 + 2x_2^2$ ✓)

#### Step 2: Initialisation at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$

$$
\mathbf{r}_0 = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{d}_0 = \mathbf{r}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 3: First iteration

$$
\mathbf{d}_0^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 34 \\ 38 \end{bmatrix} = 170 + 266 = 436
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_0^T \mathbf{r}_0 = 25 + 49 = 74
$$

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{74}{436} = \frac{37}{218} \approx 0.16972
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.16972 \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8486 \\ 1.1881 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4: Update residual and compute $\beta_0$

$$
\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 34 \\ 38 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} - 0.16972 \begin{bmatrix} 34 \\ 38 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7706 \\ 0.5505 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_1^T \mathbf{r}_1 = 0.5938 + 0.3030 = 0.8968
$$

$$
\beta_0 = \frac{0.8968}{74} \approx 0.01212
$$

$$
\mathbf{d}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7706 \\ 0.5505 \end{bmatrix} + 0.01212 \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7100 \\ 0.6353 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 5: Verify conjugacy of $\mathbf{d}_1$ and $\mathbf{d}_0$

$$
\mathbf{d}_1^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7100 & 0.6353 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 34 \\ 38 \end{bmatrix} = -24.14 + 24.14 = 0 \ \checkmark
$$

#### Step 6: Second iteration

$$
\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{d}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(-0.7100) + 2(0.6353) \\ 2(-0.7100) + 4(0.6353) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5694 \\ 1.1212 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{d}_1^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{d}_1 = (-0.7100)(-1.5694) + (0.6353)(1.1212) = 1.1143 + 0.7123 = 1.8266
$$

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{0.8968}{1.8266} \approx 0.4910
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8486 \\ 1.1881 \end{bmatrix} + 0.4910 \begin{bmatrix} -0.7100 \\ 0.6353 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5000 \\ 1.5000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 7: Verify optimality

Solving $\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}$ directly:

$$
4x_1 + 2x_2 = 5
$$

$$
2x_1 + 4x_2 = 7
$$

Yields $x_1^* = 0.5,\ x_2^* = 1.5$ — **CG converged in exactly $n=2$ iterations**, as theoretically guaranteed.

#### Analytical Matrix: Iteration Summary

| $k$ | $\mathbf{x}_k$ | $\mathbf{r}_k$ | $\mathbf{d}_k$ | $\alpha_k$ | $\beta_k$ |
|-----|----------------|----------------|----------------|------------|-----------|
| 0 | $(0, 0)^T$ | $(5, 7)^T$ | $(5, 7)^T$ | 0.16972 | 0.01212 |
| 1 | $(0.8486, 1.1881)^T$ | $(-0.7706, 0.5505)^T$ | $(-0.7100, 0.6353)^T$ | 0.4910 | — |
| 2 | $(0.5000, 1.5000)^T$ | $\approx \mathbf{0}$ | — | — | — |

---

### 3.4 Extension to Non-Quadratic Functions (Fletcher–Reeves)

For a general nonlinear function $f(\mathbf{x})$, the CG method generalises by replacing the quadratic step length with an **inexact line search** (e.g., Wolfe conditions) and the residual with the negative gradient:

$$
\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k), \qquad \mathbf{d}_{k+1} = -\mathbf{g}_{k+1} + \beta_k \mathbf{d}_k
$$

The Fletcher–Reeves coefficient becomes:

$$
\beta_k^{FR} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_{k+1}}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}
$$

**Global convergence** is guaranteed under strong Wolfe conditions. For non-quadratics, CG may need more than $n$ iterations, but typically converges in a small multiple of $n$ for well-conditioned problems.
