## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Graphical Method** is a direct, geometric, two-dimensional solution procedure for solving a **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** that contains exactly **two decision variables** ($x_1$ and $x_2$). It involves plotting all the linear constraints as half-planes on a Cartesian coordinate system, identifying the **feasible region** (the convex polygon of all points that simultaneously satisfy every constraint), and then moving the **objective function line** (iso-profit or iso-cost line) across this region to locate the **optimal extreme point (corner point)** that either maximizes or minimizes the objective.

In formal KTU notation, an LPP solved graphically is expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } \; & Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 \\
\text{subject to } \; & a_{i1} x_1 + a_{i2} x_2 \;\{\leq, \;=, \;\geq\}\; b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Graphical Method is the *foundational pre-requisite* before students learn the Simplex Method. Every Module 2 derivation or numerical in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) on the Simplex Method assumes you already understand graphical corner-point analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Building

Imagine you are a **factory manager** with two products (say, *Wooden Chairs* and *Wooden Tables*) to manufacture. Each product consumes a fixed amount of two resources — *wood* and *labour-hours*. You have a **limited budget of wood and labour**. The constraints are like fences around a playground: the fence of *wood availability* allows only a certain area, the fence of *labour* allows another, and the fence of *non-negativity* says you cannot make a negative number of chairs. The intersection of all these fences is the **playground** — this is your **feasible region**. Your job is to find the point in this playground that gives the **maximum profit**. By sliding a "profit string" (the objective function line) as far outward as possible without leaving the playground, the last point of contact is the **optimum corner**.

### 1.3 Core Components of Any Graphical LPP

| Component | Mathematical Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Decision Variables | $x_1, x_2$ | Coordinates on the 2-D plane |
| Objective Function | $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ | Family of parallel straight lines |
| Constraints | $a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 \leq b_i$ | Half-planes bounded by straight lines |
| Non-Negativity | $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$ | Restricts solution to the **First Quadrant** |
| Feasible Region | Set intersection of all half-planes | A **convex polygon** (or empty / unbounded) |
| Optimal Solution | $(x_1^*, x_2^*)$ | A **corner point** of the feasible region |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Geometric Fact (Fundamental Theorem of LPP):** If an LPP with two variables has an optimal solution, it will always occur at a **vertex (corner point)** of the convex feasible region. This is the *corner-point optimality theorem*, which is also the geometric foundation of the Simplex Method in Module 3.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Graphical Method Works — Theoretical Foundation

The validity of the Graphical Method rests on three pillars of linear algebra and convex geometry:

1. **Linearity of Constraints:** Every constraint $a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 \leq b$ defines a **half-plane** whose boundary is a straight line. The feasible region, being the intersection of finitely many half-planes, is a **convex set** (a polygon, possibly unbounded or empty).

2. **Linearity of the Objective Function:** The equation $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ represents an infinite family of **parallel lines**, each corresponding to a constant value of $Z$. The normal vector to these lines is $\nabla Z = (c_1, c_2)$.

3. **Corner-Point Optimality Theorem:** Because both the feasible set and the level sets of the objective are **convex**, the maximum (or minimum) of $Z$ over the feasible set — if it exists and is finite — must be attained at an **extreme point** of the convex polygon. Sliding the objective line outward, the **last point of tangency with the polygon** is the optimum.

> [!TIP]
> **Why parallel lines?** The objective function $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ can be rewritten as $x_2 = -\dfrac{c_1}{c_2} x_1 + \dfrac{Z}{c_2}$. The slope $-c_1/c_2$ is constant; only the intercept $Z/c_2$ changes. So as $Z$ grows, the line shifts in the direction of the gradient vector $(c_1, c_2)$.

### 2.2 Special / Degenerate Cases Encountered Graphically

| Case | Geometric Description | Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unique Optimum** | Objective line touches one corner | Single optimal point |
| **Alternate Optima (Multiple Optima)** | Objective line is **parallel** to a constraint edge and coincides with it | Infinitely many optimal solutions (entire edge) |
| **Unbounded Solution** | Feasible region is open; objective line can slide infinitely | LPP has **no finite maximum** (or minimum) |
| **Infeasible Solution** | No common intersection of all half-planes | **No feasible solution exists** — LPP is inconsistent |
| **Redundant Constraint** | Constraint line lies completely outside the feasible region | Can be removed without changing the optimum |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ | General 2-variable objective function | Compute profit/cost at any corner |
| 2 | $x_2 = \dfrac{b}{a_2} - \dfrac{a_1}{a_2} x_1$ | Intercept form of a constraint line | Plotting $x_1$ and $x_2$ intercepts |
| 3 | $x_1\text{-intercept} = \dfrac{b}{a_1}$ | Where the constraint line crosses the $x_1$-axis | Quick plotting on graph paper |
| 4 | $x_2\text{-intercept} = \dfrac{b}{a_2}$ | Where the constraint line crosses the $x_2$-axis | Quick plotting on graph paper |
| 5 | $\nabla Z = (c_1, c_2)$ | Direction of steepest increase of $Z$ | Direction of slide of objective line |
| 6 | Slope of objective line $= -\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ | Slope of the iso-profit/iso-cost line | Detect parallel-to-edge alternate optima |
| 7 | Convex combination: $\lambda P_1 + (1-\lambda) P_2$ | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ generates any edge point | Used in alternate optima cases |
| 8 | Corner-point evaluation rule | Compute $Z$ at every vertex; pick max/min | Bypass the need to draw the objective line |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** When using the *corner-point evaluation method* (a tabular variant of the graphical method), students often forget to compute the $Z$-value at the **origin** $(0,0)$. The origin is always a corner of the feasible region in a standard LPP and must always be evaluated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Planning:** Deciding optimal mix of two products in a factory under raw-material and machine-hour constraints.
- **Diet Problem (Operations Research):** Minimizing cost of two nutrients subject to minimum daily requirements.
- **Blending Problems in Petroleum Engineering:** Optimal mixing of two crude oils to meet quality (e.g., octane rating) at minimum cost.
- **Civil Engineering — Truss Design:** Choosing between two member thicknesses to minimize weight under stress constraints.
- **Portfolio Selection in Finance:** Allocating capital between two assets to maximize return under risk constraints.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Algorithm (The KTU Board Procedure)

The KTU examiners expect students to write the following **eight formal steps** in the ESE answer sheet. Memorize and reproduce them in this exact order:

**Step 1 — Formulate the LPP mathematically.**
Identify the decision variables, express the objective function in the form "Optimize $Z$", and write down all constraints including non-negativity.

**Step 2 — Convert all inequalities to equalities to plot boundary lines.**
For each constraint $a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 \leq b$, replace $\leq$ with $=$ to obtain the line $a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 = b$. This line is the *boundary* of the half-plane.

**Step 3 — Compute the intercepts.**
Set $x_1 = 0$ to find the $x_2$-intercept as $b / a_2$, and $x_2 = 0$ to find the $x_1$-intercept as $b / a_1$. These two points are plotted and joined to draw the constraint line.

**Step 4 — Identify the feasible half-plane.**
Test the origin $(0, 0)$ in the original inequality. If $0 \leq b$ is true, the feasible side includes the origin (i.e., towards the lower-left). Otherwise, the feasible side is away from the origin. Shade this half-plane.

**Step 5 — Repeat for all constraints and identify the common feasible region.**
The intersection of all shaded half-planes, restricted to the first quadrant by non-negativity, is the **feasible region** (a convex polygon).

**Step 6 — Find the corner points of the feasible region.**
The corner points are the intersections of the constraint lines that bound the polygon. Solve pairs of equations simultaneously to find their $(x_1, x_2)$ coordinates.

**Step 7 — Evaluate the objective function at every corner point.**
Substitute the coordinates of each corner into $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$. Tabulate the values.

**Step 8 — Select the optimum.**
The corner with the largest $Z$ (for maximization) or smallest $Z$ (for minimization) is the optimal solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on the Two Methods:** Step 6 + 7 is called the **Corner-Point Method** (or *enumeration method*). Step 6 + 7 can alternatively be replaced by **drawing the objective function line** $c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 = 0$ (passing through origin) and sliding it parallel to itself — this is the **Iso-Profit / Iso-Cost Line Method**. Both are accepted in KTU exams; the corner-point method is generally faster and more accurate.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard 2-Variable LPP

**Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):**
A company manufactures two products P and Q. Each unit of P requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labour. Each unit of Q requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of labour. The available machine time is 12 hours and labour is 21 hours. Profit per unit is ₹5 for P and ₹4 for Q. Formulate and solve graphically to maximize profit.

#### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Formulation:**
Let $x_1$ = units of P produced, $x_2$ = units of Q produced.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & Z = 5 x_1 + 4 x_2 \\
\text{subject to } \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 12 \quad \text{(Machine time)} \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 21 \quad \text{(Labour time)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to equations and find intercepts:**

For constraint (1): $2x_1 + x_2 = 12$
- If $x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 12$
- If $x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 6$

For constraint (2): $x_1 + 3x_2 = 21$
- If $x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 7$
- If $x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 21$

**Step 3 — Find corner points of the feasible region:**

The feasible region is a quadrilateral with vertices at:

- **A** = Origin $(0, 0)$
- **B** = $(6, 0)$ on the $x_1$-axis
- **C** = Intersection of $2x_1 + x_2 = 12$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 21$
- **D** = $(0, 7)$ on the $x_2$-axis

Solving simultaneously for C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 &= 12 \quad &\Rightarrow& \quad x_2 = 12 - 2x_1 \\
x_1 + 3x_2 &= 21 \quad &\Rightarrow& \quad x_1 + 3(12 - 2x_1) = 21 \\
x_1 + 36 - 6x_1 &= 21 \\
-5x_1 &= 21 - 36 = -15 \\
x_1 &= 3 \\
x_2 &= 12 - 2(3) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

So **C** $= (3, 6)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $Z$ at every corner:**

| Corner Point | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| B | $(6, 0)$ | $30$ |
| **C** | $\mathbf{(3, 6)}$ | $\mathbf{15 + 24 = 39}$ |
| D | $(0, 7)$ | $28$ |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The maximum value of $Z$ is **₹39**, attained at $(x_1, x_2) = (3, 6)$. Produce **3 units of P** and **6 units of Q**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge-Case Detection Rules

| Observation on Graph | Algebraic Detection Rule | Conclusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Objective line coincides with a binding constraint edge at optimum | $c_1 / a_1 = c_2 / a_2$ (slope of objective = slope of that edge) | **Alternate (multiple) optimal solutions** |
| Feasible region is closed and bounded | All constraints are $\leq$ type with positive $b$ | **Unique optimum always exists** |
| Feasible region is open (extends to infinity) in direction of gradient | At least one constraint is $\geq$ or no upper bound | **Solution may be unbounded** — test further |
| Two constraint lines are parallel with no common intersection between the half-planes | Constraints form parallel fences with opposite feasible sides | **Infeasible** — no solution |
| Origin is the *only* feasible point | All constraints have negative $b$ | Trivial / degenerate |
| A constraint line passes through the interior, not the boundary | It does not touch any corner | **Redundant** — can be dropped |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Eight-Step Graphical Solution Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Formulate LPP mathematically"] --> B["Step 2: Convert inequalities to equalities"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Find x1 and x2 intercepts of each line"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Plot lines and identify feasible half-planes"]
    D --> E{"Step 5: Does the feasible region exist?"}
    E -- "No" --> F["Conclusion: Infeasible LPP - No solution"]
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Step 6: Find all corner points of the region"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Evaluate Z at every corner point"]
    H --> I{"Step 8: Does the objective line coincide with an edge?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Conclusion: Alternate optima exist - entire edge is optimal"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Identify the corner with max or min Z"]
    K --> L["Conclusion: Unique optimal solution obtained"]
    F --> M["Report Result to User"]
    J --> M
    L --> M
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    subgraph DECISION["DECISION LOGIC LAYER"]

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]

    P1 --> Q1

    R3 -- "Yes" --> S3

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the term "feasible region" in the context of the Graphical Method of solving a Linear Programming Problem. What is its geometric shape, and why does the optimal solution always occur at a corner point?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **feasible region** is the set of all points $(x_1, x_2)$ in the Cartesian plane that simultaneously satisfy every linear constraint and the non-negativity conditions of the LPP. Geometrically, it is a **convex polygon** (possibly unbounded or empty), formed by the intersection of all the constraint half-planes.

The optimal solution always occurs at a **corner point (vertex)** of this polygon because both the feasible set and the level sets of the linear objective function are **convex sets**. By the **Corner-Point Optimality Theorem**, the extreme of a linear function over a convex polytope must occur at an extreme point (vertex) of the polytope. Hence, we only need to evaluate $Z$ at the finite set of corner points to find the optimum.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*What are the conditions under which a 2-variable LPP has (i) no feasible solution, (ii) an unbounded solution, and (iii) infinitely many optimal solutions? Mention how each case is identified graphically.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(i) No feasible solution (Infeasibility):** Occurs when the half-planes of the constraints have **no common intersection** — i.e., the feasible region is *empty*. Identified graphically when the shaded half-planes do not overlap.
- **(ii) Unbounded solution:** Occurs when the feasible region is **unbounded** in the direction of the gradient vector $(c_1, c_2)$ of the objective. The iso-profit line can be moved infinitely outward without leaving the feasible region. Identified when the polygon extends to infinity and the objective line has no last contact.
- **(iii) Infinitely many optimal solutions (Alternate Optima):** Occurs when the objective function line is **parallel to one of the binding constraint edges** at the optimum. Every point on that edge gives the same $Z$-value. Identified by slope equality $-\dfrac{c_1}{c_2} = -\dfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ of the objective and a constraint.

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model QP]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> A furniture company produces chairs and tables. Each chair requires 2 hours of carpentry and 1 hour of painting. Each table requires 1 hour of carpentry and 3 hours of painting. The available carpentry hours are 100 and painting hours are 90. The profit per chair is ₹50 and per table is ₹40.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the above as a Linear Programming Problem. **(3 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Solve the LPP using the **Graphical Method** to find the optimal production mix and the maximum profit. Show all steps and the feasible region clearly. **(11 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — LPP Formulation (3 Marks)

Let $x_1$ = number of chairs produced per week, $x_2$ = number of tables produced per week.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2 \\
\text{subject to } \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100 \quad \text{(Carpentry hours)} \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 90 \quad \text{(Painting hours)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Graphical Solution (11 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert inequalities to equalities:**

For constraint (1): $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ → Intercepts: $(50, 0)$ and $(0, 100)$
For constraint (2): $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$ → Intercepts: $(90, 0)$ and $(0, 30)$

**Step 2 — Identify corner points of the feasible region:**

The feasible region is the polygon with corners:
- O = $(0, 0)$
- A = $(50, 0)$
- B = Intersection of $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$
- C = $(0, 30)$

**Step 3 — Find B by solving the two equations simultaneously:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 &= 100 \\
x_1 + 3x_2 &= 90
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply the second equation by 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + 6x_2 &= 180 \\
2x_1 + x_2 &= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5x_2 &= 80 \\
x_2 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute back into $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + 16 &= 100 \\
2x_1 &= 84 \\
x_1 &= 42
\end{aligned}
$$

So **B** $= (42, 16)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Z at all corners:**

| Corner Point | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $Z = 50x_1 + 40x_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| O | $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| A | $(50, 0)$ | $2500$ |
| **B** | $\mathbf{(42, 16)}$ | $\mathbf{2100 + 640 = 2740}$ |
| C | $(0, 30)$ | $1200$ |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The optimal solution is to produce **42 chairs** and **16 tables**, yielding a **maximum profit of ₹2740**.

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Model QP]**

> **Question B (14 Marks):**
>
> A dietician is preparing a diet plan using two foods, F1 and F2. Each unit of F1 contains 2 units of vitamin A and 3 units of vitamin B. Each unit of F2 contains 4 units of vitamin A and 2 units of vitamin B. The minimum daily requirements are 40 units of vitamin A and 30 units of vitamin B. The cost of F1 is ₹5 per unit and F2 is ₹4 per unit.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the LPP. **(3 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Solve graphically to determine the minimum cost diet plan. Show the feasible region and the optimal corner. **(11 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — LPP Formulation (3 Marks)

Let $x_1$ = units of food F1, $x_2$ = units of food F2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & Z = 5 x_1 + 4 x_2 \\
\text{subject to } \quad & 2x_1 + 4x_2 \geq 40 \quad \text{(Vitamin A)} \\
& 3x_1 + 2x_2 \geq 30 \quad \text{(Vitamin B)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Graphical Solution (11 Marks)

**Step 1 — Find the intercepts of each constraint line:**

Constraint (1): $2x_1 + 4x_2 = 40$ → Intercepts: $(20, 0)$ and $(0, 10)$
Constraint (2): $3x_1 + 2x_2 = 30$ → Intercepts: $(10, 0)$ and $(0, 15)$

**Step 2 — Identify corner points (feasible region is unbounded above-right):**

Corner points:
- A = $(20, 0)$
- B = Intersection of $2x_1 + 4x_2 = 40$ and $3x_1 + 2x_2 = 30$
- C = $(0, 15)$

**Step 3 — Solve for B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + 4x_2 &= 40 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 + 2x_2 = 20 \\
3x_1 + 2x_2 &= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 &= 10 \\
x_1 &= 5 \\
x_2 &= \frac{20 - 5}{2} = 7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

So **B** $= (5, 7.5)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $Z$ at all corners:**

| Corner Point | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | $(20, 0)$ | $100$ |
| **B** | $\mathbf{(5, 7.5)}$ | $\mathbf{25 + 30 = 55}$ |
| C | $(0, 15)$ | $60$ |

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The minimum-cost diet plan is **5 units of F1 and 7.5 units of F2**, with a **minimum cost of ₹55**. (For a minimization problem with $\geq$ constraints, we confirm the region is unbounded but the objective line still attains a finite minimum — this is the *bounded minimum* case.)

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Graphical Method Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting non-negativity:** Marks deducted if the first-quadrant restriction is not explicitly stated in the formulation.
> 2. **Wrong feasible side:** Test the origin in *every* inequality before shading — students often shade the wrong half-plane when all $b_i$ are positive (the feasible side is the side *containing* the origin).
> 3. **Missing the origin corner:** Always include $(0, 0)$ in the corner-point table — it is a legitimate vertex.
> 4. **Unbounded minimum not flagged:** If a minimization problem has $\geq$ constraints and the feasible region is unbounded, you must check whether the objective line can slide to $-\infty$. If it cannot, the bounded minimum is valid; if it can, the problem has **no finite minimum**.
> 5. **Slope of objective line confused with gradient:** The slope of $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ is $-c_1/c_2$, not $+c_1/c_2$. This sign error misleads alternate-optima detection.
> 6. **No units in final answer:** Always mention units (₹, units, hours) in the final answer for full marks.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The Graphical Method works **only for 2-variable LPPs**; for higher dimensions, the Simplex Method is used.
- [x] Always **draw the feasible region first**, *then* identify the corners; never compute corners before confirming feasibility.
- [x] Use the **origin test**: if $(0, 0)$ satisfies an inequality, the feasible half-plane contains the origin.
- [x] The **Corner-Point Method** (evaluate $Z$ at all vertices) is more accurate than the iso-profit line method and is preferred in KTU exams.
- [x] The optimal value of $Z$ in maximization problems always lies on the **farthest** corner in the direction of the gradient $(c_1, c_2)$.
- [x] The optimal value of $Z$ in minimization problems always lies on the **nearest** corner in the direction of the gradient $(c_1, c_2)$.
- [x] **Alternate optima** are detected when the objective function line is **parallel to a binding constraint** at the optimum — this means infinitely many solutions exist.
- [x] **Infeasibility** = empty feasible region (no common intersection of half-planes).
- [x] **Unboundedness** = the objective function can increase (for max) or decrease (for min) without limit over the feasible region.
- [x] **Redundant constraint** = a constraint that does not change the feasible region if removed; it lies strictly outside the polygon.
- [x] The **Fundamental Theorem of LPP** guarantees an optimal solution (if it exists) lies at an *extreme point* of the convex feasible region.
- [x] KTU Module 2 also expects students to **interpret the solution** in the original engineering/business context — do not end with just numbers; state the practical recommendation.
