## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **Dijkstra's algorithm** and **Bellman-Ford algorithm** for the shortest path problem.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Dijkstra | Bellman-Ford |
|---|---|---|
| Edge weights | Non-negative only | Handles negative weights |
| Time complexity | $O((V+E) \log V)$ | $O(VE)$ |
| Negative cycle detection | Cannot detect | Detects |
| Approach | Greedy (settles nodes once) | Dynamic programming (relaxes all edges $V-1$ times) |

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State and explain the **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem**.

**Model Answer:**
The **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** states that in any flow network, the **maximum possible flow from a source node $s$ to a sink node $t$** is equal to the **minimum capacity of all $s\text{-}t$ cuts** in the network.
Mathematically:
$$
\max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S,T)} C(S, T)
$$
where $C(S, T)$ is the capacity of cut $(S, T)$ separating $s$ and $t$.
This theorem is fundamental in network optimization and forms the theoretical basis of the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 ESE Pattern, Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose either A or B)*

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Find the shortest path from node **1** to node **6** in the following directed graph using **Dijkstra's Algorithm**.

| Edge | 1-2 | 1-3 | 2-3 | 2-4 | 3-4 | 3-5 | 4-5 | 4-6 | 5-6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | 4 | 2 | 1 | 5 | 8 | 10 | 2 | 6 | 3 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Initial:** dist(1)=0, all others=∞.

**Iteration 1 — Visit node 1 (dist = 0):**
- dist(2) = min(∞, 0+4) = **4**, prev(2) = 1
- dist(3) = min(∞, 0+2) = **2**, prev(3) = 1

Visited: {1}.

**Iteration 2 — Visit node 3 (smallest dist = 2):**
- dist(2) = min(4, 2+1) = **3**, prev(2) = 3
- dist(4) = min(∞, 2+8) = **10**, prev(4) = 3
- dist(5) = min(∞, 2+10) = **12**, prev(5) = 3

Visited: {1, 3}.

**Iteration 3 — Visit node 2 (dist = 3):**
- dist(4) = min(10, 3+5) = **8**, prev(4) = 2
- (3 visited, skip)

Visited: {1, 3, 2}.

**Iteration 4 — Visit node 4 (dist = 8):**
- dist(5) = min(12, 8+2) = **10**, prev(5) = 4
- dist(6) = min(∞, 8+6) = **14**, prev(6) = 4

Visited: {1, 3, 2, 4}.

**Iteration 5 — Visit node 5 (dist = 10):**
- dist(6) = min(14, 10+3) = **13**, prev(6) = 5

Visited: {1, 3, 2, 4, 5}.

**Iteration 6 — Visit node 6 (dist = 13).** Destination reached.

**Final Shortest Path:** $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6$, total weight = **13**.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute the **expected project duration and critical path** for the given project network. Activity durations (in days) are:

| Activity | 1-2 | 1-3 | 2-4 | 3-4 | 3-5 | 4-5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Duration | 5 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 6 | 1 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Forward Pass:**
- $ES(1) = 0$, $EF(1) = 0$
- Node 2: $ES(2) = EF(1) + d(1,2) = 0 + 5 = 5$, $EF(2) = 5$
- Node 3: $ES(3) = EF(1) + d(1,3) = 0 + 3 = 3$, $EF(3) = 3$
- Node 4: $ES(4) = \max(EF(2) + 4, EF(3) + 2) = \max(9, 5) = 9$, $EF(4) = 9$
- Node 5: $ES(5) = \max(EF(3) + 6, EF(4) + 1) = \max(9, 10) = 10$, $EF(5) = 10$

**Backward Pass (project end = 10):**
- $LF(5) = 10$, $LS(5) = 10$
- Node 4: $LF(4) = LS(5) - d(4,5) = 10 - 1 = 9$, $LS(4) = 9$
- Node 3: $LF(3) = \min(LS(4) - 2, LS(5) - 6) = \min(7, 4) = 4$, $LS(3) = 4$
- Node 2: $LF(2) = LS(4) - 4 = 9 - 4 = 5$, $LS(2) = 5$
- Node 1: $LF(1) = 0$

**Slack Calculation:**

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | Slack |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 ✓ Critical |
| 1-3 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 1 |
| 2-4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | 0 ✓ Critical |
| 3-4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 | 4 |
| 3-5 | 3 | 9 | 4 | 10 | 1 |
| 4-5 | 9 | 10 | 9 | 10 | 0 ✓ Critical |

**Critical Path:** **1 → 2 → 4 → 5**, Total Duration = **10 days**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Find the **maximum flow** from source **S** to sink **T** in the following network using the **Ford-Fulkerson algorithm**:

| Edge | S-A | S-B | A-B | A-T | B-T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity | 8 | 6 | 3 | 5 | 7 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1:** Path $S \to A \to T$, bottleneck = $\min(8, 5) = 5$.
- Flow: $f(S,A) = 5$, $f(A,T) = 5$. **Total = 5**.

**Iteration 2:** Path $S \to B \to T$, bottleneck = $\min(6, 7) = 6$.
- Flow: $f(S,B) = 6$, $f(B,T) = 6$. **Total = 11**.

**Iteration 3:** Path $S \to A \to B \to T$ (in residual graph, $S \to A$ has residual $8-5=3$).
- Bottleneck = $\min(3, 3, 7-6) = \min(3, 3, 1) = 1$.
- Flow: $f(S,A) = 6$, $f(A,B) = 1$, $f(B,T) = 7$. **Total = 12**.

**Iteration 4:** Residual capacity $S \to A = 8 - 6 = 2$, but $A \to B$ residual = $3 - 1 = 2$, $B \to T$ residual = $7 - 7 = 0$. **No augmenting path.**

**Maximum Flow = 12.**

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Construct a **Minimum Spanning Tree** for the following graph using **Kruskal's algorithm**:

| Edge | A-B | A-C | A-D | B-C | B-D | C-D | C-E | D-E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | 2 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Sort edges by weight.

| Sorted Edge | A-D | A-B | A-C | B-C | B-D | C-D | C-E | D-E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |

**Step 2:** Apply Kruskal's rule (add if no cycle).

| Step | Edge | Weight | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A-D | 1 | **Add** | A, D in different sets |
| 2 | A-B | 2 | **Add** | A in {A,D}, B separate |
| 3 | A-C | 3 | **Add** | C separate |
| 4 | B-C | 4 | **Reject** | Cycle: A-B-C-A |
| 5 | B-D | 5 | **Reject** | Cycle: A-B-D-A |
| 6 | C-D | 6 | **Reject** | Cycle: A-C-D-A |
| 7 | C-E | 7 | **Add** | E separate |
| 8 | D-E | 8 | **Reject** | Cycle: A-D-E-C-A |

**MST Edges:** {A-D, A-B, A-C, C-E}, Total Weight = $1 + 2 + 3 + 7 = \mathbf{13}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Dijkstra trap:** Students often forget to mark a node as "visited" and accidentally process it twice. Always maintain a clear visited/unvisited log table.
> 2. **Max-Flow conservation error:** Common mistake — at intermediate nodes, total inflow must equal total outflow. Recheck this after every augmentation.
> 3. **Critical Path = Longest, NOT Shortest:** Many students wrongly pick the shortest path through the network. The critical path is the **longest** path (it determines minimum project duration).
> 4. **PERT variance sum:** When multiple critical paths exist, sum the variances of activities on *all* critical paths. Students often only pick one.
> 5. **Kruskal's cycle check:** Always verify using Union-Find. Forgetting this check leads to incorrect MSTs.
> 6. **Slack computation:** $Slack = LS - ES$, NOT $LF - EF$ written alone. Show both for full credit.
> 7. **Forward vs Backward Pass:** $ES$ uses $\max$ of predecessors' $EF$; $LS$ uses $\min$ of successors' $LS$. Reverse the inequality direction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shortest Path:** Dijkstra = non-negative weights, $O((V+E) \log V)$. Bellman-Ford = handles negative weights.
- **Minimum Spanning Tree:** Greedy algorithms (Prim, Kruskal). MST has exactly $V-1$ edges and no cycles.
- **Maximum Flow:** Ford-Fulkerson / Edmonds-Karp uses **augmenting paths in residual graphs**. BFS guarantees polynomial time.
- **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:** $\max$ flow = $\min$ cut capacity. Always quote this in any max-flow answer.
- **CPM vs PERT:** CPM is deterministic (single duration per activity). PERT uses $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ and variance $\sigma^{2} = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^{2}$.
- **Critical Path:** Longest path through the network. Activities on it have **zero slack**.
- **Slack:** $LS - ES$. Zero slack = critical.
- **PERT probability:** $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma_{cp}$, use standard normal table.
- **Real-world:** MST for telecom/civil networks; SPP for routing; Max Flow for capacity; CPM/PERT for project management.
- **Algorithm choice:** Match the algorithm to the *graph properties* (neg weights, all-pairs, cycles, etc.) — this is the #1 KTU differentiator in 14-mark answers.
- **Notation:** Always label nodes, distances, and predecessor pointers clearly in tabular form for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulated Annealing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT758 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulated Annealing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a probabilistic, trajectory-based metaheuristic optimization technique designed to locate the global optimum of an objective function defined over a discrete (or continuous, discretized) search space. The method is mathematically modeled on the physical process of **annealing in metallurgy**, wherein a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to allow its atoms to settle into a low-energy, defect-free crystalline lattice (the global minimum energy configuration).

In optimization terminology, SA maps:
- **System state** ↔ Candidate solution $\mathbf{x}$ in the search space
- **Energy** $E$ ↔ Objective function value $f(\mathbf{x})$ to be minimized
- **Temperature** $T$ ↔ Control parameter governing the probability of accepting uphill moves
- **Cooling schedule** ↔ Annealing trajectory that gradually reduces $T \to 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SA belongs to the family of **nature-inspired metaheuristics** and is classified as a **local search method with stochastic hill-climbing capability**, allowing controlled escape from local minima — a key limitation of deterministic gradient-based techniques.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Glass Blower

Imagine a **glass blower** crafting a delicate vase. After shaping the glass at high temperature, the artisan allows it to cool very slowly inside a controlled oven.

- If the vase is cooled **rapidly** (quenched), internal stresses remain, leaving the glass brittle with hidden defects — analogous to a **local minimum** trap.
- If cooled **gradually** (annealed), atoms have enough thermal energy to rearrange into the most stable, stress-free configuration — analogous to reaching the **global minimum**.

In SA, the "temperature" $T$ represents how willing the algorithm is to temporarily accept a *worse* solution (an uphill move) in order to escape a local valley. As $T$ decreases, the algorithm behaves more like a greedy steepest-descent, converging on the best solution found.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Initial Value | Units |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------|-------|
| Initial Temperature | $T_0$ | $\mathbf{100}$ to $\mathbf{1000}$ | Dimensionless (energy units) |
| Cooling Coefficient | $\alpha$ | $\mathbf{0.90}$ to $\mathbf{0.99}$ | Unitless ratio |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.0}$ (often absorbed) | J/K (normalized to 1) |
| Equilibrium Iterations | $L$ | $\mathbf{100}$ to $\mathbf{1000}$ | Iterations per temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Annealing"?** The terminology comes from the statistical mechanics formulation by **Metropolis et al. (1953)**, later adapted for combinatorial optimization by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt & Vecchi (1983)** — the founding paper of SA as an optimization algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Reasoning — The Logical Flow

The Simulated Annealing algorithm operates on the following principle:

1. **Initialization**: Begin with an arbitrary feasible solution $\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}}$ and a high initial temperature $T_0$.
2. **Neighborhood Generation**: Produce a new candidate solution $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ by applying a small stochastic perturbation to $\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}}$.
3. **Energy (Objective) Evaluation**: Compute the change in objective function:
   $$\Delta E = f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) - f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}})$$
4. **Acceptance Decision** via the **Metropolis Criterion**:
   - If $\Delta E \leq 0$ (improvement), always accept $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$.
   - If $\Delta E > 0$ (worsening), accept $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ with probability:
   $$P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$
5. **Cooling Schedule**: Reduce the temperature $T$ according to a predefined cooling law.
6. **Termination**: Stop when $T \approx 0$ (frozen state), a fixed iteration count is reached, or no improvement is observed over several cycles.
7. **Return**: Output the best solution $\mathbf{x}_{\text{best}}$ observed across all iterations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Allow Worse Solutions?** Pure greedy descent converges to the *nearest* local minimum. By probabilistically accepting worse moves at high $T$, SA gains a global exploration capability. As $T \to 0$, the algorithm asymptotically behaves as a greedy descent — locking in the global optimum with probability 1 (under the **logarithmic cooling schedule**, proven by Geman & Geman, 1984).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Simulated Annealing Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | KTU Exam Use |
|---|-------------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $P = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$ | Metropolis acceptance probability (for $\Delta E > 0$) | Core acceptance test |
| 2 | $\Delta E = f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) - f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}})$ | Energy difference between neighbors | Required to compute $P$ |
| 3 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad \alpha \in (0,1)$ | **Geometric (multiplicative) cooling** | Most commonly tested schedule |
| 4 | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \Delta T$ | **Linear (additive) cooling** | Used when gradual descent needed |
| 5 | $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{\ln(k + 1)}$ | **Logarithmic cooling** | Guarantees convergence to global optimum |
| 6 | $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{1 + \beta \cdot k}$ | **Cauchy cooling** | Faster than logarithmic, slower than geometric |
| 7 | $P \geq u \sim U(0,1)$ | Decision rule using uniform random variate $u$ | Implementation detail |
| 8 | $E_{\text{avg}}(T) = \sum_i P_i(T) \cdot E_i$ | Equilibrium expected energy (Boltzmann distribution) | Theoretical background |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU valuation, the absolute value notation $\lvert \Delta E \rvert$ must be written using `\vert` or `\mid` in handwritten/typed answers to clearly indicate magnitude. The vertical bar is also reserved for tables — **never use it ambiguously in formulas**.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where is SA Applied?

- **VLSI Circuit Design**: Optimal placement of millions of transistors on a chip (Kirkpatrick's original 1983 application — IBM chip layout).
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)**: Routing optimization in logistics, robotics, and PCB drilling.
- **Job-Shop & Production Scheduling**: Sequencing $n$ jobs on $m$ machines.
- **Structural Engineering**: Truss weight minimization under stress constraints.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning**: Search over discrete architecture spaces.
- **Image Processing**: Image segmentation, noise filtering (simulated by statistical physics).

### 2.4 Convergence Properties

- **Geometric cooling**: $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$ — practically efficient but **no formal global convergence guarantee**.
- **Logarithmic cooling**: $T_k = T_0 / \log(k + 1)$ — **asymptotically convergent to global optimum**, but impractically slow for engineering use.
- **Trade-off**: Fast cooling (large $\alpha$ decrement) → faster runtime, risk of local minima. Slow cooling → near-global optimum, expensive runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently test whether students can *identify the cooling schedule* given a problem and *compute $T_k$ at a given iteration $k$*. Always state the schedule name and the resulting $T_k$ explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metropolis Acceptance Criterion

The Metropolis criterion arises from **statistical mechanics** and the Boltzmann probability distribution. The derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Boltzmann Distribution.**
For a physical system in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the probability of the system being in a state with energy $E_i$ is given by:

$$P(E_i) = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_i}{k_B T}\right)}{Z(T)}$$

where $Z(T) = \sum_{j} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_j}{k_B T}\right)$ is the **partition function** (normalization constant).

> *This expresses the fact that low-energy states are exponentially more probable, but high-energy states remain thermally accessible at high $T$.*

**Step 2 — Transition Probability Between Two States.**
Consider two states with energies $E_{\text{old}}$ and $E_{\text{new}}$. The ratio of their Boltzmann probabilities is:

$$\frac{P(E_{\text{new}})}{P(E_{\text{old}})} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}}}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$

> *Here we used the property that the partition function $Z(T)$ cancels out in the ratio — a critical simplification.*

**Step 3 — Metropolis Acceptance Rule.**
Define the acceptance probability for transitioning from state `old` to state `new` as:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = \min\!\left\{1, \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)\right\}$$

> *The $\min\{1, \cdot\}$ operator ensures:*
> - *If $\Delta E \leq 0$ (improvement), $\exp(-\Delta E/T) \geq 1$, so $P_{\text{accept}} = 1$ (always accept).*
> - *If $\Delta E > 0$ (worsening), $\exp(-\Delta E/T) < 1$, so $P_{\text{accept}} < 1$ (probabilistic acceptance).*

**Step 4 — Algorithm Decision Rule.**
Generate a uniform random number $u \sim U(0,1)$. Accept the new state if:

$$u \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$

> *This makes the Metropolis criterion implementable in any algorithmic framework.*

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $f(x) = (x - 4)^2 + 5$ over integer $x \in [0, 10]$ using Simulated Annealing with:
- Initial solution: $x_0 = 1$
- Initial temperature: $T_0 = 10$
- Cooling schedule: $T_{k+1} = 0.5 \cdot T_k$ (geometric, $\alpha = 0.5$)
- Neighbourhood: $x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{current}} \pm 1$ (random choice)

**Step-by-Step Execution Table:**

| Iter $k$ | $T_k$ | $x_{\text{current}}$ | $x_{\text{new}}$ | $f(x_{\text{cur}})$ | $f(x_{\text{new}})$ | $\Delta E$ | $\exp(-\Delta E/T)$ | $u$ | Accepted? |
|----------|-------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|---------------------|-----|-----------|
| 0 | 10.0 | 1 | 2 | 14 | 9 | $-5$ | — | — | ✓ Yes (improvement) |
| 1 | 5.00 | 2 | 3 | 9 | 6 | $-3$ | — | — | ✓ Yes |
| 2 | 2.50 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 5 | $-1$ | — | — | ✓ Yes |
| 3 | 1.25 | 4 | 5 | 5 | 6 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/1.25} = 0.4493$ | $0.30$ | ✓ Yes (probabilistic) |
| 4 | 0.625 | 5 | 4 | 6 | 5 | $-1$ | — | — | ✓ Yes |
| 5 | 0.3125 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 5 | $0$ | $1.000$ | — | ✓ Yes |

> *After sufficient iterations with $T \to 0$, the algorithm locks onto $x^* = 4$, yielding $f_{\min} = 5$.*

**Final Result:**
$$x^* = 4, \qquad f(x^*) = (4 - 4)^2 + 5 = 5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When solving numerical SA problems, you MUST show:
> 1. Explicit $\Delta E$ calculation at each step.
> 2. The exponential probability using the current $T$.
> 3. The decision (accept/reject) with reasoning.
> 4. The new $T_k$ from the cooling schedule for the next iteration.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Greedy Hill Descent | Simulated Annealing |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Accepts worse solutions? | ❌ Never | ✅ Probabilistically |
| Escapes local minima? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (at high $T$) |
| Global optimum guarantee? | ❌ No | ✅ Asymptotic (log cooling) |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| Parameter sensitivity | Low | High ($T_0$, $\alpha$, $L$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Simulated Annealing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> InitTemp["Set T0 and cooling schedule alpha"]
    InitTemp --> InitSol["Initialize current solution xCur and best solution xBest"]
    InitSol --> LoopCheck{"T greater than Tmin?"}
    LoopCheck -- No --> Output["Return xBest and f xBest"]
    LoopCheck -- Yes --> EqLoop["For L iterations at this T"]
    EqLoop --> GenNeigh["Generate neighbor xNew by perturbation"]
    GenNeigh --> CalcDelta["Compute delta E = f xNew minus f xCur"]
    CalcDelta --> DeltaTest{"delta E less than or equal to 0?"}
    DeltaTest -- Yes --> Accept["Accept xNew as xCur"]
    DeltaTest -- No --> Metropolis["Compute P = exp minus delta E divided by T"]
    Metropolis --> RandCheck{"u less than P? where u is uniform 0,1"}
    RandCheck -- Yes --> Accept
    RandCheck -- No --> Reject["Reject xNew, keep xCur"]
    Accept --> BestCheck["If f xCur less than f xBest, update xBest"]
    BestCheck --> DecrementT["T equals alpha times T"]
    Reject --> DecrementT
    DecrementT --> LoopCheck
    Output([End])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of SA Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        direction TB
        A1[Initial Solution x0]
        A2[Initial Temperature T0]
        A3[Cooling Rate alpha]
    end

    subgraph CoreEngine[SA Core Engine]
        direction TB
        B1[Neighborhood Generator]
        B2[Energy Evaluator f x]
        B3[Metropolis Acceptance Module]
        B4[Temperature Scheduler]
    end

    subgraph Memory
        direction TB
        C1[Current Solution xCur]
        C2[Best Solution xBest]
    end

    subgraph Output
        direction TB
        D1[Optimal xStar]
        D2[Optimal f xStar]
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> B4
    A3 --> B4

    C1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> B3

    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize: Set T0 and x0
    Initialize --> HighTemp: T is large
    HighTemp --> Explore: Accept uphill moves frequently
    Explore --> Equilibrate: Reach thermal equilibrium at current T
    Equilibrate --> CoolDown: T equals alpha times T
    CoolDown --> LowTemp: T approaches 0
    LowTemp --> Exploit: Reject uphill moves almost always
    Exploit --> Converge: Lock into xBest
    Converge --> [*]: Return xStar and f xStar
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The algorithm passes through three behavioral phases:
> 1. **Exploration Phase** (high $T$): broad search, accepts most moves.
> 2. **Balanced Phase** (medium $T$): selective uphill acceptance.
> 3. **Exploitation Phase** (low $T$): fine-tuning near optimum.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Simulated Annealing. State the Metropolis acceptance criterion and explain its role in the algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Simulated Annealing (SA) is a **probabilistic metaheuristic** for global optimization, inspired by the physical annealing process in metallurgy. It is used to find the global minimum of an objective function by allowing controlled acceptance of worse solutions to escape local minima.

The **Metropolis acceptance criterion** governs whether to accept a new solution $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ generated from the current solution $\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}}$. It is given by:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \Delta E \leq 0 \\[4pt] \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right), & \text{if } \Delta E > 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\Delta E = f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) - f(\mathbf{x}_{\text{current}})$ and $T$ is the current temperature.

**Role in the algorithm:**
- When $\Delta E \leq 0$, the new solution is better and is always accepted (downhill move).
- When $\Delta E > 0$, the new solution is worse, but is accepted with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / k_B T)$, enabling escape from local minima.
- As $T \to 0$, the acceptance of uphill moves becomes vanishingly small, and the algorithm converges to a local/global optimum.

*(1 mark — Definition; 1 mark — Criterion; 1 mark — Role explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three cooling schedules used in Simulated Annealing. Write the formula for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three standard cooling schedules in SA are:

| Schedule | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| **Geometric (Multiplicative)** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad \alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| **Linear (Additive)** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \Delta T$ |
| **Logarithmic** | $T_k = \dfrac{T_0}{\ln(k+1)}$ |

*Geometric is widely used due to its simplicity; logarithmic provides theoretical convergence to the global optimum but is slow.*

*(1 mark per schedule with formula — 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **physical analogy** of Simulated Annealing with the metallurgical process of annealing. Discuss how the energy, temperature, and equilibrium concepts map to the optimization domain. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Metropolis criterion** with its mathematical derivation from the Boltzmann distribution. Show how the acceptance probability $P = \exp(-\Delta E / k_B T)$ emerges from statistical mechanics. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Analogy [7 Marks]:**

In **metallurgical annealing**, a metal (e.g., steel) is:
1. Heated to a very high temperature, allowing atoms to move freely and randomly — high internal energy state.
2. Held at the high temperature long enough for the system to reach **thermal equilibrium** — atoms arrange into a high-entropy, disordered state.
3. Cooled **extremely slowly** (annealed) so that atoms have time to settle into a **low-energy, ordered crystalline lattice** — minimum free energy configuration.

If cooled too quickly (quenching), the atoms get trapped in a **metastable, defect-ridden structure** with internal stresses.

**Mapping to Optimization:**

| Physical Process | Optimization Equivalent |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| Material system state | Candidate solution $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Internal energy $E$ | Objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Temperature $T$ | Control parameter (initially high) |
| Thermal equilibrium | Stable probability distribution at constant $T$ |
| Slow cooling | Decreasing $T$ schedule |
| Crystalline lattice | Global minimum $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ |
| Quenching (rapid cooling) | Greedy local search — trapped in local minimum |

At high $T$, the algorithm explores broadly, accepting worsening moves — analogous to atoms freely moving. As $T$ decreases, the system "freezes" into a minimum energy state — the global optimum.

*(2 marks — Annealing process description; 2 marks — Mapping table; 2 marks — High T exploration, Low T exploitation; 1 mark — Concluding remark)*

**(b) Metropolis Criterion Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The Metropolis criterion is rooted in the **Boltzmann probability distribution** of statistical mechanics. The derivation proceeds in the following steps:

**Step 1:** For a system in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$, the probability of being in a state $i$ with energy $E_i$ is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$P(E_i) = \frac{1}{Z(T)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $Z(T) = \sum_j \exp(-E_j / k_B T)$ is the **partition function**.

**Step 2:** Consider two states, the current state with energy $E_{\text{old}}$ and the proposed new state with energy $E_{\text{new}}$. The ratio of their probabilities is:

$$\frac{P(E_{\text{new}})}{P(E_{\text{old}})} = \frac{\exp(-E_{\text{new}}/k_B T)}{\exp(-E_{\text{old}}/k_B T)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}}}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Defining the energy difference $\Delta E = E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}}$, the ratio simplifies to:

$$\frac{P(E_{\text{new}})}{P(E_{\text{old}})} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The Metropolis algorithm defines the transition probability as:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = \min\!\left\{1, \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)\right\}$$

**Step 5:** For algorithmic implementation, generate a uniform random number $u \sim U(0,1)$ and accept if:

$$u \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\Delta E \leq 0$, then $\exp(-\Delta E / k_B T) \geq 1$, so $P_{\text{accept}} = 1$ — always accept improving moves.
- If $\Delta E > 0$, the probability is strictly less than 1 — worse moves accepted with decreasing likelihood as $T$ decreases.

This ensures that the system spends most time in low-energy states (solutions) but occasionally visits higher-energy states at high $T$, enabling global exploration.

*(2 marks — Boltzmann distribution; 2 marks — Ratio derivation; 2 marks — Metropolis formula; 1 mark — Implementation rule)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step algorithm** of Simulated Annealing for minimizing $f(\mathbf{x})$. Clearly define the neighbourhood generation, acceptance criterion, and cooling schedule. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Solve the following minimization problem using Simulated Annealing:

Minimize $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 15$ for integer $x \in [0, 8]$.

Use: $x_0 = 0$, $T_0 = 5$, geometric cooling $\alpha = 0.5$, neighbourhood $x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{current}} \pm 1$. Apply uniform random variates $u \in \{0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.8, 0.3\}$ at each step as needed. Perform 6 iterations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Algorithm [7 Marks]:**

```
ALGORITHM: Simulated Annealing
INPUT:    Initial solution x0, initial temperature T0, 
          cooling rate alpha, minimum temperature Tmin
OUTPUT:   Approximate global minimum xStar and f(xStar)

STEP 1:   Initialize xCur ← x0, xBest ← x0
          Compute f(xCur), f(xBest)
          Set T ← T0

STEP 2:   WHILE (T > Tmin) DO
            REPEAT L times (for thermal equilibrium):
              STEP 2.1: Generate neighbour xNew from xCur
                       (small stochastic perturbation)
              STEP 2.2: Compute deltaE = f(xNew) - f(xCur)
              STEP 2.3: IF deltaE ≤ 0 THEN
                          xCur ← xNew
                        ELSE
                          Generate u ~ U(0,1)
                          IF u ≤ exp(-deltaE/T) THEN
                            xCur ← xNew
                          END IF
                        END IF
              STEP 2.4: IF f(xCur) < f(xBest) THEN
                          xBest ← xCur
                        END IF
            END REPEAT
            STEP 2.5: T ← alpha × T    (cooling)
          END WHILE

STEP 3:   RETURN xStar = xBest, f(xStar) = f(xBest)
```

**Key components:**
- **Neighbourhood**: $N(\mathbf{x})$ — set of solutions reachable by a single perturbation (e.g., $\pm 1$ in 1-D, bit-flip in binary).
- **Acceptance criterion**: Metropolis rule — always accept improvements, probabilistically accept worsening moves.
- **Cooling schedule**: Rule for $T_k \to T_{k+1}$; commonly geometric: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$.

*(2 marks — Algorithm structure; 2 marks — Neighbourhood definition; 2 marks — Acceptance logic; 1 mark — Termination and cooling)*

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

Given: $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 15 = (x - 3)^2 + 6$, so optimal $x^* = 3$, $f_{\min} = 6$.

Pre-computed function values:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(x)$ | 15 | 10 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 10 | 15 | 22 | 31 |

**Iteration Table:**

| Iter | $T_k$ | $x_{\text{cur}}$ | $x_{\text{new}}$ | $f(x_{\text{cur}})$ | $f(x_{\text{new}})$ | $\Delta E$ | $\exp(-\Delta E/T)$ | $u$ | Accept? | $x_{\text{best}}$ |
|------|-------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|---------------------|-----|---------|------------------|
| 1 | 5.0 | 0 | 1 | 15 | 10 | $-5$ | — | — | ✓ Yes | 1 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 1 | 2 | 10 | 7 | $-3$ | — | — | ✓ Yes | 2 |
| 3 | 1.25 | 2 | 3 | 7 | 6 | $-1$ | — | — | ✓ Yes | 3 |
| 4 | 0.625 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 7 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/0.625} = 0.2019$ | $0.4$ | ✗ No | 3 |
| 5 | 0.3125 | 3 | 2 | 6 | 7 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/0.3125} = 0.0408$ | $0.7$ | ✗ No | 3 |
| 6 | 0.15625 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 7 | $+1$ | $e^{-1/0.15625} = 0.0017$ | $0.8$ | ✗ No | 3 |

**Final Result:**
$$x^* = 3, \qquad f(x^*) = 6$$

> *Note: Once $T$ drops sufficiently low, the algorithm refuses to leave the minimum even when perturbed — characteristic of SA's exploitation phase.*

*(2 marks — Function table; 3 marks — Iteration table with all columns; 1 mark — Decision logic with explicit comparison $u \leq \exp$; 1 mark — Final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update $T$**: After every "equilibrium loop," students often forget to apply the cooling rule $T \leftarrow \alpha T$. Always show the new $T$ before the next iteration.
> 2. **Wrong sign in $\Delta E$**: $\Delta E = f(x_{\text{new}}) - f(x_{\text{current}})$, **not** the reverse. Wrong sign → wrong probability → wrong decision.
> 3. **Confusing acceptance direction**: The exponential $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ applies **only** when $\Delta E > 0$. For $\Delta E \leq 0$, the answer is always "accept" — no calculation needed.
> 4. **Missing uniform random comparison**: The acceptance must use $u \leq \exp(-\Delta E / T)$, where $u$ is drawn from $U(0,1)$. Skipping the comparison step loses marks.
> 5. **Not stating the cooling schedule name**: Examiners expect students to *name* the schedule (geometric, linear, logarithmic) along with the formula.
> 6. **Stopping too early**: SA must be run until $T < T_{\min}$ or a max iteration count. Stopping after one pass = incomplete solution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Simulated Annealing**

- **Origin**: Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, Vecchi (1983) — adapted from Metropolis et al. (1953) statistical mechanics simulation.
- **Classification**: Probabilistic, trajectory-based metaheuristic; local search with stochastic hill-climbing.
- **Core Equation** (acceptance of worsening moves):
$$P_{\text{accept}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right), \quad \Delta E > 0$$
- **Always Accept Rule**: When $\Delta E \leq 0$, no probability check needed — the move is always accepted.
- **Cooling Schedule (Geometric — most common)**:
$$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad \alpha \in (0,1)$$
- **Cooling Schedule (Logarithmic — convergence guarantee)**:
$$T_k = \frac{T_0}{\ln(k+1)}$$
- **Three Phases**: Exploration ($T$ high) → Balanced → Exploitation ($T \approx 0$).
- **Convergence**: Asymptotic to global optimum **only** under logarithmic cooling (Geman & Geman, 1984).
- **Algorithm Inputs**: $x_0$, $T_0$, $\alpha$, $L$ (inner loop length), $T_{\min}$, neighbourhood structure $N(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Algorithm Output**: $\mathbf{x}_{\text{best}}$ (best solution observed across all iterations, not just the final one).
- **Comparison with Greedy Descent**: SA accepts worse moves probabilistically → escapes local minima.
- **Comparison with Genetic Algorithms**: SA uses **single-solution trajectory**, GA uses **population-based search**.
- **Key Advantages**: Simple to implement, problem-independent, handles discrete/continuous, no gradient needed.
- **Key Disadvantages**: Slow convergence, sensitive to $T_0$, $\alpha$, $L$ tuning, no convergence guarantee with fast cooling.
- **Real-World Applications**: VLSI design, TSP, job-shop scheduling, image segmentation, neural network training, protein folding.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"SA is a hot-to-cold journey — at high $T$ it explores recklessly, at low $T$ it settles greedily into the global valley."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are "Principles" in Optimization Techniques?

In the context of KTU PEFTT758 – Optimization Techniques (Module 4), the term **"Principles"** refers to the *foundational rules, conditions, and governing theorems* that decide whether a candidate solution is optimal, feasible, and computationally reachable. Optimization is not merely a "trial-and-error" search; it rests upon rigorous mathematical principles such as **optimality, convexity, duality, and convergence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Optimization principles* are the set of mathematical conditions and logical rules (necessary, sufficient, and Kuhn-Tucker conditions) that govern the existence, characterization, and computation of the optimum of an objective function subject to constraints.

In simpler words, **principles are the "rules of the game"** that any optimization algorithm (whether Linear, Non-Linear, Dynamic, or Metaheuristic) must respect to guarantee that the answer obtained is genuinely the best (or at least a good enough) solution.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Mountain Climber's Rulebook

Imagine you are standing somewhere on a hilly terrain (the **search space**) in thick fog, and your goal is to reach the **lowest valley** (the **optimum/minimum**). You cannot see far. The "Principles" are the rules that tell you:

| Real-World Clue | Optimization Principle |
|---|---|
| The ground tilts downward in every direction | **Negative Gradient** → Descent Direction |
| A wall blocks your path downhill | **Active Constraint** (Boundary) |
| A flat plateau with no slope | **Stationary Point / Saddle Point** |
| The valley is bowl-shaped | **Convexity Principle** |
| Two different paths lead to the same valley | **Duality Principle** |

So **Principles = the compass, the map legend, and the physical laws** of the optimization landscape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Principles" is a High-Yield Module 4 Topic in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus structures Module 4 around advanced (often non-linear / metaheuristic) methods. Without mastering the underlying *principles*, students cannot justify algorithm choice, check feasibility, or interpret Kuhn-Tucker conditions — all of which are routine 7-to-14 mark questions in ESE.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Optimization Principles

The principles can be grouped into **four pillars** (these are tested frequently):

1. **Optimality Principles** – Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions, Fritz John conditions, Weierstrass theorem.
2. **Convexity Principles** – Convex sets, convex functions, global vs. local optima.
3. **Duality Principles** – Primal-Dual relationship, weak and strong duality theorems.
4. **Convergence & Search Principles** – Convergence criteria, descent directions, step-length rules (Armijo, Wolfe), stopping criteria.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **"O-C-D-C"** → **O**ptimality, **C**onvexity, **D**uality, **C**onvergence. These four principles appear in nearly every KTU Module 4 question.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Quantities Used

The following scalar quantities are fundamental to *every* principle:

- **$f(\mathbf{x})$** – Objective function to be minimized (or maximized).
- **$\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$** – Decision variable vector.
- **$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$** – Inequality constraints ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$).
- **$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$** – Equality constraints ($j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$).
- **$\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$** – Gradient (first-order information).
- **$\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})$** – Hessian matrix (second-order information).
- **$\lambda_i \ge 0$** – Lagrange / KKT multipliers for inequality constraints.
- **$\mu_j$** – Unrestricted multipliers for equality constraints.
- **$\mathbf{x}^\*$** – Optimal point.
- **$f(\mathbf{x}^\*)$** – Optimal objective value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention in KTU Boards:** *Minimize* is the default. Equality constraints use **$\mu$**, inequality constraints use **$\lambda \ge 0$**. Always write the Lagrangian form first, then derive conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillar I — The Optimality Principles

### 2.1.1 Weierstrass Extreme Value Theorem (Existence)

> A continuous function $f: \mathbf{S} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on a **non-empty, compact** (closed and bounded) set $\mathbf{S} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ **always attains** a global minimum and a global maximum.

**Why this matters:** Before *solving* an optimization problem, you must ensure the optimum *exists*. If the feasible region is unbounded, a global minimum may not exist (e.g., minimizing $f(x) = e^{-x}$ over $x \in \mathbb{R}$).

### 2.1.2 First-Order Necessary Condition (Unconstrained)

If $f$ is continuously differentiable and $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is a local minimizer, then:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

This is the **stationary point condition**. A point with $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ is called a **stationary point** — it could be a minimum, maximum, or saddle point.

### 2.1.3 Second-Order Necessary & Sufficient Conditions (Unconstrained)

| Type | Condition | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| **2nd-Order Necessary** | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\*)=\mathbf{0}$ and $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\*)$ is **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is a local minimizer |
| **2nd-Order Sufficient** | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\*)=\mathbf{0}$ and $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\*)$ is **Positive Definite (PD)** | $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is a **strict** local minimizer |

**Definiteness Tests:**
- All leading principal minors of $H$ are **positive** $\Rightarrow$ **Positive Definite**.
- Eigenvalues $\lambda_i > 0 \ \forall i$ $\Rightarrow$ **Positive Definite**.
- $\mathbf{x}^\top H \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \ne \mathbf{0}$ $\Rightarrow$ **Positive Definite**.

### 2.1.4 Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions — The Crown Jewel

For the problem:
$$\min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \ h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

The **KKT necessary conditions** state that at the optimum $\mathbf{x}^\*$, there exist multipliers $\lambda_i^\* \ge 0$ and $\mu_j^\*$ such that:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(Stationarity)} &\quad \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0} \\[4pt]
\text{(Primal Feasibility)} &\quad g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{(Dual Feasibility)} &\quad \lambda_i^* \ge 0 \\[4pt]
\text{(Complementary Slackness)} &\quad \lambda_i^* \cdot g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary Slackness** is the *most-tested* KKT condition. Its physical meaning: *"If a constraint is not active (strictly satisfied), its multiplier is zero; if its multiplier is non-zero, the constraint is exactly active (binding)."*

### 2.1.5 Fritz John Conditions (Weaker Form)

When **no constraint qualification (CQ)** like LICQ holds, only **Fritz John conditions** are guaranteed — a multiplier $\lambda_0 \ge 0$ is added to $\nabla f$:

$$\lambda_0 \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = \mathbf{0}, \quad \lambda_0, \lambda_i \ge 0, \quad (\lambda_0, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) \ne \mathbf{0}$$

If a CQ holds, $\lambda_0$ can be normalized to 1, recovering the KKT conditions.

---

## 2.2 Pillar II — The Convexity Principles

### 2.2.1 Convex Set
A set $\mathbf{S}$ is **convex** if for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbf{S}$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:

$$\theta \mathbf{x} + (1-\theta) \mathbf{y} \in \mathbf{S}$$

### 2.2.2 Convex Function
$f$ is **convex** on $\mathbf{S}$ if for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbf{S}$ and $\theta \in [0,1]$:

$$f(\theta \mathbf{x} + (1-\theta) \mathbf{y}) \le \theta f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\theta) f(\mathbf{y})$$

### 2.2.3 The Golden Theorem of Convex Optimization

> *If $f$ is convex and the feasible region is a convex set, **every local minimum is a global minimum**.*

For differentiable $f$, convexity is equivalent to:

$$f(\mathbf{y}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$$

**Tests for Convexity (twice-differentiable case):**
- $f$ is convex $\iff$ $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})$ is **PSD** for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{S}$.
- $f$ is **strictly convex** $\iff$ $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})$ is **PD** for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{S}$.

---

## 2.3 Pillar III — The Duality Principles

For every optimization problem (**primal**), there is a companion (**dual**) problem constructed via the **Lagrangian function**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

The **dual function** is:

$$\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

The **dual problem** is:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\lambda} \ge 0, \, \boldsymbol{\mu}} \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

### 2.3.1 Weak Duality
For any primal feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual feasible $(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \ge \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

So the dual gives a **lower bound** (for minimization) on the primal optimum.

### 2.3.2 Strong Duality
If a **constraint qualification** holds (e.g., Slater's condition for convex problems), the optimal values are equal:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, \boldsymbol{\mu}^*)$$

This is the theoretical guarantee behind algorithms like the Simplex method's dual interpretation, SDP solvers, and interior-point methods.

### 2.3.3 Duality Gap
$$\text{Gap} = f(\mathbf{x}^*) - \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^*, \boldsymbol{\mu}^*)$$

Zero gap $\Rightarrow$ **Strong Duality**. Practical solvers report this gap as a stopping criterion.

---

## 2.4 Pillar IV — The Convergence & Search Principles

Any iterative optimization algorithm generates a sequence $\{\mathbf{x}_k\}$. Convergence principles answer: *"When do we stop?"*

### 2.4.1 Descent Direction Principle
A direction $\mathbf{d}_k$ is a **descent direction** at $\mathbf{x}_k$ if there exists $\bar{\alpha} > 0$ such that:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) < f(\mathbf{x}_k) \quad \forall \alpha \in (0, \bar{\alpha})$$

Equivalently, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top \mathbf{d}_k < 0$ (the **angle** between gradient and direction is obtuse).

### 2.4.2 Step-Length (Line Search) Principles

| Rule | Condition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Exact Line Search** | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha>0} f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ | Theoretical optimum step |
| **Armijo Rule** | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha \nabla f_k^\top \mathbf{d}_k$ | Sufficient decrease |
| **Wolfe Conditions** | Armijo + $ \mid \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^\top \mathbf{d}_k \mid \le c_2 \mid \nabla f_k^\top \mathbf{d}_k \mid$ | Curvature condition |

### 2.4.3 Stopping Criteria
1. **Gradient norm:** $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \le \epsilon_1$
2. **Function change:** $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \le \epsilon_2$
3. **Step size:** $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert \le \epsilon_3$
4. **Max iterations:** $k \ge K_{\max}$

### 2.4.4 Rate of Convergence
If $\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^p} = \mu$:

- $p = 1, \, 0 < \mu < 1$ $\Rightarrow$ **Linear** (e.g., Gradient Descent)
- $p = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ **Quadratic** (e.g., Newton's method)
- $p > 1$ (not 2) $\Rightarrow$ **Superlinear** (e.g., Quasi-Newton / BFGS)

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Principle | Key Formula / Condition | When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weierstrass (Existence) | $f$ continuous on compact set $\Rightarrow \exists$ global min/max | Verify existence first |
| 2 | FONC (Unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for interior optimum |
| 3 | SONC (Unconstrained) | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ PSD | 2nd-order necessary |
| 4 | SOSC (Unconstrained) | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ PD | 2nd-order sufficient |
| 5 | KKT Stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j = 0$ | Constrained smooth problems |
| 6 | Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Active/inactive constraint check |
| 7 | Convexity Test | $H = \nabla^2 f$ PSD $\iff f$ convex | Verify global minimum |
| 8 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | Foundation for KKT & Duality |
| 9 | Dual Function | $\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}$ | Construct dual problem |
| 10 | Weak Duality | $f(\mathbf{x}) \ge \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$ | Always true |
| 11 | Strong Duality | $f^* = \phi^*$ under Slater's CQ | Convex problems |
| 12 | Descent Direction | $\nabla f^\top \mathbf{d} < 0$ | Direction validity test |
| 13 | Armijo Condition | $f(\mathbf{x}+\alpha \mathbf{d}) \le f(\mathbf{x}) + c_1 \alpha \nabla f^\top \mathbf{d}$ | Sufficient decrease in line search |
| 14 | Wolfe Curvature | $\mid \nabla f(\mathbf{x}+\alpha \mathbf{d})^\top \mathbf{d} \mid \le c_2 \mid \nabla f^\top \mathbf{d} \mid$ | Curvature in line search |
| 15 | Newton's Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -H^{-1} \nabla f$ | Quadratic convergence |
| 16 | Gradient Descent | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Linear convergence |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (KTU Boards):** The vertical bar `|` is reserved for **table column separators** in KTU answer sheets. For absolute values or norms, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ to keep handwriting clear and avoid grading confusion.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Principles

| Engineering Field | Application of Optimization Principles |
|---|---|
| **Structural / Civil Engineering** | Minimum weight truss design — KKT conditions identify active stress constraints |
| **Mechanical Design** | Optimal gear ratios, thermal systems — convexity ensures global optimum |
| **Electrical Power Systems** | Economic dispatch — duality gives shadow prices (locational marginal prices) |
| **Aerospace / Control** | Trajectory optimization — Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (continuous-time KKT) |
| **Machine Learning / AI** | Loss minimization — SGD uses descent & Armijo principles |
| **Manufacturing / OR** | Production planning — strong duality underpins Simplex sensitivity analysis |
| **Signal Processing** | Filter design — convex optimization (LASSO, SVM) guarantees global solution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern solvers like **Gurobi, CPLEX, Mosek, IPOPT** internally use *every* principle listed above. Engineers who understand these principles can debug solver failures, choose correct formulations, and interpret solver output (dual values, KKT error, infeasibility certificates).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: KKT Conditions from First Principles

### Setup
Consider the problem:
$$\min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \ i = 1, \ldots, m$$

with $f, g_i$ continuously differentiable.

### Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$$

**Why a Lagrangian?** It "absorbs" the constraints into the objective so that unconstrained calculus can be applied. The multipliers $\lambda_i$ act as **penalty prices** for violating each constraint.

### Step 2 — Define the Lagrangian Dual Function
$$\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) = \inf_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda})$$

For any $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \ge 0$ and any feasible $\mathbf{x}$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le f(\mathbf{x}) \quad (\text{since } g_i \le 0, \lambda_i \ge 0)$$

Taking the infimum over $\mathbf{x}$:
$$\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \le f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \text{ feasible } \mathbf{x}$$

So $\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \le f(\mathbf{x}^*) = f^*$. This proves **Weak Duality** (Pillar III).

### Step 3 — Stationarity of Lagrangian
For the **interior** optimum (no active constraints), $\mathcal{L}$ must be stationary w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$$

This is the **KKT Stationarity Condition**.

### Step 4 — Complementary Slackness
If constraint $g_k$ is **inactive** ($g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$), the optimum should not depend on $\lambda_k$, so $\lambda_k = 0$.
If $\lambda_k > 0$, the constraint must be **active**: $g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$.

In either case:
$$\lambda_k \cdot g_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \quad \forall k$$

### Step 5 — Dual Feasibility
The multiplier for an inequality constraint cannot be negative (else the Lagrangian's penalty would reward constraint violation). Hence:
$$\lambda_i \ge 0 \quad \forall i$$

### Step 6 — Primal Feasibility
The optimum must satisfy the original constraints:
$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0 \quad \forall i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The four KKT conditions (Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness) are the *necessary* conditions for $\mathbf{x}^\*$ to be a local optimum (under a suitable constraint qualification). For **convex** problems, they are also **sufficient**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: KKT Application

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x - 2)^2$ subject to $g(x) = x - 1 \le 0$.

**Step 1 — Identify the unconstrained minimizer:**
$\frac{df}{dx} = 2(x-2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$. But $g(2) = 1 > 0$, so $x=2$ is **infeasible**.

**Step 2 — Check constraint boundary:**
The feasible region is $x \le 1$. The closest feasible point to $x=2$ is $x=1$.

**Step 3 — Apply KKT:**
- Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = (x-2)^2 + \lambda(x-1)$
- Stationarity: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2(x-2) + \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2 - \frac{\lambda}{2}$
- Complementary Slackness: $\lambda (x-1) = 0$

**Case A:** $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$, but $g(2) = 1 > 0$ → infeasible. Reject.
**Case B:** $x = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2(1-2)(-1) = 2$. Since $\lambda = 2 \ge 0$ ✓ and $g(1) = 0$ ✓.

**Optimal Solution:** $x^* = 1$, $f^* = (1-2)^2 = \mathbf{1}$, $\lambda^* = 2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of $\lambda^* = 2$:** The shadow price. If the constraint is relaxed to $x \le 1 + \delta$, the optimal objective decreases by approximately $2\delta$ for small $\delta$. This is a direct KTU exam interpretation question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Newton's Method Direction (Convergence Principle)

**Taylor expansion** of $f$ around $\mathbf{x}_k$:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{d}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f_k^\top \mathbf{d} + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^\top H_k \mathbf{d}$$

**Differentiate** w.r.t. $\mathbf{d}$ and set to zero:
$$\nabla f_k + H_k \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{d}_k = -H_k^{-1} \nabla f_k$$

This is the **Newton direction**. Plugging back:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{d}_k) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \tfrac{1}{2} \nabla f_k^\top H_k^{-1} \nabla f_k$$

Since $H_k$ is PD, the second-order term is **negative** $\Rightarrow$ guaranteed descent.

**Convergence rate:** For $f \in C^3$ near $\mathbf{x}^\*$ with PD Hessian:
$$\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert \le C \Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert^2$$

This is **quadratic convergence** — the number of correct digits approximately doubles each iteration.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Convexity from Hessian Positive Definiteness

**Statement:** If $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite) for all $\mathbf{x}$, then $f$ is convex.

**Proof sketch using second-order Taylor expansion:**
$$f(\mathbf{y}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x}) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x})^\top \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{z}) (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x})$$

where $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x} + \theta(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x})$, $\theta \in (0,1)$. Since $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{z}) \succeq 0$:
$$f(\mathbf{y}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^\top (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{x})$$

This is the **first-order convexity condition**. It says the function lies *above* all its tangent hyperplanes. ∎

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Principle Do I Use?"

| Situation in Problem | Recommended Principle | Why |
|---|---|---|
| No constraints, smooth $f$ | FONC / SONC | Standard calculus suffices |
| Box / linear constraints | KKT | Multipliers give shadow prices |
| Equality-only constraints | Lagrange Multipliers | Special case of KKT with $\mu$ |
| Constraint qualification fails | Fritz John | Weaker, always valid |
| Need global guarantee | Convexity + Slater | Convex problems = global optima |
| Iterative algorithm design | Descent + Armijo + Wolfe | Ensures convergence |
| Comparing algorithms | Rate of Convergence | Linear / superlinear / quadratic |
| Checking solver output | Duality Gap | Zero gap $\Rightarrow$ optimum reached |
| Verifying $f$ shape | Hessian Definiteness | Eigenvalues / leading minors test |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** State the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) necessary conditions for the optimization problem: minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

**Model Answer:**
The KKT conditions state that at the optimal point $\mathbf{x}^\*$, there exist multipliers $\lambda_i^\* \ge 0$ and $\mu_j^\*$ such that the following four conditions hold:
1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\*) + \sum \lambda_i^\* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^\*) + \sum \mu_j^\* \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^\*) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{x}^\*) \le 0, \ h_j(\mathbf{x}^\*) = 0$
3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\lambda_i^\* \ge 0$
4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\lambda_i^\* g_i(\mathbf{x}^\*) = 0$

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Distinguish between local and global optima. State the theorem relating convexity to global optimality.

**Model Answer:**
A point $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is a **local minimum** if $f(\mathbf{x}^\*) \le f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ in a small neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^\*$. It is a **global minimum** if the inequality holds for **all feasible** $\mathbf{x}$. The **theorem of convex optimization** states: *If $f$ is convex and the feasible region is a convex set, then every local minimum of $f$ is also a global minimum.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q-B1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KKT Comprehensive Problem**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the necessary KKT conditions for the problem: minimize $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $i = 1, \ldots, m$, assuming differentiability and a suitable constraint qualification.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Apply KKT to solve: Minimize $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$ subject to $x + y \le 5$, $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$. Find the optimal point and interpret the multipliers.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof Sketch:**
1. Form Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$.
2. At the optimum, the Lagrangian must be stationary w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$ → Stationarity condition.
3. Multipliers must be non-negative so that constraint violation is penalized (not rewarded) → Dual feasibility.
4. If a constraint is not active, its multiplier must be zero to not affect stationarity → Complementary slackness.
5. Optimum must satisfy original constraints → Primal feasibility.

Formally, using a perturbation argument (Gordan's theorem of alternatives or Farkas' lemma) under a constraint qualification, the KKT conditions are *necessary*. For convex problems, they are also *sufficient*. ∎

**(b) Solution:**

Rewrite: $f(x,y) = (x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2$. The unconstrained minimum is $(2,3)$ with $f = 0$. Check feasibility: $2+3 = 5 \le 5$ ✓, $x, y \ge 0$ ✓. So $(2,3)$ is feasible.

Since $f$ is **convex** (Hessian = $2I$ is PD), KKT are sufficient for global optimum.

KKT stationarity:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2(x-2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2(y-3) = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3$$

At $(2,3)$: $g_1(x,y) = x+y-5 = 0$ (active), $g_2 = -x = -2$ (inactive), $g_3 = -y = -3$ (inactive).

By complementary slackness, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$. Let $\lambda_1$ be the multiplier for $x+y \le 5$.

Stationarity with active constraints:
$$2(x-2) + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 0$$
$$2(y-3) + \lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 0$$

Hence the unconstrained minimum is feasible and is the **global optimum** with $f^* = 0$, $\lambda_1 = 0$ (constraint not "tight" in a sensitivity sense — already at vertex).

**Optimal Solution:** $(x^*, y^*) = (2, 3)$, $f^* = 0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Duality & Convexity**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the Lagrangian dual function and the dual problem. State and explain the weak and strong duality theorems.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Verify whether $f(x, y) = x^2 - 2xy + 3y^2 - 4x + 2y$ is convex on $\mathbb{R}^2$. If convex, determine the global minimum using the gradient condition.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Definition of Dual:* The Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$. The **dual function** is $\phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}$. The **dual problem** is $\max \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$ subject to $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \ge 0$.

*Weak Duality:* For any primal feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual feasible $(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$: $f(\mathbf{x}) \ge \phi(\boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$. Hence $f^* \ge \phi^*$.

*Strong Duality:* If a **constraint qualification** holds (e.g., Slater's condition: there exists a strictly feasible point for convex problems), then $f^* = \phi^*$ and the duality gap is zero.

**(b)** *Convexity Test:* The Hessian of $f$ is:
$$H = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$$

Principal minors:
- $H_{11} = 2 > 0$
- $\det(H) = 2 \cdot 6 - (-2)(-2) = 12 - 4 = 8 > 0$

Both leading principal minors positive $\Rightarrow$ $H$ is **Positive Definite** $\Rightarrow$ $f$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}^2$.

*Global minimum:* Since $f$ is strictly convex, the stationary point is the unique global minimum.

$$\nabla f = \begin{pmatrix} 2x - 2y - 4 \\ -2x + 6y + 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

From the first equation: $2x - 2y = 4 \Rightarrow x = y + 2$.
Substitute into the second: $-2(y+2) + 6y + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow 4y - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0.5$.
Then $x = 2.5$.

**Optimal point:** $(x^*, y^*) = (2.5, 0.5)$, $f^* = (2.5)^2 - 2(2.5)(0.5) + 3(0.5)^2 - 4(2.5) + 2(0.5) = 6.25 - 2.5 + 0.75 - 10 + 1 = \mathbf{-4.5}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Complementary Slackness:** This is the single most-skipped KKT condition. Always list all four explicitly. *Penalty: 1–2 marks per missing condition.*
> 2. **Confusing PSD vs. PD:** Writing "positive definite" when only "positive semi-definite" applies. PSD = necessary; PD = sufficient. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Ignoring Constraint Qualification:** KKT are *necessary* under CQ. Without CQ, only Fritz John holds. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 4. **Mistaking Weak Duality for Strong:** Weak duality is **always** true; strong duality requires CQ. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
> 5. **Not Verifying Feasibility:** Solving $\nabla f = 0$ and reporting without checking constraints. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Sign errors in Lagrangian:** Inequality constraints use $+ \lambda_i g_i$ (NOT $-$). *Penalty: 1 mark.*
> 7. **Using `|` in matrices/norms:** KTU answer scripts use handwriting; overloading `|` for both table borders and absolute value is the most common *legibility* mistake. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Principles of optimization =** conditions for existence, characterization, and computation of an optimum.
- [ ] **Weierstrass theorem** guarantees existence of optima on compact sets.
- [ ] **FONC (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ → *necessary*.
- [ ] **SONC:** $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succeq 0$ → *necessary*.
- [ ] **SOSC:** $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ → *sufficient* (strict local min).
- [ ] **KKT conditions** = Stationarity + Primal Feasibility + Dual Feasibility + Complementary Slackness.
- [ ] **Complementary Slackness** $\lambda_i g_i = 0$ is the most-tested KKT line.
- [ ] **Fritz John** = KKT without assuming CQ; a multiplier $\lambda_0$ on $\nabla f$ is added.
- [ ] **Convex function** = Hessian PSD everywhere. Strictly convex = PD.
- [ ] **Convex + convex set** ⟹ local min = global min.
- [ ] **Lagrangian:** $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ (with $\lambda_i \ge 0$ for inequalities).
- [ ] **Weak duality** (always): $f^* \ge \phi^*$ (for minimization).
- [ ] **Strong duality** (under Slater / CQ): $f^* = \phi^*$, duality gap = 0.
- [ ] **Descent direction:** $\nabla f^\top \mathbf{d} < 0$.
- [ ] **Armijo condition** ensures sufficient function decrease.
- [ ] **Wolfe conditions** = Armijo + curvature, used in line search.
- [ ] **Newton direction:** $\mathbf{d} = -H^{-1} \nabla f$ → quadratic convergence.
- [ ] **Gradient descent direction:** $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla f$ → linear convergence.
- [ ] **Stopping criteria:** gradient norm, function change, step size, max iterations.
- [ ] **Shadow price** = KKT multiplier = marginal change in optimum per unit relaxation.
- [ ] **Real-world link:** KKT underlies Simplex sensitivity, interior-point methods, SQP, ML loss minimization, and economic dispatch in power systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Memory Hook:** Think of Optimization Principles as the **"Three C's + One D"**:
> **C**onditions (KKT) + **C**onvexity (Global) + **C**onvergence (Algorithm) + **D**uality (Bounds). Master these four, and Module 4 becomes a scoring zone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
